machine_value: Adds support for the packed/THD ("thd") sequence format so multiple variable-length sequences can be packed into one tensor without padding, avoiding wasted compute on pad tokens. It introduces a `PackedSeqParams` dataclass carrying `cu_seqlens_q/kv` and `max_seqlen_q/kv`, threads it through GPTModel→TransformerBlock→Attention→TEDotProductAttention, and refactors `apply_rotary_pos_emb` into bshd/thd + fused/unfused variants (including `fused_apply_rotary_pos_emb_thd`). It gates `max_seqlen_q/kv` on TE>=1.3.0. This is a new capability (latent inefficiency of padded batches), not a fix restoring previously-working performance.

Adds a per-offload-group knob (`fine_grained_offloading_max_inflight_offloads`) controlling how many D2H activation-offload copies may be in flight before the main stream does `wait_event` on older offload events. `_drain_offload_pending` walks a per-group FIFO of offload events and calls `cur.wait_event(old_evt)` once the pending count exceeds the cap. Larger caps allow more D2H↔compute overlap (better GPU utilization / fewer bubbles); 0 serializes by waiting after every offload; None inserts no joins. Under full-iteration CUDA-graph capture the path does not rely on `record_stream`, so these explicit joins are required — hence a config assert forces a non-negative integer there.

Adds an opt-in path (overlap_param_gather_with_optimizer_step) to overlap the distributed-optimizer param all-gather of the first model chunk with the optimizer step, and an align_param_gather mode so all pipeline stages launch param all-gathers simultaneously (dispatched from schedules.py via start_param_sync using torch.distributed._coalescing_manager). Both hide/align exposed all-gather communication behind compute to reduce pipeline bubbles and improve throughput. get_megatron_optimizer is restructured to split off the first dense model chunk so only its AG overlaps the optimizer step. delay_param_gather/delay_grad_reduce config keys are renamed to align_param_gather/align_grad_reduce.

Previously BertModel.__init__ hard-asserted that flash/fused attention be disabled ("Bert currently does not support flash attention. Please set NVTE_FLASH_ATTN=0..."), forcing BERT onto the slower unfused attention path. This commit removes that block and adds _santiy_check_attention_and_get_attn_mask_dimension, which (for TE>=1.7 with flash/fused enabled) builds the attention mask in [b,1,1,s] ("b11s") shape instead of [b,1,s,s] ("b1ss"), the layout the optimized flash/fused TE kernels require. This lets BERT dispatch to the fused/flash attention kernel path.

Adds "Hybrid CUDA graphs" support: MambaLayer now instantiates a CudaGraphManager when config.enable_cuda_graph, and its new __call__ routes forward through cudagraph replay in the inference decode phase (attention_mask is None and inference_context.decode_mode) as well as training/validation, eliminating per-layer kernel-launch overhead. The MambaStack.forward now builds a per-batch sequence_len_offset tensor when enable_cuda_graph/flash_decode is set for static-batch decode, and wraps each layer in a per-layer FP8 autocast context (get_fp8_context, with first/last layers kept in BF16). These are new capabilities making inference (and graphed training) faster — a latent inefficiency (CPU-bound kernel launch overhead in the many-small-op Mamba decode path) is removed, not a restored regression.

Enables the interleaved (virtual pipeline) schedule with P2P communication overlap at pipeline-parallel size 2, previously disallowed (required PP>2). At PP=2 both P2P directions occur between the same two ranks; issuing both on the same NCCL process group serializes them. The fix routes the even-send/odd-recv direction through the pipeline group and the even-recv/odd-send direction through torch.distributed.group.WORLD (a distinct process group), which lets the two independent, opposite-direction communications overlap instead of serializing — reducing pipeline bubbles. The guard checks in parallel_state.py and arguments.py are relaxed from >2 to >1 (only when overlap_p2p_comm is set).

Introduces spec-based modular transformer layers, and in the GPT decoder spec wires the MLP block to a new `TELayernormMLP` wrapper around `te.pytorch.LayerNormMLP`. Per its docstring this "combines `LayerNorm` and the MLP (2 x feedforward layers) into a single module which is performance-efficient as it removes the unnecessary FP8 -> FP32 casts." The self-attention QKV likewise routes through a fused `TELayerNormColumnParallelLinear`. This fuses layernorm with the adjacent GEMM, avoiding extra kernel launches and dtype round-trips (esp. under FP8). It is a latent-inefficiency optimization / feature enablement, not a fix to a previously-regressed path (the prior code path always used separate layernorm + linear modules).

Adds opt-in sequence packing for RL/GRPO training (--rl-use-sequence-packing, --rl-sequence-packing-bin-size default 8192, --rl-sequence-packing-algo fifo|round-robin). Instead of one padded sample per micro-batch, variable-length sequences are packed into fixed-size bins, eliminating padding compute waste; the round-robin algo distributes bins cyclically across DP ranks "for better load balancing", reducing straggler-induced pipeline bubbles. This raises effective throughput/GPU-util of RL training when enabled. No prior regression is repaired — it is a new efficiency capability behind a flag (default off).

During GPT dataset sample-index building, `sequence_lengths` is an mmap-ed numpy array passed into the C++ `helpers.build_sample_idx`. When many documents are indexed relative to the array size (high "access density"), random page-faulting reads over the mmap while holding the GIL are slow. The commit adds a heuristic: if `len(document_index)*2 > len(sequence_lengths)`, take a `.copy()` to force sequential preload into RAM, speeding the C++ call and improving parallelism (GIL held less). This is a latent-inefficiency optimization to dataset build / startup time, not a regression fix — the `build_sample_idx` call was unchanged since the code moved to core.

Refactors RL KV-cache offload from `memory_buffer = kv_cache.cpu()` (allocates a fresh CPU tensor, freeing/reallocating GPU memory at a new virtual address) to `torch_memory_saver.pause/resume("kv_cache")`, which unmaps the physical GPU pages backing the KV cache while retaining a fixed virtual address. Because the virtual address is preserved on resume, previously-captured CUDA graphs remain valid, so the code drops the `assert reset_cuda_graphs` requirement (and the help note "Use only when all ... cudagraphs and the KV cache fit on device"). Net effect: frees GPU memory for training during RL rollouts without forcing costly CUDA-graph re-capture/reset.

Adds an alternative MoE token dispatcher (MoEAlltoAllTokenDispatcher) that exchanges only the tokens each expert-parallel rank needs via torch.distributed.all_to_all_single with per-rank split sizes (all-to-all-v), instead of the existing AllGather dispatcher which replicates all tokens across the whole EP group before permutation. This reduces inter-rank communication volume and the memory of the dispatched buffer, which grow with EP world size under the allgather scheme. It also adds a dedicated _EXPERT_MODEL_PARALLEL_GROUP process group and a TP-region logit all-gather in the router for the alltoall path. Selection is via config; it is a new opt-in path, not a fix to a pre-existing regression.

In fully-parallel distributed checkpoint loading, distribute_shards_to_ranks now takes cross_parallelization_group_loads and adds `int(sh_id_ranks[0] in cross_parallelization_group_loads)` as the highest-priority sort key, so shards whose main replica lives in another (DP) parallelization group are assigned last. This aligns the load distribution with the save distribution, so each rank preferentially loads (reads from storage) the shards it saved, minimizing cross-DP reads of the checkpoint. determine_main_replica_uniform_distribution was reworked to compute group_has_main_replica / group_has_non_main_replica and derive cross_parallelization_group_loads to drive this. It reduces scattered/cross-rank storage reads during checkpoint load, improving load I/O efficiency.

Adds per-layer CUDA graph capture/replay for GPT TransformerLayers (new megatron/core/transformer/cuda_graphs.py: CudaGraphManager/CudaGraphRunner using TE's make_graphed_callables). TransformerLayer.__init__ builds a CudaGraphManager when config.enable_cuda_graph and self.training, and __call__ routes forward through it. Capturing each layer's fwd/bwd into a CUDA graph and replaying it eliminates per-kernel CPU launch overhead and inter-kernel GPU bubbles, improving throughput/GPU utilization for a latent, always-present inefficiency (launch overhead). It is not restoring performance that a prior commit broke, so it is an optimization rather than a regression-fix.

DDP grad-reduce overlap dispatches the async reduce-scatter/all-reduce once all grads in a bucket group are ready (overlapping communication with backward compute). The original design tracked readiness with a set (`params_with_grad`) and fired the collective when `len(params_with_grad) == len(params)`, guarded by `assert param not in self.params_with_grad, "Cannot set grad twice"`. For models with repeated/tied parameters (backward hook fires multiple times per param), this path was unusable. The commit replaces the set with per-param counts (`per_param_grad_ready_counts` vs a `golden_per_param_grad_ready_counts` learned during a first batch), so the collective is dispatched only when every repeated grad-ready event has fired, enabling the overlap optimization for that model class.

Rewrites partial/local CUDA-graph buffer management to cut persistent GPU memory. (1) Replaces TensorReusePool — which kept strong refs to every allocated buffer (`tensor_strong_refs: list = []`, docstring: buffers "will never be freed by the memory allocator") — with `alloc_tensor_from_graph_mempool`, allocating into the driver-pinned graph mempool and tagging `is_from_global_mempool=True`. (2) Adds `make_weakref` so mempool I/O buffers become weak refs whose memory can be reclaimed across graphs while staying valid on replay. (3) Removes the per-layer wgrad `.clone()` in `_CudagraphReplayNode.backward` (was `wgrads = (g.clone() ...)` cloned to survive the next graph replay), replacing it with a CUDA event (`_cudagraph_wgrad_ready_event`) that makes the grad-reduce stream wait on graph-replay completion (added waits in param_and_grad_buffer.py). This removes redundant clone allocations and strong-ref retention.

Adds native NVFP4 weight support to DDP + the distributed optimizer, gated behind a new `fp4_param_gather` flag (default False). When enabled, params are kept in FP4 and the param all-gather communicates packed FP4 bytes directly. The buffer uses a dual-buffer layout: the param buffer is sized at packed numel (N//2, two FP4 values per uint8 byte) while the grad buffer stays at full numel, and `modify_nvfp4_rowwise_storage` remaps the NVFP4 tensor's internal rowwise uint8 storage into the contiguous DDP buffer so the all-gather moves half the bytes of a BF16/FP8 gather. This reduces param all-gather communication volume and weight memory. No prior FP4-gather path existed, so this is a latent-capability optimization, not restoration of a regressed behavior.

In the MoE token dispatcher (DeepEP path), the previously-separate permute-with-probs and quantization-alignment padding operations are fused into a single TE kernel (moe_permute_and_pad_with_probs), and the corresponding unpermute+unpad is fused via pad_offsets passed to fused_unpermute. This eliminates extra passes over the token buffer for FP8/FP4 training, reducing launch/memory-traffic overhead in the MoE dispatch. It's a latent inefficiency (ops were always separate) being fused, not a regression restoration.

Dataloader worker processes forked from a CUDA-initialized parent inherit open file descriptors to /dev/nvidia* devices. These FDs keep references into NVIDIA memory space, so GPU memory cannot be reclaimed if a worker is delayed or fails to exit. The fix adds close_nvidia_fds() in worker_init_fn (scanning /proc/self/fd and os.close-ing any link starting with /dev/nvidia) and now runs worker_init_fn whenever num_workers>0 (previously gated on args.exit_signal_handler), so the FD-closing runs in all multi-worker setups.

HybridStack.forward previously always ran its layers eagerly with no activation-checkpointing path; this commit adds a `recompute_granularity == 'full' and self.training` branch that calls the newly extracted `checkpointed_forward` (moved from TransformerBlock._checkpointed_forward into megatron/core/recompute.py). Activation recomputation trades extra backward compute to drop stored activations, reducing peak activation memory so HybridModels can train at larger batch/sequence sizes. This is a new capability (latent limitation), not a fix restoring behavior an earlier commit broke.

The vocab-parallel cross entropy forward previously did `logits = vocab_parallel_logits.clone()` (a full-size copy of the [*, partition_vocab_size] logits tensor) and then `exp_logits = logits.exp()` (another full-size allocation). The commit removes the clone and operates in-place on the input (`vocab_parallel_logits.sub_(...)` and `torch.exp(vocab_parallel_logits, out=exp_logits)` reusing the buffer), so two large logits-sized activation buffers are no longer allocated. Only the small predicted_logits_1d is cloned/made contiguous. This reduces peak activation memory of the loss computation.

In free_output_tensor() (pipeline-parallel schedule), the scalar used to pseudo-free a sent output tensor's .data was allocated on CPU and copied to GPU: `torch.FloatTensor([0]).to(output_tensor.data)`. This commit allocates it directly on device via `torch.cuda.FloatTensor([0])`, removing a per-microbatch host allocation plus a synchronous host-to-device copy on the compute path.

Gradient-clipping helpers created small CUDA tensors via torch.tensor([0], device='cuda'), which materializes the value from a Python list on the host and triggers an implicit host→device copy/sync on every call. Replacing them with torch.zeros(1, device='cuda') allocates directly on-device, removing the redundant host/device sync per optimizer step. Same replacement applied to dummy_overflow_buf (x2), grad_norm fallback, and total_num_zeros.

Within a commit primarily adding multimodule 1F1B pipeline support, BridgeCommunicator now caches broadcast process groups (`_broadcast_pg_cache` + `_get_or_create_broadcast_pg`) keyed by sorted rank sets, instead of calling `dist.new_subgroups_by_enumeration(..., backend='nccl')` on every `__init__`. This avoids creating duplicate NCCL communicators for identical rank sets — each NCCL communicator consumes GPU memory (buffers) and setup time — so the cache reduces per-communicator memory/startup overhead when many BridgeCommunicators share the same broadcast rank layout. `HyperCommGrid.destroy()` / `destroy_broadcast_pgs()` add reclamation of these groups. (Other hunks — `torch.cat(dim=self.dim_mapping['b'])`, 2D/3D shape prep — are correctness, not perf.)

Adds two opt-in performance knobs for MoE all-to-all overlap. (1) `high_priority_a2a_comm_stream` config: when True, `set_streams(high_priority=...)` creates the communication stream with CUDA high priority (`torch.cuda.Stream.priority_range()`), letting A2A comm kernels preempt compute kernels for better overlap in combined 1f1b scheduling. (2) `moe_hybridep_num_sms_preprocessing` (default 108) threads a `num_sms_preprocessing_api` param through HybridEPDispatch into `init_hybrid_ep_buffer`, tuning SM allocation for the preprocessing (metadata scan) kernel. Both default to prior behavior so this is a latent tuning improvement, not a regression fix.

In GRPO RL training with --rl-offload-optimizer-during-inference, the optimizer state + grad buffers are offloaded to CPU before inference to free GPU memory. The commit moves the restore (model[0].restore_grad_buffers() + optimizer.restore_from_cpu()) from immediately after rollout collection in get_environment_rollouts to later inside get_grpo_data_iterator (gated by the new optimizer_is_on_cpu arg). This keeps the optimizer state off the GPU during the intervening logprobs-computation forward passes (rl/compute-old-logprobs / rl/compute-ref-logprobs), extending the memory-saving window so GPU memory is available for the logprobs phase instead of being prematurely reclaimed by optimizer state.

In create_packed_seq_params_for_bin, the cu_seqlens tensor for Transformer Engine THD attention was padded to `bin_size` (the padded sequence length in tokens, i.e. thousands) instead of `max_sequences_per_bin` (max #sequences per bin, 32/50). The fix changes `if len(cu_seqlens) < bin_size: out = cu_seqlens.new_full((bin_size,), bin_size)` to use `max_sequences_per_bin`, and lowers the default `--rl-sequence-packing-max-sequences-per-bin` from 50 to 32. The oversized cu_seqlens created thousands of zero-length ghost sequences, inflating the TE attention working set / memory footprint. Golden values confirm mem-allocated-bytes dropping from ~99.6GB to ~55.3GB and mem-max-allocated-bytes from ~101.7GB to ~57.1GB.

In initialize_word_embeddings(), the last pipeline stage unconditionally allocated a second full VocabParallelEmbedding (padded_vocab_size x hidden_size) as word_embeddings_for_head. For T5 with pipeline_model_parallel_size=2, the last stage already holds the decoder's self.language_model.embedding.word_embeddings, so this extra vocab-sized parameter table was redundant and wasted GPU memory. The fix guards the allocation with `(not hasattr(self.language_model, 'embedding') or self.language_model.embedding is None)` so the duplicate embedding is only created when the stage lacks its own embedding.

Adds an opt-in `fp16_lm_cross_entropy` path to BERT: when enabled, `mpu.vocab_parallel_cross_entropy` is applied directly to the fp16 `lm_logits` inside the model, skipping the `.float()` upcast of the large [tokens, vocab] logits tensor used in the default path (`lm_logits.contiguous().float()`). Avoiding the fp32 upcast of the vocab-parallel logits reduces activation memory and compute for the loss.

The commit adds per-request ("mixed-batch") sampling to the dynamic inference engine: sampling params move from a single engine-wide `SamplingParams` onto each `DynamicInferenceRequest`, and the new `sample_from_dynamic_logits` loops over `active_sampling_map: List[Tuple[SamplingParams, List[int]]]`, calling `sample_from_logits` once per distinct sampling-param group every generation step. To avoid this per-step Python loop when all requests share the same params, a `static_sampling: bool = False` engine flag is added whose docstring states it "avoids needing to loop through all requests and their sampling parameters every generation step, improving latency." So the perf artifact is an opt-in latency optimization (static_sampling) guarding a newly-introduced per-step loop.

Adds a JIT-fused (jit_fuser) forward/backward implementation of the gated-GELU (GeGLU) activation and its bias variant in a new fused_bias_geglu.py, and wires MLP.forward to dispatch to bias_geglu_impl when activation_func==F.gelu and config.gated_linear_unit is True. Previously the gated-GELU case fell through to bias_gelu_impl (the non-gated path) / unfused elementwise chunk+gelu ops, incurring extra kernel launches and memory traffic. Fusing chunk/gelu/multiply into one autograd Function reduces intermediate tensors and kernel-launch overhead.

SharedExpertMLP uses a side CUDA stream to overlap shared-expert compute with the MoE dispatcher when --moe-shared-expert-overlap is set. The original code `if self.stream is None: self.stream = torch.cuda.Stream()` read the class attribute (None) but then assigned to a per-instance attribute, so every transformer layer's SharedExpertMLP created its own separate CUDA stream. The fix assigns to `self.__class__.stream` and shares that single stream across all instances, reducing the number of streams from one-per-layer to one total. Fewer streams reduces stream-management/scheduling overhead and can improve overlap behavior / GPU utilization.

The parent commit f16be74ff introduced defer_embedding_wgrad_compute, which hides pipeline-flush bubble latency by buffering the output-layer input activations and grad_outputs for every micro-batch and deferring the embedding wgrad GEMMs to the flush phase. That buffering was unconditional (embedding_activation_buffer.append(input_parallel); grad_output_buffer.append(grad_output)), so activation memory grew linearly with micro-batches per global batch. This commit adds a wgrad_deferral_limit knob so buffering stops once len(embedding_activation_buffer) >= limit (and grad wgrad_compute runs inline: `if wgrad_deferral_limit == 0 or len(grad_output_buffer) < wgrad_deferral_limit`), bounding the extra activation memory while still hiding the bubble for the first N micro-batches. It's a memory-vs-bubble tuning refinement of an existing optimization, not a fix for any regression.

Switches the REALM/ICT FAISS MIPS index from a CPU flat index (added via IndexIDMap and moved with index_cpu_to_gpu) to a native GPU index: `self.block_mips_index = faiss.GpuIndexFlat(res, ..., config)` with `config.useFloat16 = True`, gated behind a new `--faiss-use-gpu` flag. This accelerates nearest-block retrieval search on GPU in fp16. Also adds `detach(query_embeds)` and a `torch.no_grad()` block around the search to avoid autograd overhead. Note it introduces a Python double `for` loop remapping GPU indices via `id_map`, a scalar-level overhead on the GPU path.

In megatron.core the tensor-parallel linear (LinearWithGradAccumulationAndAsyncCommunication) forced its matmuls to fp32 via `with torch.cuda.amp.autocast(enabled=False): return ...apply(*args)`, and `forward_step` never activated autocast around the model forward. This commit removes the hard `autocast(enabled=False)` disable, decorates the autograd Function's forward/backward with `@custom_fwd`/`@custom_bwd` (the AMP helpers that cast inputs to the autocast dtype), and wraps the forward pass in `torch.autocast("cuda") if torch.is_autocast_enabled()`. As a result, when AMP is enabled the core linear GEMMs run in fp16/bf16 on Tensor Cores instead of full fp32, restoring the mixed-precision fast path (subject: 'Make autocast functional for megatron core').

In `_bias_dropout_add_func`, when in eval mode with no tensor requiring grad, the op now runs in-place: `x.add_(bias)`, `torch.nn.functional.dropout(..., inplace=inplace)`, and `out.add_(residual)` instead of allocating new tensors via `x + bias` / `residual + out`. This removes intermediate allocations in the bias-dropout-add fusion for inference. Additionally the inference forward paths are switched from `torch.no_grad()` to `torch.inference_mode()` (in dynamic_engine, run_one_forward_step, and the text-generation controllers), which further reduces autograd bookkeeping/versioning overhead.

In RL sequence packing, SequencePacker.pack_sequences materialized a dense 4D boolean attention mask of shape [num_bins, 1, bin_size, bin_size] (allocated via torch.zeros, filled per sequence with torch.tril, then bitwise_not_'d), threaded it through distribute_packed_bins/create_empty_bins, and stored it on PackingContext.packed_attention_mask. But the actual training consumer load_packed_data_by_index never reads packed_attention_mask — attention masking is done via cached PackedSeqParams/cu_seqlens (thd format) for Transformer Engine ("Get cached PackedSeqParams for proper attention masking in Transformer Engine"). The commit removes this fully-unused dense mask, eliminating an O(num_bins·bin_size^2) GPU allocation plus per-sequence tril/bitwise-not compute that was pure dead work.

When untie_embeddings_and_output_weights is set, the input embedding and output head weights are separate, so there is no need to (a) create a second zero-initialized VocabParallelEmbedding copy on the last pipeline stage or (b) run the initial cross-stage sync collective torch.distributed.all_reduce(...group=mpu.get_embedding_group()) inside initialize_word_embeddings. This commit passes share_word_embeddings=not args.untie_embeddings_and_output_weights and guards `if not args.untie_embeddings_and_output_weights: self.initialize_word_embeddings(...)`, eliminating a redundant embedding all-reduce and an extra weight buffer in the untied configuration.

Finalizes an opt-in `--empty-unused-memory-each-iter` flag (levels 0/1/2) that calls torch.cuda.empty_cache() during train_step and (newly) the evaluate loop to reduce CUDA memory fragmentation. This is a latent-inefficiency mitigation tool (default off), not a fix restoring previously-good performance. Note the tradeoff: empty_cache() forces device syncs and re-allocation, so enabling it trades throughput for lower fragmentation/peak memory.

Adds an opt-in `disable_grad_reduce` flag to `ColumnParallelLinear`. When True, the forward takes the `input_parallel = input_` path instead of `copy_to_tensor_model_parallel_region(input_)`, whose autograd backward (`_CopyToModelParallelRegion.backward` -> `_reduce`) performs a `torch.distributed.all_reduce` of the input gradient across the TP group. Skipping this lets the caller (LoRA adapter in NeMo) delay and fuse the reduction with other gradient collectives, reducing collective count/overhead. Defaults to False, so no change to existing behavior.

The Torch-Dist save strategy previously called PyTorch's built-in save_state_dict with a thread-based FileSystemWriter(thread_count=self.thread_count), so per-file writes were serialized by the GIL. This commit adds FileSystemWriterAsync, which splits write items into buckets (_split_by_size_and_type) and forks one process per bucket (ctx.get_context('fork').Process → write_preloaded_data) that each writes its file in true parallel, bypassing the GIL. It also does a synchronous D2H copy up-front (detach().to('cpu', non_blocking=True)) and splits the save into plan/write/finalize stages to enable async saving. Result: faster checkpoint save I/O.

In the ORQA supervised finetune forward step, `neg_context_tokens` can have a different first-dimension (number of negative contexts) on each data-parallel rank. The code later performs a `torch.distributed.all_gather` on these tensors; NCCL all_gather requires identical tensor shapes on all participating ranks, so mismatched sizes cause the collective to deadlock (hang). This commit adds logic that first all_gathers each rank's first-dim length, computes the max across ranks, and pads every rank's `neg_context_tokens` to that max via F.pad before the gather so the collective's shapes match. The commit itself is a WIP debug step (contains print statements and an `exit()`); the follow-up commit ebfbfcec9 factors the same padding into `check_and_append_tensor_for_gather` and removes the debug exit.

The Mamba prefix-cache budget (prefix_caching_mamba_gb) was previously sized only for the durable cache: `max_slots = total_bytes // per_slot_bytes`. But MambaSlotAllocator also allocates fixed CUDA-graph "scratch" buffers (intermediate_ssm_out/intermediate_conv_out) of size MAX_INTERMEDIATE_OFFSETS_PER_REQUEST(=3) * max_requests slots with the same per-slot footprint, which were NOT counted against the budget. Total GPU usage therefore silently exceeded mamba_gb by scratch_bytes and could OOM at startup, especially when scratch_slots > max_slots (large max_requests). The fix reserves scratch_bytes from the budget first (`max_slots = (total_bytes - scratch_bytes) // per_slot_bytes`), and raises a ValueError if the budget can't even fit the scratch plus one durable slot instead of silently over-allocating.

The text-generation Flask inference server runs with app.run(threaded=True), so concurrent HTTP requests can enter the critical section (send_do_generate()'s torch.distributed.broadcast plus generate()) simultaneously on rank 0. Interleaving collective operations across ranks deadlocks the distributed group, hanging the server. The fix wraps the send_do_generate()+generate() region in a module-level threading.Semaphore (sem.acquire()/sem.release()) to serialize generation so collectives stay lock-step across ranks.

SHARP (in-network all-reduce offload) accelerates data-parallel gradient all-reduce. The fix corrects the enablement so SHARP is actually applied: (1) the DP barrier that registers the SHARP target group now always uses the context-parallel-inclusive DP group (with_context_parallel=True) instead of only when cp>1, so with cp=1 the collective happens over the plain dp group as intended for SHARP; (2) it replaces the wrong env var NCCL_SHARP_DISABLE=1 (which would disable SHARP outright) with NCCL_COLLNET_ENABLE=0 to correctly restrict CollNet/SHARP to the barrier-registered DP process groups. Net effect: DP all-reduce collectives get network-offloaded acceleration that the just-landed code failed to activate.

In the RL dynamic inference engine builder `get_dynamic_inference_engine`, the newly-added CUDA-graph config params `cuda_graph_max_tokens` and `cuda_graph_mixed_prefill_count` were not forwarded to `DynamicInferenceContext`, so the context silently used constructor defaults (`cuda_graph_max_tokens=None`, `cuda_graph_mixed_prefill_count=16`) instead of the user's `--inference-dynamic-batching-cuda-graph-*` args. These values feed `CUDAGraphBatchDimensionBuilder.generate_cuda_graph_batch_dimensions_list`, which determines which batch/token sizes get CUDA graphs captured. Missing the args means graph coverage doesn't match the configured workload, causing fallback to eager (non-graphed) execution / suboptimal graph coverage for RL inference. The fix adds the two `args.*` passes to restore intended config-driven graph capture.

In the TransformerEngine path with activation recompute (uniform/block granularity), the recompute call sites invoked the custom forward without passing is_first_microbatch. TE uses is_first_microbatch to cache the FP8-cast weights across microbatches; when it is not supplied (defaults to None) TE re-casts/re-quantizes weights on every microbatch instead of reusing the cache, adding redundant per-microbatch weight-cast work. The fix passes is_first_microbatch (and rotary_pos_emb as a keyword) to the TE recompute call sites, re-enabling the weight cache.

When --moe-shared-expert-overlap is set, SharedExpertMLP replaces the TELinear's built-in TP communication with a manual scheme (pre_forward_comm does the AllGather, post_forward_comm does the ReduceScatter, on a dedicated stream) and sets `linear.parallel_mode = None` to disable TE's own comm. However the TE Userbuffers TP-comm-overlap flags (ub_overlap_rs_fprop / ub_overlap_ag_dgrad / ub_overlap_ag_fprop / ub_overlap_rs_dgrad), enabled by --tp-comm-overlap, were left True. So when both features were combined, TE still attempted Userbuffers-overlapped collectives on a linear whose parallel_mode had been disabled, conflicting with the manual shared-expert overlap path. The fix force-disables those four UB overlap flags on the shared-expert linears.

Adds an opt-in path that swaps the bf16 tensor_parallel.ColumnParallelLinear LM-head output projection for a TE TELMHeadColumnParallelLinear running under the MXFP8 fp8 autocast context (`with get_fp8_context(self.config): super().forward(input_)`), so the large vocab-projection GEMM executes in MXFP8 instead of bf16 — reducing compute/memory-bandwidth cost of the output projection. This is a new capability, not a fix to a previously-regressed path.

In Megatron-FSDP's GradReducePipeline, gradient reduce-scatter is overlapped with backward compute using rotating "double buffers". The pre-fix code counted queued FSDP units and only decremented keep_n (the number of in-flight grad-reduce buffers kept alive / not waited-on) after `len(double_buf_units) > 2`, reserving too few double buffers. This forces the pipeline to wait_for_previous_grad_reduce too eagerly (or risk reusing a buffer whose RS hasn't finished), collapsing the comm/compute overlap and inserting a stall/bubble in the backward pass. The fix lowers the threshold to `> 1` (keeps one more buffer alive) and additionally runs `wait_for_previous_grad_reduce(keep_n)` inside `torch.cuda.stream(self.rs_stream)` so the wait is correctly ordered on the reduce-scatter stream.

VocabParallelEmbedding.forward() contained `assert not torch.any((input_ < 0) | (input_ >= self.num_embeddings))`, which runs on every forward pass on the GPU input tensor. Evaluating that boolean in a Python `assert` forces a GPU→CPU synchronization each step, stalling the pipeline and creating bubbles. The commit removes the per-step GPU check and moves the equivalent OOB validation into GPTDataset.__getitem__ (CPU-side, during data loading), where `assert not torch.any(tokens >= self.vocab_size)` runs off the training critical path.

In calc_params_l2_norm, the all_reduce summing sharded_norm_2 over the DP+CP group was nested inside `if len(sharded_params_data) > 0:`. With the distributed optimizer, sharded main-params can be unevenly distributed across ranks, so some ranks entered the block and called the collective while others skipped it — a mismatched collective causing a distributed hang/deadlock. The fix adds an `else: sharded_norm_2 = torch.zeros(...)` branch and hoists the all_reduce outside the conditional so every rank in the group participates unconditionally (same pattern already used for MoE norms per issue #409).

The Transformer Engine parallel cross-entropy fusion (added in 0f60adbd3 as a "general performance flag") is hard-disabled via an assert in both ModelParallelConfig.__post_init__ and validate_args when cross_entropy_loss_fusion=True and cross_entropy_fusion_impl=='te'. Users/configs are forced onto the 'native' fused CE path (fused_vocab_parallel_cross_entropy). All release/perf functional-test configs flip '--cross-entropy-fusion-impl: te' -> 'native' and the MoE perf-flag README does likewise, so the TE fused kernel — a throughput optimization — is removed from the sanctioned config surface. The stated reason is "stability issues" (a correctness concern), but it disables a performance feature/path.

The commit adds a functional throughput regression test for the dynamic inference engine: it records `throughput = total_output_tokens / total_time` into json_results, seeds golden values (e.g. 18.42, 144.48, 7.129 tok/s across TP configs), and adds assertions in test_inference_regular_pipeline.py that current throughput must be within 4 tok/s below golden and not >30% above (else update goldens). It also adds a real CLI flag `--decode-only-cuda-graphs` plumbed to `use_cuda_graphs_for_non_decode_steps=not args.decode_only_cuda_graphs`, and a new test case exercising decode-only cuda graphs. The change is test/CI scaffolding around existing runtime behavior, not a perf fix or optimization itself.

get_nccl_options(pg_name, nccl_comm_cfgs) looks up per-communicator NCCL performance knobs (cga_cluster_size/max_ctas/min_ctas, and now net_name) by key name; the commit (1) adds a net_name option so users can force IB vs socket transport per communicator, and (2) standardizes the config key names used per process group (e.g. 'exp'->'ep', 'tp'->'ep_tp', 'tp_exp'->'tp_ep_mp', 'mp'->'tp_ep_pp', 'dp'->'ep_dp', 'cp'->'hcp', 'dp_cp'->'intra_dp_cp'/'inter_dp_cp', 'embd'->'pos_embd'). These knobs tune collective-communication performance, but they only take effect when a user supplies a matching key in nccl_communicator_config_path; when the key is absent get_nccl_options returns None and NCCL defaults are used, so default runtime behavior is unchanged.

Reverts the dynamic-inference KV-cache buffer CLI arg from `--inference-dynamic-batching-active-buffer-size-gb` back to `--inference-dynamic-batching-buffer-size-gb`, and adjusts the block-allocator sizing semantics: total KV blocks are now derived so that total GPU buffer == `buffer_size_gb` when `unified_memory_level==0` (and `2*buffer_size_gb` only when UVM level 1). Previously (the reverted PR #1983 state) the buffer was always doubled (`total_count = 2*active_count+1`) assuming a same-size CPU paused buffer in unified memory, so a given arg value implied twice the GPU-resident KV cache. This changes how much GPU memory the KV cache reserves for a given config value, which affects max concurrent requests/tokens and thus inference throughput/latency headroom.

core_transformer_config_from_args() builds kw_args by copying only args whose name matches a TransformerConfig dataclass field. TransformerConfig defines `fp8: bool = True` (default True) but there is no CLI arg named `fp8` (the flags are `--fp8-e4m3`/`--fp8-hybrid`), so the auto-copy loop never set it and `fp8` stayed at its default True. Result: the TE fp8_autocast / FP8 GEMM compute path was silently enabled regardless of the user's flags (and the requested e4m3/hybrid format was not translated). This commit adds explicit `kw_args['fp8'] = args.fp8_e4m3 or args.fp8_hybrid` (plus fp8_e4m3/fp8_margin), so FP8 compute is engaged only when actually requested — an accuracy/config change that toggles the FP8 fused-GEMM performance path on/off.

Introduces a dedicated `apply_rope_fusion: bool = False` config knob gating the fused RoPE kernel `fused_apply_rotary_pos_emb`, replacing an ad-hoc debug hack (commit 8f44952c3) that had abused the `bias_activation_fusion` flag. It also wraps the apex import in try/except (HAVE_APPLY_ROPE_FUSION) so the fused path is skipped gracefully when apex is unavailable. Effectively the fused rotary-embedding kernel is OFF by default; when explicitly enabled it fuses the RoPE apply into one kernel instead of the unfused `apply_rotary_pos_emb` elementwise ops, affecting attention throughput. The MLP change (`self.activation_func = self.config.activation_func`) is a functional correctness restore, not perf.

In pretrain_retro.py the RETRO decoder block spec is built with use_transformer_engine flipped from False to True. Per decoder_spec.py this switches get_retro_decoder_block_spec from get_retro_decoder_layer_local_spec (local ColumnParallelLinear/RowParallelLinear/DotProductAttention/FusedLayerNorm) to get_retro_decoder_layer_te_spec (Transformer Engine fused kernels: TEColumnParallelLinear, TERowParallelLinear, TEDotProductAttention, TENorm), i.e. defaulting to the fused/optimized attention+linear+norm code path which is typically higher-throughput.

This commit reverts MR!2786 (commit 9a5a6656), which had added "aggregate communication" to custom FSDP: batching per-bucket all-gather/reduce-scatter collectives into larger communication units via queue_bucket_to_all_gather, auto-computing suggested_communication_unit_size (2x FSDP-module param count, or bucket_size*2) for AG prefetch / RS queue capacity, and triggering aggregate collectives in fp8 mixed-precision training. Reverting restores the older per-param all_gather_params path and separate _grad_acc/reduce-gradients logic, i.e. it backs out a collective-aggregation throughput optimization on the FSDP comm path (likely due to a functional/stability issue, since golden values were also reverted).

Refactors MoE token routing from index-based to mask-based. permute() replaces torch.argsort(flatten_indices)+moe_gather.apply with routing_map.bool().T + masked_select + index_select; preprocess() replaces torch.histc(indices) with routing_map.sum(dim=0), and the multi-local-expert path replaces torch.repeat_interleave(expert_ids_per_ep_rank, ...) (which the old comment states 'calls stream synchronization internally') with sort_chunks_by_idxs using a precomputed num_global_tokens_per_local_expert_cpu split. This reworks CUDA sync points (no_sync/before_ep_alltoall/before_finish) in the hot dispatch path. Net perf effect is ambiguous: it removes one implicit device sync but adds dense mask allocations/transposes (torch.zeros_like(logits) masks, .T.contiguous()) that scale with num_experts. No regression is restored and no measured speedup is stated; this is foundational representation change enabling later perf work.

Debugging/WIP commit in the "viewless tensor" work series exploring a memory leak in activation checkpointing: assigning to `tensor.data` while `tensor._base is not None` (a view) leaks the base allocation, which "accumulate[s] over iterations". It adds a `safely_set_tensor_data_attr()` guard around the `split_tensor_into_1d_equal_chunks` `.data` assignment in CheckpointFunction, plus a scratch `make_standalone_tensor()` helper. However it is exploratory scaffolding — scratch `# >>>`/`# <<<` markers, commented-out `pax`/`lutil` debug imports, a `hidden_states = hidden_states.clone()` (which adds a copy), and the assert message even dereferences `tensor._base.shape` after asserting `_base is None`. Not a shipped fix.

On XPU the weightless RMSNorm path (weight is None) dispatched to the native PyTorch eager implementation, and in the fused_add case did extra x.copy_()/x_residual.copy_() writes. This commit instead synthesizes a torch.ones weight and calls the fused torch.ops._C.rms_norm / fused_add_rms_norm XPU kernel, replacing the unfused eager path (plus redundant tensor copies) with the single fused kernel. The comment even notes 'Kernel requires weight tensor, pass ones'.

The Sampler had a dedicated `torch.cuda.Stream()` (`_copy_stream`) used to prepare sampling tensors, followed by `torch.cuda.current_stream().wait_stream(self._copy_stream)`. This commit removes both the stream and the cross-stream sync, running `SamplingTensors.from_sampling_metadata` directly on the default stream. Because that function already builds tensors in pinned memory and issues `.to(device, non_blocking=True)` H2D copies (see `from_lists`: `pin_memory = not in_wsl()` and "Because the memory is pinned, we can do non-blocking transfer to device"), the extra copy stream + `wait_stream` synchronization was redundant overhead that added per-step latency in the sampler without providing overlap benefit.

Adds `@support_torch_compile` to `ModernBertModel` and `BertWithRope`, enabling torch.compile / cuda-graph capture for these encoder/pooling models in v1. Previously they ran the forward in eager mode (per-op kernel launches, no captured graph). Supporting changes make the compile path viable: `forward` signatures are made static/non-Optional (`input_ids: torch.Tensor`, `positions: torch.Tensor`) with consistent naming, the `position_ids` alias plumbing is removed, and a tensor-truthiness bug `if inputs_embeds:` is fixed to `if inputs_embeds is not None:` (which would error/misbehave under trace).

In the BatchedDeepGemmExperts MoE path, the previous code ran silu-mul activation, tensor reshapes, and per-token-group FP8 quantization as separate ops (self.activation + per_token_group_quant_fp8) plus an extra workspace2 allocation. This commit fuses silu(y[:H])*y[H:] and group-wise FP8 quantization into a single Triton kernel (silu_mul_fp8_quant_deep_gemm), eliminating an intermediate buffer and multiple kernel launches / small ops in the DeepEP low-latency EP+DP hot path. layer.py additionally enables conditional use_fp8_dispatch (was hardcoded False) to cut data movement when quant dtype/block size match.

In the V1 scheduler's WAITING loop, when a request needed a LoRA that couldn't fit within max_loras, the old code did `break`, halting scheduling of ALL remaining waiting requests for that step — even non-LoRA requests or requests using already-scheduled LoRAs (documented by the removed TODO: "This is too conservative and could be optimized"). The fix replaces `break` with a skip: the blocking request is `appendleft`'d to a `skipped_waiting_requests` deque and `continue`s, so head-of-line blocking is removed and other schedulable waiting requests can be admitted, improving batch fill and throughput under LoRA. It also unifies the FSM-skip and LoRA-skip logic into one `is_skipped` path.

Adds an opt-in FlashInfer fused top-k/top-p sampling kernel (gated by VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_SAMPLER, default off): when enabled, the sort-based _apply_top_k_top_p (`if do_top_p_top_k and flashinfer_top_k_top_p_sampling is None`) is skipped and top-k/top-p + multinomial sampling are fused into a single GPU kernel via _top_k_top_p_multinomial_with_flashinfer. Separately, _multinomial replaces `probs[:, None, :].expand(...).contiguous().view(...)` with `probs.repeat_interleave(num_samples, dim=0)`; the removed comment states the expand path was equivalent to repeat_interleave which 'forces a GPU<->CPU sync', so this reduces host-device syncs in the sampler for n>1 sampling.

The original DeepGEMM warmup ran one JIT-compiled GEMM for every M value from max_tokens down to 1 (`num_tokens -= 1` loop) and every MoE M in block_m steps, which the code comment says "increases the engine startup time by a couple of minutes." The commit adds a `_generate_optimal_warmup_m_values` heuristic that only warms up M values at DeepGEMM kernel-config boundaries (wave transitions across block_ms/block_ns and block_m multiples), enabled by a new default "relax" mode, cutting the number of warmup GEMMs (and thus JIT compilations) at startup while aiming to cover the same set of distinct kernels.

Rewrites the cp_gather_and_upconvert_fp8_kv_cache CUDA kernel (DeepSeek-V3.2 MLA FP8 KV-cache gather+dequant). The original launched a grid of (batch_size, num_splits) with a heuristic num_splits (2/4/16) and block(576) threads, doing scalar per-element FP8 loads/stores (one thread per byte: `no_pe_ptr[tid]`, `scaled_convert<...>(val, scale)`). The new kernel assigns one warp per output token (256 threads = 8 warps/block, grid sized by total_tokens), performs vectorized 128-bit int4 loads of the 512 FP8 bytes, batched fp8::scaled_vec_conversion<bf16_8_t,uint2> upconversion, vectorized int4 BF16 stores, and vectorized RoPE copy. A binary search over workspace_starts assigns tokens to requests, replacing the imbalanced fixed-split heuristic. Result: better memory-bandwidth utilization and load balancing across variable-length prefills.

The marlin GEMM previously enforced TORCH_CHECK(a.is_contiguous(), "A is not contiguous"), forcing any caller with a non-contiguous activation view to materialize an extra .contiguous() copy before every quantized GEMM. This commit threads lda = A.stride(0) through permute_cols_kernel and Marlin (replacing the hardcoded prob_k stride: a_gl_stride = lda/8, A += ...*lda/8) and relaxes the check to only require a.stride(1)==1 plus 16-byte/stride(0)%8 alignment, so non-contiguous inputs are accepted directly — eliminating the activation copy/alloc on the quantized inference path.

The Parakeet audio feature extractor previously padded each entire audio to a single normalized length (_normalize_audio_length) and fed it as one tensor through the sound encoder; for long audios (up to MAX_AUDIO_LEN_S = 10 minutes) this created huge encoder activation tensors and OOM'd. The fix chops audio into fixed ~30s clips (split_audio_into_clips using _clip_target_samples = clip_duration_s * sampling_rate), runs the encoder on bounded-size clips, and re-groups per-item embeddings via audio_num_clips, bounding peak encoder memory regardless of audio length.

In TP>1 with a KV connector (NIXL disaggregated prefill), get_finished() on the worker previously performed a per-step, blocking object-serialization collective: rank 0 called tp_group.recv_object() from every other rank and ranks 1..N-1 called tp_group.send_object() to rank 0, to aggregate done_sending/done_recving before reporting to the scheduler. This commit removes that worker-side cross-rank exchange (deleting _done_sending_count/_done_recving_count and the send_object/recv_object logic) and instead has each worker report its own finished sets; the scheduler process (MultiprocExecutor._aggregate_workers_output / _async_aggregate_workers_output) counts responses across workers. This eliminates an extra synchronous inter-rank round-trip on the model-execution critical path each step when a connector is active.

CompressedTensorsW8A8Fp8MoEMethod never computed/passed an `allow_deep_gemm` flag, so both fused-MoE dispatch calls used the default (False) and fell back to the Triton fused_moe kernel instead of the faster DeepGEMM fp8 path for block-quantized weights. The fix adds `self.allow_deep_gemm` (guarded by block_quant, VLLM_MOE_USE_DEEP_GEMM, is_deep_gemm_supported(), and block-size alignment matching get_mk_alignment_for_contiguous_layout()) and passes `allow_deep_gemm=self.allow_deep_gemm` into both MoE calls; it also removes an over-strict `assert apply_router_weight_on_input is False` in fused_experts that could block the deepgemm branch.

get_request_block_hasher() is called on the per-request scheduler path to compute hashes of newly-completed KV-cache blocks for prefix caching. The commit adds an early-return guard `if start_token_idx + block_size > num_tokens: return []` so that when the newly-added tokens do not complete a full block (the common case in the decode phase, where ~1 token is appended per step), the function skips the downstream mm-index setup and hashing loop entirely, reducing per-step CPU overhead.

The silu_and_mul_per_block_quant CUDA kernel was rewritten from a one-thread-block-per-group design (scalar one-element loads + a log2(group_size) shared-memory tree reduction with a __syncthreads per level) to a one-warp-per-group design using vectorized wide vector loads/stores (EPT = group_size/32 elements per lane) and a pure __shfl_xor_sync warp-shuffle abs-max reduction with no shared memory and no __syncthreads. This removes sync barriers and improves memory coalescing on a kernel described as memory-latency-bound. fmaxf is order-invariant so the per-group scale stays bit-identical.

In compute_probs (rejection sampler / spec decode), temperature scaling is already applied inline (`logits = logits / temperature.unsqueeze(-1)`) before the softmax, so the removed `compiled_softmax` helper was always called with the default `temperature=1.0`, making its compiled `logits / temperature` a no-op divide-by-one with no fusion gain. The `@torch.compile` + `torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic` machinery added guard/recompilation overhead for no benefit; replacing it with a plain `logits.softmax(dim=-1, dtype=torch.float32)` removes that overhead. This is a minor cleanup, not a fix for a real before/after regression.

The gate function should_use_deepgemm_for_fp8_linear decides whether an FP8 blockwise-scaled linear layer routes to the DeepGEMM kernel. Previously it required the weight N dim (weight.shape[0]) to be a multiple of 128; this commit relaxes it to a multiple of 64 (N_MULTIPLE=64, K_MULTIPLE=128). Layers whose output dim is a 64- but not 128-multiple were previously excluded and fell back to a slower matmul path; now they use the faster DeepGEMM kernel. The overly conservative 128 restriction was always present since the gate was added, so this widens coverage rather than restoring lost performance.

The int4 weight "unified encode" preprocessing step (nibble remapping applied at weight load/reorder time for W4A8 GEMM) previously used CUTLASS's host-side `cutlass::unified_encode_int4b`, which encodes on CPU element-by-element. The commit replaces it with a GPU kernel `unified_encode_int4b_device` that uses a 256-entry constant-memory lookup table (`kNibbleLUT`) mapping two 4-bit values per byte and processes 16-byte (uint4) vectors in a grid-stride loop, moving the encode onto the device and vectorizing it. This reduces model preprocessing/load time for W4A8, not steady-state inference throughput.

The scheduler sorted the entire `waiting` queue via `self.policy.sort_by_priority(now, self.waiting)` on every scheduling step. The fix removes this sort and instead maintains FCFS order structurally: preempted sequence groups are inserted at the front (`self.waiting.insert(0, seq_group)`) and new groups appended at the back, eliminating a redundant O(n log n) sort in the per-step scheduling hot path.

In TPUWorker.prepare_worker_input, `_make_src_to_dst` was called every execution step for blocks_to_swap_in/out and blocks_to_copy and unconditionally built two `torch.tensor(...)` on the src/dst device (including the XLA/TPU device). In the common case these mappings are empty, so this created empty tensors and issued needless device/XLA graph work each step. The fix adds `if not mapping: return None`, returning early; callers already guard with `is not None` and `numel() > 0`, so the empty-tensor materialization per step is skipped.

On XPU, the worker shutdown path never released device memory pools nor gave oneCCL/Level Zero time to tear down. This commit adds XPUWorker.shutdown() which calls XpuMemAllocator.instance.release_pools() (preventing a device-memory resource leak), sets a default shutdown_timeout=5 so oneCCL/Level Zero resources are released gracefully (comment: without it "subsequent server startups on the same devices may hang during CCL initialization"), adds XPU to the gc.collect()/empty_cache()/synchronize() cleanup path in gpu_model_runner, and guards the CuMemAllocator release to cuda-alike platforms only. It fixes a latent leak/hang, not a specific earlier regression.

When prompt logprobs are requested with chunked prefill, the previous V1 code streamed a full EngineCoreOutput for every partial prefill chunk (to ship partial prompt logprobs) and called torch.cuda.synchronize() on every step in _get_prompt_logprobs_dict, plus allocated a fresh LogprobsTensors per chunk. This commit aggregates chunk slices into a single preallocated CPU tensor (LogprobsTensors.empty_cpu) in the model runner, returns logprobs only once at prefill completion (scheduler now skips partial-prefill outputs; output_processor sets is_prefilling=False), and guards the CUDA sync with `if prompt_logprobs_dict:`. This removes per-chunk output processing/detokenization overhead and a per-step device sync.

Removes hard gates that blocked FP8 KV cache in vLLM V1 ("V1 does not yet support fp8 KV cache" in config.py and "FlashAttention V1 with FP8 KV cache not yet supported" in v1/attention/backends/flash_attn.py) and replaces the FA-version check with a new capability helper flash_attn_supports_fp8() (FA v3 + device capability major==9, i.e. Hopper). This lets users opt into --kv-cache-dtype fp8 under V1, which stores the KV cache in fp8 to reduce GPU memory footprint (allowing larger effective cache / higher concurrency). It is feature enablement of a previously-disabled path, not a fix for a performance regression.

Previously PiecewiseBackend compiled each shape range lazily: __call__ -> _maybe_compile_for_range_entry ran inductor compilation on the first runtime call that hit an uncompiled range, so the first inference request touching a new shape range paid an inline torch.compile/inductor compilation cost as a latency stall on the serving path. This commit moves all compilation up front into __init__ via compile_all_ranges() (using create_concrete_args/get_fake_args_from_graph), and __call__ now merely asserts range_entry.compiled and dispatches. Compilation cost is thereby shifted from the hot request path to warmup/init, eliminating first-request-per-shape stalls.

The EPLB rebalance algorithm (balanced_packing, replicate_experts, rebalance_experts_hierarchical, preserve_intragpu_slots) previously ran on torch tensors on weight.device (typically GPU). The hot spot was replicate_experts' Python loop `for i in range(num_log, num_phy): redundant_indices = (weight / logcnt).max(dim=-1).indices; phy2log[:,i]=...; logcnt[arangen, redundant_indices]+=1` which launches tiny GPU kernels and forces an implicit device sync each iteration on small (num_experts-sized) arrays, plus rebalance_experts_hierarchical's many small gather/scatter_/unflatten GPU ops and repeated .cpu().numpy()/from_numpy().to(device) round-trips per balanced_packing call. This commit converts the whole algorithm to NumPy on CPU, moving to CPU once at the entry (`weight_np = weight.float().cpu().numpy()`) and back to torch/device only at the very end (`torch.from_numpy(phy2log_np).to(device)`), eliminating per-iteration kernel launches and device syncs for these small control-flow-heavy index operations.

The single translation unit csrc/punica/bgmv/bgmv_all.cu instantiated all 18 FOR_BGMV_WIDE_NARROW dtype-combination kernels serially in one compile, which is very slow for CUDA templates. The commit splits it into 18 separate .cu files (one per input/output/weight dtype combo, generated by generator.py) so nvcc can compile them in parallel, and adds --build-arg max_jobs=16 to the docker build so those units actually run concurrently. Net effect: faster build/compile time, no runtime kernel behavior change.

Removes the AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer, which coalesced concurrent encode/decode requests into single batched tokenizer calls within a 2ms window (max_batch_size=32) to amortize per-call tokenizer overhead under concurrent serving load. The renderer is reverted to plain per-request thread-pool offloading via make_async(self._encode/_decode). This is the removal of a throughput optimization on the API tokenization path; net effect (win vs regression) depends on whether the micro-batching overhead/latency outweighed its batching savings, which the commit does not quantify.

The Numba kernel _prepare_prefill_inputs was declared with an explicit type-signature list (@numba.jit([types.none(int32[:], ...)], nopython=True, cache=True)); per the removed source comment "With the type annotations, this function is pre-compiled before the first call," this forces eager Ahead-of-Time compilation at import/decoration time, adding to process startup latency. The commit switches to @numba.njit(cache=True), i.e. lazy JIT that compiles only on first call and reuses the on-disk cache across runs, trading a possible first-call stall for lower/faster startup. Bundled with this is a refactor moving the query_start_loc cumsum out of the numba kernel into np.cumsum in model_runner.py and simplifying the kernel to a vectorized token-copy loop.

Adds an opt-in on-disk cache for media (image/video/audio) fetched from HTTP URLs during multimodal inference. Before fetching, `load_media`/`load_media_async` now call `_get_cached_bytes(url)` and return early on a hit; after fetching, `_put_cached_bytes(url, data)` stores bytes (sha256-keyed filename) with TTL + LRU size-bounded eviction. This eliminates redundant network downloads of the same media across requests, cutting fetch latency. Previously every request re-downloaded the URL — a latent inefficiency, not a regression, since no caching ever existed.

The refactor moves the sampling ops into SamplingStates methods, and in doing so adds a new fast-path guard to apply_temperature: if every request in the batch has temperature 0.0 (greedy) or 1.0 (no-op), it returns early with the comment "No request requires temperature. Skip the kernel launch." Previously apply_temperature(logits, ...) was called unconditionally on every decode step, launching a GPU kernel even when temperature scaling was a no-op. This removes a redundant per-step GPU kernel launch on the common greedy/temp=1.0 path. The min_p and top_k/top_p paths already had do_min_p/do_top_k/do_top_p guards before this commit and are only refactored (no behavior change).

In the DeepEP+DeepGEMM MoE path, several always-inefficient operations are optimized: deepep_ht_prepare_finalize._do_combine replaces a full float32 upcast + elementwise multiply + downcast + `.sum(dim=1)` with an in-place `fused_expert_output.mul_(topk_weights...)` and a fused `ops.moe_sum` writing into a preallocated output, avoiding extra allocations and a float32 round-trip. deep_gemm_moe.apply reuses workspace buffers (quant_out views workspace13 as fp8, and `torch.index_select(..., out=out)` instead of `workspace3[inv_perm]`) to avoid extra allocations. per_token_group_quant_fp8 gains an `out_q` param to reuse a caller buffer. The int64 offset casts in _per_token_group_quant_fp8 are an overflow-correctness fix, not perf.

The old CPU W8A8 path (`DNNLPrimitiveHelper::gemm_s8s8_jit` in dnnl_helper.hpp) reconstructed a `dnnl::matmul::primitive_desc` and instantiated `dnnl::matmul matmul(matmul_pd)` on every GEMM call, and on aarch64 also reordered/repacked the weight (`dnnl::reorder(b_m, mat_weights_mem)`) each call — repeated oneDNN primitive JIT-creation and weight prepacking per forward pass. The refactor adds a `DNNLPrimitiveCache` (LRU list+map keyed by M-size / class key), prepacks the weight once in the `W8A8MatMulPrimitiveHandler` constructor (`prepack_weight(...)`, then the original weight is released), and reuses cached matmul primitives via `get_matmul_cache`/`get_or_create`, eliminating the per-call primitive creation and repacking.

FlashInfer autotuning runs benchmark sweeps over kernel implementations at warmup ("autotuning runs benchmarks for each implementation... Without autotuning, FlashInfer will rely on heuristics, which may be significantly slower"). Previously flashinfer_autotune() wrote its results to a `tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="vllm_flashinfer_autotune_")` and then deleted the dir (`os.unlink`/`os.rmdir`) at the end of every run, so the expensive autotune sweep was re-executed on every process startup. This commit replaces the ephemeral tempdir with a persistent, versioned+hashed cache directory (`VLLM_CACHE_ROOT/flashinfer_autotune_cache/<version>/<arch>/<hash>/autotune_configs.json`, overridable via VLLM_FLASHINFER_AUTOTUNE_CACHE_DIR) so tuned tactics survive across restarts, cutting repeated warmup/startup autotune cost. It also adds a `world.barrier()` before non-leader ranks load configs to avoid a read-before-write race in the multi-rank broadcast path.

Previously vllm/config.py disabled the custom all-reduce kernel entirely whenever pipeline_parallel_size > 1, forcing all tensor-parallel all-reduces onto the slower NCCL path. This commit removes that blanket disable and instead only passes use_custom_allreduce=False to the PP GroupCoordinator (which does point-to-point send/recv, not all-reduce), while the TP group regains the faster custom all-reduce kernel. Net effect: TP all-reduce latency improves in PP+TP deployments.

The grammar_bitmask() path filled per-request bitmasks in a single serial CPU loop (the old code even carries the comment "This outer loop can likely be parallelized to improve performance of bitmask generation for large batches"). The commit dispatches fill_bitmask work in chunks of 16 to a ThreadPoolExecutor (max_workers = min(cpu//2, 8)) when batch > 128 seqs and no spec tokens, parallelizing the CPU-bound xgrammar fill_next_token_bitmask calls. It also caches _is_terminated on XgrammarGrammar to avoid repeated matcher.is_terminated() calls, and adds skip_out_indices to drop the indices arg to the bitmask kernel when shapes already align, plus a .contiguous() on the bitmask tensor.

The old `pixel_shuffle` performed the spatial-to-channel reshuffle in two stages, each ending in `.permute(0, 2, 1, 3).contiguous()`, forcing two materialized intermediate tensor copies per call; `pixel_shuffle_dynamic_res` inlined the same two-copy logic. The rewrite does a single 6-D `x.view(n, new_h, r, new_w, r, c)` followed by one `permute(...).reshape(...)`, removing the redundant `.contiguous()` copies, and consolidates the dynamic-res loop to reuse `pixel_shuffle` + `flatten(1, 2)`. This reduces per-image/per-frame memory copies in the vision encoder path.

Replaces the naive standalone LoRA MoE align kernel (moe_lora_align_sum_kernel: single-threaded prefix-sum done by threadIdx.x==0, single-block-per-LoRA launch <<<max_loras, blockDim>>>, and a conditional-write hack `atomicAdd(&sorted_token_ids[...], (i-numel)*mask)`) with a refactor that reuses the tuned non-LoRA path. The base kernel's warp-based shared_counts counting + `cub::BlockScan` exclusive-sum are factored into shared __device__ functions (`_moe_align_block_size`, `_count_and_sort_expert_tokens`) parameterized by model_offset/token_mask, and the LoRA kernels launch 2 blocks per LoRA (`lora_idx = blockIdx.x/2`, `blockIdx.x % 2` for the fill block) plus a gridDim.y-parallel count-and-sort. This makes the always-slow LoRA alignment step use the same parallelized reduction/sorting as the base MoE align path.

Rewrites the ARM (aarch64) CPU vectorization backend (csrc/cpu/cpu_types_arm.hpp, ~1130 lines changed) to build vector types on top of PyTorch's at::vec::Vectorized<T> via a generic NxVectorizedTVecReg abstraction, and removes the ARM_BF16_SUPPORT compile guard so BF16 vector types (BF16Vec8/16) are always available on ARM. This eliminates scalar fallback paths: e.g. in cpu_attn_impl.hpp the sink-attention BF16->FP32 conversion previously used a scalar `for (int i=0;i<16;++i) s_aux_fp32[i]=static_cast<float>(curr_s_aux[i])` loop when ARM_BF16_SUPPORT was undefined, now always using the vectorized BF16Vec16->FP32Vec16 conversion. It also removed the dispatch guard that dropped BFloat16 from VLLM_DISPATCH_CASE_FLOATING_TYPES on non-BF16 ARM builds.

Padded prompt/output token tensors were built via Python list comprehensions (`tokens + [vocab_size] * pad`) and then `torch.tensor(nested_python_list, ...)`, both of which are slow, per-element Python-loop CPU work on the sampling hot path. The commit preallocates a `np.full((N, max_len), vocab_size, dtype=int64)` array, fills each row in place, and wraps it with `torch.from_numpy` (pinning afterward if needed), avoiding the expensive Python-list construction and torch's nested-list-to-tensor conversion.

This is primarily a refactor conforming the TPU worker/model runner to the shared ModelRunnerBase/ModelRunnerInputBase abstractions, but it carries two incidental latency optimizations on the TPU inference path. (1) In execute_model, a nested _copy_to_device with a `clone: bool` flag was added with the comment: "When x is a slice of a CPU tensor, XLA may copy the whole original tensor to TPU instead of only copying x. To avoid this, we copy x after cloning." — reducing host->TPU transfer volume for per-prompt slices. (2) _prepare_prompt was rewritten to pad EACH prompt to its own smallest power-of-2 length (`_get_padded_prefill_len(prompt_len)` per prompt, flat 1D packing) instead of the old scheme that padded the whole 2D batch to `_get_padded_prefill_len(max(prompt_lens))`, cutting wasted prefill compute/compilation for short prompts in a mixed batch.

In InputBatch.make_dummy, `input_buffers.query_start_loc[0] = 0` assigns a Python scalar to a single element of a CUDA tensor via integer-scalar indexing, which takes a __setitem__ path that forces a host-device (CPU-GPU) synchronization. The fix changes it to `input_buffers.query_start_loc[:1] = 0` (slice assignment), which broadcasts the scalar into the 1-element view via an async fill on the GPU stream, avoiding the stall. This runs during dummy/warmup/CUDA-graph-capture setup.

Adds an opt-in "IndexCache" feature for DeepSeek-V3.2 DSA (sparse attention) models that skips redundant per-layer top-k token selection. In mla.py the indexer call is gated by `not self.skip_topk`; when skipped the layer reuses the `topk_tokens` buffer written by a previous layer. In deepseek_v2.py, per-layer `_skip_topk` is derived from `index_topk_freq` or `index_topk_pattern`, so top-k is computed on only a subset of layers, reducing total sparse-attention compute for deep models.

In GraniteSpeechConformerAttention.forward, pos_attn was computed via torch.sum(query_states.unsqueeze(-2) * rel_pos_emb_expanded, dim=-1), which broadcast query_states (b,n,h,i,1,d) against rel_pos_emb_expanded (1,1,1,i,j,d) and materialized a full b,n,h,i,j,d intermediate product tensor before reducing over d. The commit replaces this with torch.einsum('bnhid,ijd->bnhij', query_states, rel_pos_emb) * self.scale, contracting directly to b,n,h,i,j and eliminating the large intermediate, lowering peak memory.

The DeepSeek-V3.2 sparse indexer's k-cache gather (`cp_gather_indexer_k_quant_cache`) was originally a pure-Python function that looped `for b in range(batch_size)` over requests, doing per-request `torch.arange`, advanced-index gathers, and `torch.cat` on the host, then final `torch.cat`s — many small ops with host/device synchronization overhead. This commit deletes that Python implementation and replaces the call with a single fused CUDA kernel `ops.cp_gather_indexer_k_quant_cache`, collapsing the per-batch loop into one device-side gather (also changing the k_scale layout from [seq,1] float32 to [seq,4] uint8 viewed as float32).

In ExtractHiddenStatesProposer.prepare_next_token_ids_padded (called every decode step in the extract_hidden_states spec-decode path), the parent built the backup token tensor per step via `backup_tokens_gpu = torch.tensor([requests[...].get_token_id(...) for i in range(num_reqs)], dtype=torch.int32, device=device)`, which allocates a fresh GPU tensor and does an implicit host->device transfer each step, plus a `.tolist()` sync of `num_tokens_no_spec`. The commit pre-allocates a reusable pinned `CpuGpuBuffer` (`self.backup_next_token_ids`) in __init__, fills its numpy backing array in the loop, then `copy_to_gpu(num_reqs)` — eliminating the per-step allocation and using a pinned async H2D copy. Latent inefficiency, always slow since the feature was introduced; no earlier commit degraded it.

The fused_moe_lora shrink GEMM is a tall-skinny matmul (contraction dim K=hidden_size large, output N=max_lora_rank small), which under-occupies the GPU because few M×N tiles are launched. This commit makes SplitK functional: the kernel now decodes pid into pid_sk=pid%SPLIT_K and pid_m_n=pid//SPLIT_K, offsets offs_k=pid_sk*BLOCK_SIZE_K+arange(...) so each of the SPLIT_K programs covers a distinct K partition and accumulates its partial product via tl.atomic_add. This parallelizes the K reduction across more CTAs, raising SM occupancy for the small-rank shrink kernel. Prior to this the SplitK grid/atomic scaffolding existed (#27291) but every split program computed identical pid_m/pid_n with offs_k starting at 0, so SPLIT_K>1 was non-functional.

Replaces the runtime cudagraph batch-size padding computation (`get_graph_batch_size` arithmetic plus a `padded_size in _BATCH_SIZES_TO_CAPTURE` membership scan over a ~1027-element list) with a precomputed `bs_to_padded_graph_size: List[int]` table built once at config init, looked up in O(1) via the new `pad_for_cudagraph(batch_size)`. This removes a repeated list-membership scan from the per-step model-runner padding path. Purely a latent inefficiency/refactor cleanup — no earlier commit degraded this behavior.

In the V2 GPUModelRunner, Sampler, RejectionSampler, PromptLogprobsWorker, StructuredOutputsWorker and PoolingRunner were unconditionally constructed in __init__ on every pipeline-parallel rank. Only the last PP rank actually samples, so on non-last ranks these workers wasted GPU memory — notably the Sampler's penalties_state.output_bin_counts, a [max_num_reqs, vocab_size] GPU tensor. The commit guards their creation with `if self.is_last_pp_rank and not self.is_pooling_model:` (and gates PoolingRunner on is_last_pp_rank), making the runner objects Optional and adding None-guards on all use sites; post_update now passes output_bin_counts=None on non-last ranks.

FusedMoEModularKernel.forward previously allocated intermediate gemm/activation workspaces sized to the full token count M (workspace_shapes(a1, a1q, M, ...)) and ran all experts in a single apply() call, so peak workspace memory grew linearly with M and could OOM on large-M (prefill) MoE. This commit adds supports_chunking() to FusedMoEPermuteExpertsUnpermute and, when supported, splits activations into VLLM_FUSED_MOE_CHUNK_SIZE-token chunks, sizing workspace13/workspace2 to CHUNK_SIZE (only the final fused_out is full-M) and looping apply() per chunk. This bounds peak intermediate memory independent of M, matching the chunking that already existed in the non-modular fused_moe.py path.

Enables vLLM's shared-memory CCL (SHM all-reduce/send-recv) path for the CPU backend on Arm/AArch64, which was previously compiled and wired only for x86/AVX512. The commit builds csrc/cpu/shm.cpp for ASIMD (non-Apple), registers the shm_manager torch bindings under `#if defined(__AVX512F__) || defined(__aarch64__)`, lets CpuCommunicator use init_shm_manager when arch==ARM, adds AArch64 acquire/release atomics (replacing x86 volatile + _mm_mfence, and `yield` for `_mm_pause`) to correctly synchronize the SHM ring under the weaker Arm memory model, and adds an INT8Vec64 vector type plus a full-CPU (no-SMT) autobind for Arm workers. Net effect: TP/DP collectives use fast shared-memory transport instead of the generic torch.distributed path, accelerating multi-NUMA CPU inference.

The commit removes the `supports_update_block_table: bool = False` override on `Mamba1AttentionMetadataBuilder`, letting it inherit the base class value of `True`. This flag gates an optimization introduced in #29627 (gpu_model_runner._build_attn_group_metadata): when multiple hybrid KV-cache groups share the same (KVCacheSpec, builder type), vLLM builds the attention metadata once, caches it, and for subsequent groups calls the cheap `builder.update_block_table()` (a `copy.copy` + block-table swap) instead of running the full `build()`/`_compute_common_metadata()` again. Because `BaseMambaAttentionMetadataBuilder` already implements a correct `update_block_table`, the Mamba1 override was unnecessarily forcing a full rebuild per group; removing it enables the shared-metadata caching fast path for Mamba1 hybrid models.

In the scheduler's grammar-bitmask collection method, the change (1) adds an early return `if not scheduler_output.has_structured_output_requests: return None` to skip the per-scheduled-request list comprehension entirely when no request uses structured output (the common case), and (2) adds `and not request.is_prefill_chunk` so requests that are still in chunked prefill (and thus produce no output token this step) no longer set the has_structured_output flag or get a bitmask row. This eliminates a per-scheduling-step CPU no-op that the pre-existing `PERF:` comment explicitly described as 'a big no-op' filling the bitmask during chunked prefill.

fused_experts_impl processed tokens through a serial Python `for chunk in range((num_tokens // CHUNK_SIZE) + 1)` loop keyed on `VLLM_FUSED_MOE_CHUNK_SIZE`, re-running `moe_kernel_quantize_input`, `moe_align_block_size`, and two `dispatch_fused_moe_kernel` launches (+ moe_sum) per chunk. This commit sets `M = num_tokens` and removes the loop, doing input quantization, alignment, and both GEMM dispatches once over the whole batch. It also deletes the `supports_chunking` machinery and the `VLLM_FUSED_MOE_CHUNK_SIZE` env var. This removes per-chunk launch/quantization overhead that only manifested when num_tokens exceeded the chunk size (large prefill / high batch).

The DeepSeek MLA FP8 cache kernel (concat_and_cache_ds_mla_kernel) is restructured for higher memory throughput and lower occupancy overhead. The block size drops from 576 threads to 96 threads (block(576)->block(96)); each thread now handles 8 NoPE elements (vectorized int4 load, uint64 store) and 2 RoPE elements (int32 load/store) instead of 1 scalar element. The per-tile max reduction moves from a full 32-lane warp reduction + shared-memory (smem[20]) staging + two __syncthreads barriers to a half-warp (width-16) VLLM_SHFL_XOR_SYNC_WIDTH reduction with the scale kept in registers, removing shared memory and both syncthreads. This coalesces global memory accesses and eliminates cross-warp synchronization, speeding the cache write path.

Two enablement changes on the ROCm sparse-MLA path: (1) the metadata builder's `_cudagraph_support` is flipped from `AttentionCGSupport.NEVER` to `AttentionCGSupport.UNIFORM_SINGLE_TOKEN_DECODE`, allowing single-token decode steps to be captured into a CUDA/HIP graph and thereby eliminating per-step kernel-launch overhead/bubbles; (2) the hard `aiter_paged_mqa_logits_module = None` override in `rocm_fp8_paged_mqa_logits` is removed so the faster aiter `deepgemm_fp8_paged_mqa_logits_stage1` kernel is used instead of the pure-torch reference fallback. Both were temporary placeholders added when the feature was first introduced, so this enables performance that was never previously working (latent), not a regression restoration.

The static/dynamic INT8 quant CUDA kernels processed the hidden dimension one element at a time in a scalar `for (i=tid; i<hidden_size; i+=blockDim.x) out[i] = float_to_int8_rn(input[i]/scale)` loop, issuing narrow per-element loads/stores. The commit replaces these loops with `vectorize_with_alignment<16>` (new vectorization_utils.cuh), which handles an unaligned prefix scalar-wise then does 16-byte coalesced vectorized loads/stores for the aligned body, improving memory bandwidth utilization of these bandwidth-bound kernels. It also folds division into a precomputed `inv_s` reciprocal-multiply, fuses min/max into one MinMax BlockReduce for the azp path, and reduces the BlockReduce template width from 1024 to 256.

Enables the RMSNorm+quant (RMSNormQuantFusionPass) and Activation+quant (ActivationQuantFusionPass) inductor fusion passes on the XPU platform. These fold the norm/activation and quantization ops into fused kernels, reducing kernel launches and intermediate-tensor traffic. Previously XPU unconditionally listed fuse_norm_quant and fuse_act_quant in fusion_passes_to_disable; this commit removes them from that set, imports the two passes under current_platform.is_xpu(), gates the disable loop on compilation mode != NONE, and fixes the pattern empty-tensor helpers to use current_platform.device_type instead of a hardcoded device='cuda' (which would allocate the wrong device and prevent pattern matching on XPU).

Query FP8 quantization was performed inside the FlashAttention backend via the custom op ops.scaled_fp8_quant, which is an opaque boundary that torch.compile cannot fuse. The commit moves query quantization into the Attention layer using a plain torch op (QuantFP8, PER_TENSOR static), gated by a new backend flag supports_quant_query_input, so torch.compile can fuse the quantization into preceding ops and reduce per-op decoding overhead.

Removes a full-device `torch.accelerator.synchronize()` call from `rearrange_expert_weights_inplace` in the EPLB weight-rebalance path. This call was a global barrier that blocks the CPU until all outstanding GPU work completes, forcing a pipeline stall on every EPLB rearrangement. It was originally added defensively ("We need this synchronize to run, but I don't know why") and, having been determined unnecessary, its removal eliminates the stall. This is dead-work removal of an always-present inefficiency, not restoration of behavior an earlier commit broke.

MLA attention previously (default VLLM_MLA_PERFORM_MATRIX_ABSORPTION) pre-computed and materialized large "absorbed" weight matrices W_Q_UK ([Lq, N*Lkv]) and W_UV_O ([N*Lkv, H]) via einsum in process_weights_after_loading, and at decode projected q_c into a wide [Sq, N*Lkv] latent that inflated the attention/output tensors. This commit removes materialization: it stores only transposed W_UV (N,L,V) and W_UK_T (N,P,L) and computes q_up/v_up projections with per-head torch.bmm at runtime (_q_proj_and_k_up_proj / _v_up_proj_and_o_proj), avoiding the large absorbed-weight copies and the associated FP8 requantization machinery (Fp8LinearGenericOp, scaled_quantize).
