Net effect: reduced weight-memory footprint and less inflated attention compute at the cost of small extra bmm work.

Enables the native HIP C++ per_token_group_fp8_quant kernel on ROCm by removing the `#ifndef USE_ROCM` guards in torch_bindings.cpp/ops.h, building the .cu unconditionally in CMakeLists.txt, including <hip/hip_fp8.h>, and adding 64-lane-wavefront-correct shuffle reductions (GroupReduceMax and the packed register kernel use `warpSize`-based masks with explicit width). Because per_token_group_quant_fp8() in fp8_utils.py already routes via `current_platform.is_cuda_alike()`, ROCm previously fell through to the slower Triton fallback; now it uses the fused C++/HIP kernel. Additionally kFp8Dynamic128Sym/kFp8Dynamic64Sym are registered to per_token_group_fp8_quant in QUANT_OPS, enabling RMSNorm+block-fp8-quant fusion (rms_norm_per_block_quant) that could not fire before.

vllm/envs.py's module-level __getattr__ re-evaluates each environment variable on every access (each entry in environment_variables is a lambda calling os.environ.get plus parsing/casting). The commit adds enable_envs_cache(), which wraps __getattr__ in functools.cache after service init (called in EngineCoreProc post-init and WorkerProc after load_model), so repeated env var reads on hot paths hit a cache instead of recomputing/reparsing os.environ each time.

During model/config loading, vLLM checked for the existence of multiple config files on huggingface.co, each check issuing a separate HTTP round-trip via huggingface_hub.file_exists. This commit introduces a @cache'd list_repo_files() and reimplements file_exists() as a membership test on that cached file list, so repeated existence checks for the same repo collapse into a single HTTP request. It also @caches get_pooling_config and get_sentence_transformer_tokenizer_config to avoid re-fetching. Net effect: fewer network round-trips → lower startup latency, especially over slow/rate-limited connections to HF.

The old `_sample` looped over each sequence group in Python ("# TODO(woosuk): Optimize."), calling `torch.argmax`/`torch.multinomial`/`torch.topk` and `_sample_from_prompt`/`_sample_from_generation_tokens` per-sequence, each with `.item()`/`.tolist()` GPU→CPU syncs inside the loop — so sampling overhead scaled linearly with batch size and serialized many tiny kernel launches. The rewrite categorizes sequences by `SamplingType` (GREEDY/RANDOM/BEAM) and runs one batched `torch.argmax(logprobs, dim=-1).cpu()` and `torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=max_best_of, ...).cpu()` per category, plus a single batched `torch.topk` for logprobs, collapsing per-sequence syncs into one transfer per category.

MoonViT's vision encoder is switched from fully-replicated linears (ReplicatedLinear for wqkv/wo/MLP) plus an ad-hoc attention dispatch (VL_VISION_ATTENTION_FUNCTIONS over flash_attn_2/sdpa) to (a) tensor-parallel linears (QKVParallelLinear/ColumnParallelLinear/RowParallelLinear with disable_tp keyed off mm_encoder_tp_mode, and num_heads split per TP partition via divide(num_heads, tp_size)), and (b) the shared MMEncoderAttention layer, which selects device-specific backends (FlashAttn, ROCm AITER FA, XPU FA, TPU PALLAS, torch SDPA) and honors mm_encoder_attn_backend. This distributes vision-encoder compute across TP ranks and unlocks faster/hardware-native attention kernels instead of the previous replicated-only, fa2-or-sdpa path.

Adds a `deepstack_input_embeds_num_tokens` counter to Qwen3-VL and Qwen3-Omni-MoE thinker models so that `_get_deepstack_input_embeds` and `_clear_deepstack_input_embeds` early-return when the buffer holds no active payload (counter == 0). Previously `_clear_deepstack_input_embeds` unconditionally launched `deepstack_input_embeds[idx][:num_tokens].zero_()` GPU kernels across all `deepstack_num_level` levels every forward step; the guard skips these redundant zeroing kernels on steps with no deepstack payload. The added `raise ValueError` branches are safety guards, not the perf change.

This warmup module pre-triggers Triton JIT compilation of Qwen kernels so they don't compile on the serving hot path. The commit (a) removes the explicit num_warps=4/num_stages=3 overrides from the _zero_kv_blocks_kernel warmup launch so its @triton.jit compile key matches production, and (b) rewires control flow so _warm_zero_kv_blocks_kernel always runs when zero_config is present (previously skipped when the runner-zeroer path succeeded). Production launches the kernel with default warps/stages (see KVBlockZeroer.zero_block_ids in utils.py, which computes blk_size and launches without warps/stages), so the prior warmup compiled an unused variant and the real kernel would still JIT during serving.

Reduces per-scheduler-iteration Python object allocations on the CPU-side hot path. Introduces a `PyObjectCache` (utils.py) that pre-allocates and recycles `SequenceGroupMetadata`, `SchedulerRunningOutputs`, and `ScheduledSequenceGroup` objects across iterations instead of allocating fresh ones each step; a `BlockTable` class (block.py) that caches `block_ids` so `get_block_table` returns cached `.ids()` rather than rebuilding `[block.block_number for block in block_table]` each call; fast-paths for single-token decode slot mapping (`compute_slot_mapping` add_slot for seq_len-1) and single-waiting-seq block-table assignment (avoids `.copy()`); and list-merge optimizations in `_schedule_default` that extend cached lists instead of concatenating with `+`. All target steady-state e2e CPU overhead, not a GPU kernel.

The n-gram speculative-decode proposer previously looped `for n in range(self.max_n, self.min_n - 1, -1)` calling `_find_subarray_kmp` once per n, each doing a fresh KMP scan over the whole context plus rebuilding an lps array — up to (max_n-min_n+1) full O(context_len) passes per propose() call. The commit replaces this with a single `_find_longest_matched_ngram_and_propose_tokens` numba-jitted function that does ONE KMP pass over the reversed tokens to find the longest suffix-matching n-gram, collapsing the repeated scans into a single O(context_len) traversal.

Consolidates padded-tensor construction into make_tensor_with_pad/make_ndarray_with_pad and applies host-side micro-optimizations: numpy padding switches from `np.zeros([len(x),max_len],dtype=np.int32)+pad` (two allocations + add) to a single `np.full((len(x),max_len),pad,dtype=dtype)`; max-length computation switches from a `max(len(...) for ...)` genexpr to `max(map(len, x))` (per the added comment "map is faster than a genexpr over len"); and duplicated `max_block_table_len = max(len(bt) for bt ...)` passes at attention/model-runner call sites are removed since the function now derives max_len internally. In sampling_metadata.py the numpy-then-torch.from_numpy penalty-token build is replaced by the consolidated helper with pin_memory. These shrink per-step CPU overhead on the input/sampling tensor build path.

During streaming tool-call parsing, the old Gemma4 parser re-ran tool_call_event over the entire accumulated buffer on every incoming chunk, re-scanning the whole argument body from the start each time to find the (not-yet-arrived) TOOL_CALL_END marker — O(n^2) in argument length as it streams in chunks. The fix introduces a Gemma4Mode state machine with a persistent Gemma4ArgsScanState { scanned_len, in_string } so gemma4_raw_args_until_tool_call_end resumes scanning from scanned_len each chunk, examining each byte once → O(n). Argument JSON is parsed only once at the end via parse_gemma4_args.

In each schedule() step the waiting queue was traversed and any blocked requests (WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS / WAITING_FOR_FSM / WAITING_FOR_STREAMING_REQ) were popped into a per-step temporary queue and re-prepended to the head of self.waiting at the end. Under PD disaggregation many requests sit in WAITING_FOR_REMOTE_KVS for multiple steps, so they were repeatedly re-scanned at the head of the waiting queue every step — redundant scheduler CPU work. The fix introduces a persistent self.skipped_waiting queue (populated at enqueue via _enqueue_waiting_request and _is_blocked_waiting_status) so blocked requests no longer pollute/re-scan the main waiting queue; _select_waiting_queue_for_scheduling picks between the two while preserving FCFS/priority order.

The `kv_block_hashes` accessor was a `@property` that constructed a fresh `ConstantList(self._kv_block_hashes)` wrapper object on every access. Since this attribute is read frequently on the scheduler/prefix-caching hot path per request, it caused repeated small object allocations. The fix precomputes the `ConstantList` once in `__init__` (and re-creates it only inside `set_kv_block_hashes` when the backing list is replaced), turning it into a plain cached attribute so reads incur no allocation.

gc.freeze() moves all live static heap objects out of GC's scanning scope so periodic oldest-generation collections don't repeatedly traverse them, reducing GC pause times during serving. Previously this freeze was only applied in EngineCoreProc (the subprocess/multiproc path). This commit relocates the freeze into a helper freeze_gc_heap() (which does staged gc.collect(0/1/2) then gc.freeze()) and calls it in the EngineCore base __init__, so in-process engine usage that never goes through EngineCoreProc also benefits. It also adds gc.unfreeze() before distributed cleanup so frozen objects can be reclaimed at shutdown.

Speculative-decode scoring previously used "batch expansion": each proposed token position was expanded into a separate pseudo-sequence and fed to the target model, so the scoring forward pass cost grew with batch size × proposal length. This commit adds an MQAScorer (vllm/spec_decode/mqa_scorer.py) that scores all k+1 candidate positions per request in a single multi-query forward pass using a new decode_query_len attention-metadata field in the flash-attn backend, avoiding the redundant expanded batch. It becomes the default scorer (disable_mqa_scorer defaults False) when flash-attn backend + enforce_eager + non-ngram + matching draft/target max_model_len hold. This reduces per-step scoring latency rather than fixing a regression.

DeepSeekV2FusedQkvAProj.forward had a Python-level data-dependent branch (`if 0 < num_tokens <= 16`) selecting the min-latency `ops.dsv3_fused_a_gemm` kernel for small batches. Data-dependent branching on the dynamic num_tokens dim is not supported under vLLM's torch.compile integration (causing graph breaks/recompiles or the fast path being unusable in the compiled model). The commit moves the branch into a `direct_register_custom_op` (`min_latency_fused_qkv_a_proj`) with a fake impl, making it an opaque op so the compiled graph stays intact while still dispatching to the min-latency GEMM for num_tokens<=16.

Structured outputs (grammar bitmask) were previously incompatible with async scheduling because the bitmask must be computed after the prior step's sampled tokens are known. This commit splits model execution into two phases (`execute_model` returning None, then `sample_tokens(grammar_output)`), adds a `pending_structured_output_tokens` flag on SchedulerOutput (set by AsyncScheduler._update_after_schedule), and factors the bitmask into a separate GrammarOutput. In step_with_batch_queue, when structured-output tokens are pending the GPU execute still runs async while only sampling is deferred until the previous output is processed; otherwise the grammar bitmask is fetched immediately and sampling is queued async. This lets structured-output requests use async scheduling's pipelining (overlapping GPU compute with CPU/scheduler work) instead of falling back to a fully-synchronous path, reducing pipeline bubbles / improving throughput for that workload.

Speculative decoding always computed token log probabilities in both draft and target models and serialized the full sampler output (logprobs tensors) from GPU to CPU each step. Because the finally-accepted tokens may differ from the target model's sampled tokens, that logprob compute + host sync was wasted work. This commit adds a `disable_logprobs` path (default True) that, via the new `TargetModelRunner.prepare_model_input`, sets `model_input.sampling_metadata.skip_sampler_cpu_output = self.disable_logprobs`, so only the sampled_token_id GPU tensors are serialized and the CPU logprob sync/serialization is skipped — reducing per-step decode latency.

Adds a new SM100 (Blackwell) CUTLASS block-scaled grouped GEMM path for MXFP4 W4A4 MoE: mxfp4_blockwise_moe_kernel.cu implements a grouped GEMM using cutlass::mx_float4_t operands with E8M0 (float_ue8m0_t) block scales and 32-element groups (KernelPtrArrayTmaWarpSpecialized1SmMxf4Sm100, MmaTileShape 128x128x128), plus mxfp4_experts_quant.cu for per-expert FP4 quantization, exposed via new custom ops (cutlass_mxfp4_group_mm, silu_and_mul_mxfp4_experts_quant) and wired into fused_moe/cutlass_moe.py. It provides a native low-precision (4-bit weight & activation) tensor-core MoE path that previously did not exist for this hardware, improving MoE GEMM throughput.

Adds a "padded" drafting path for EAGLE speculative decoding, enabled by default via the new config `disable_padded_drafter_batch=False`. Instead of removing rejected tokens and building a compacted, variable-length draft batch (which requires copying sampled_token_ids to CPU and per-request index gymnastics), the new `prepare_next_token_ids_padded`/`prepare_inputs_padded` keep a uniform padded batch and compute next-token-ids and valid-token counts entirely on-device. The padded next-token function's docstring explicitly states it "must use device functions... and should not introduce any blocking CPU-GPU synchronization." Keeping token indices undisturbed ("Deferred computation should not disturb the original indices") preserves fixed shapes so cudagraphs/uniform-decode shapes can be reused across draft steps, reducing per-step CPU-GPU sync and rebuild overhead in the multi-token draft loop.

Adds an opt-in `use_inductor_graph_partition` config (default False) that performs piecewise-CUDAGraph splitting inside Inductor's codegen (at `cudagraph_unsafe`-tagged ops) *after* all fusions, instead of vLLM's Dynamo/FX-level piecewise split which happens before fusion. Attention custom ops are tagged `torch._C.Tag.cudagraph_unsafe`; a `maybe_use_cudagraph_partition_wrapper` context manager registers a custom Inductor partition wrapper that wraps each partition with vLLM's static CUDAGraph wrapper. Motivation is that FX-level splitting fragments the graph before Inductor fusion, whereas partitioning post-fusion allows more fusion (e.g. attention+quant fusion across the whole graph) while still keeping attention outside cudagraphs, and supports both full and piecewise cudagraph 'without compiling twice' (docstring).

Extends the vision-encoder "full CUDA graph" capture/replay framework (previously image-only) to also cover video inputs for Qwen3-VL. Video items use different mm_kwargs keys (pixel_values_videos/video_grid_thw) and contribute T frames per item, so the manager gains a max_frames_per_batch budget and a modality-routing protocol (get_input_modality / input_key_by_modality) that lets video forwards run via pre-captured CUDA graphs instead of eager launches, removing per-launch CPU/kernel-launch overhead for the ViT encoder. It is a new fast path for a workload that was always run eagerly, not a fix for a regression.

Rewrites the per-token fp8 group-quant "packed" CUDA kernel (group_size==128) from a shared-memory design into a register-resident fast path: each thread holds 16 source elements (2× uint4 = 32 B) in registers across absmax-reduce → scale → quantize, eliminating shared memory and the __syncthreads() barrier. It uses an 8-lane (THREADS_PER_GROUP=8, down from 16) intra-warp __shfl_xor_sync reduction, vectorized 128-bit loads/stores, and bit-math UE8M0 scale extraction, so no DRAM double-read via smem is needed. This makes an already-working quant kernel faster (latent inefficiency), not a fix for a prior regression.

Adds a native FP8 matrix-core (MFMA) path to the ROCm custom paged-attention decode kernel for gfx942/gfx950. Previously fp8 KV cache was up-converted to fp16 (`convert_b8x8_custom`) and fed to the 16x16x16 fp16 MFMA instruction; the new path computes Q dynamic scaling (q_max via warpReduceMax -> q_scale, target 224.0) and runs QK^T and PV directly with the wider `gcn_mfma16x16x32_instr<__hip_fp8_e4m3>` fp8_fp8/bf8_bf8 MFMA instructions, avoiding the fp8->fp16 upconversion and using higher-throughput fp8 matrix-core ops. It is a new opt-in fast path (MFMAType::Fp8), not a fix to a prior slowdown.

In NIXL P/D disaggregation, decode (D) must notify prefill (P) after pulling KV blocks so P can free them. If a request was aborted while still in D's waiting queue, `update_state_after_alloc` was never called so `do_remote_prefill` stayed True, and `request_finished` fell through its early return doing nothing — D never sent a free-notification, permanently stranding (leaking) the prefill KV-cache blocks on P. The fix detects `do_remote_prefill` still True at request_finished and enqueues the request with empty block_ids into `_reqs_need_recv` so the worker notifies and frees P's blocks.

Adds prefix caching: shared prompt prefixes are stored once (new Prefix/PrefixPool in vllm/prefix.py, block_manager reserves/reuses prefix blocks via `seq_group.prefix.allocated` so `num_required_blocks -= seq_group.prefix.get_num_blocks()`), and a new Triton `context_attention_fwd` kernel (triton_kernel/prefix_prefill.py) computes attention over already-cached context KV plus the new query tokens. This avoids recomputing the shared prefix's KV for every request, reducing prompt-phase compute and improving throughput/TTFT when many requests share a common prefix.

Adds a hand-tuned Triton fused-MoE kernel config JSON (E=64,N=896,device_name=NVIDIA_H20.json) providing per-batch-size BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K, GROUP_SIZE_M, num_warps, num_stages. The fused_moe kernel looks up this file at runtime; when present it uses these tuned launch parameters instead of the generic default heuristic, speeding up the DeepSeek VL2 tiny MoE layer on H20. This is a latent inefficiency (the shape was always run with fallback defaults), not a regression. The benchmark_moe.py edits (add --model-prefix, switch AutoConfig->get_config, getattr(torch, config.torch_dtype)) are perf-infra/tuning-script changes that generate such configs.

Adds a new torch.compile fusion pass (MLAAttnQuantFusionPass) that matches the pattern `unified_mla_attention_with_output` op -> FP8-static / NVFP4-dynamic quant and rewrites it so the attention op writes the quantized output directly (passing output_scale / output_block_scale and an FP8/FP4 output buffer), collapsing the separate quant op. The stated intent (docs/design/fusions.md) is "eliminating a full-precision memory round-trip of the attention output." It extends the existing `fuse_attn_quant` optimization (dense Attention, 3-7%) to MLAAttention (DeepSeek-V2/V3/V3.2). This is a latent-inefficiency optimization of an always-slow quant path, not a regression fix — no earlier commit degraded this; the code path never had the fusion.

Adds an atomicAdd-based partial-sum reduction path to the GPTQ/AWQ Marlin GEMM kernel as an alternative to the barrier-synchronized global reduce. When enabled, threadblocks accumulate directly into a zero-initialized output C via atomicAdd (skipping the lock acquire/release + global_reduce and the fp32 C_tmp buffer). A heuristic should_use_atomic_add_reduce() selects this path when max(m,64)*n < 64*2048 and k>=2048, i.e. small output dimension n with large reduction dim k — the case where the global reduce's synchronization overhead dominates. This makes an always-slow small-n path faster; it is not restoring performance broken by an earlier commit.

Replaces the custom CUDA punica BGMV (batched GEMV) kernels — which were used for both prefill and decode LoRA computation — with Triton kernels that add a dedicated SGMV (GroupGEMM + SPLIT-K) path for the prefill stage. punica.py's `shrink_prefill`/`expand_prefill` now dispatch to `sgmv_shrink`/`sgmv_expand` (grouped GEMM over sequences) while decode keeps `bgmv_*`; the sgmv kernel docstring states "introducing SPLIT-K can improve performance", and `compute_meta` merges consecutive requests using the same LoRA via `unique_consecutive` "improving sgmv kernel inference performance", plus a `no_lora` fast-path skips the kernel launch entirely. Doing per-token GEMV for many-token prefill was always suboptimal; grouped GEMM is the more efficient formulation. No earlier commit degraded this — the CUDA BGMV path was always there — so this is a latent-inefficiency optimization, not a regression fix.

Model Runner V2's execute_model previously hardcoded `cudagraph_runtime_mode=CUDAGraphMode.NONE` with a `# TODO(woosuk): Support piecewise CUDA graph.` on the eager path, so batches that weren't full-graph decode ran fully in eager mode with no CUDA graph capture, incurring per-op CPU launch overhead. This commit adds a piecewise capture routine (`_capture_piecewise_graph`) plus a runtime dispatcher (`get_cudagraph_runtime_mode`) that selects FULL / PIECEWISE / NONE per batch (mixed prefill-decode and uniform-decode), routing eligible batches through torch.compile's CUDAGraphWrapper piecewise graphs and adding DP-synced mode selection in dp_utils. This reduces kernel-launch overhead / GPU bubbles for mixed and piecewise-capturable batches that were previously eager-only.

Full rewrite of the CPU attention backend. Replaces the old paged-attention v1/v2 kernels (csrc/cpu/attention.cpp, cache.cpp — deleted) with a new scheduler-driven, ISA-dispatched flash-attention-style implementation (cpu_attn.cpp + cpu_attn_impl.hpp + cpu_attn_vec/vec16.hpp). Critically it adds an AMX BF16 tile path (cpu_attn_amx.hpp, CPU_CAPABILITY_AMXBF16, -mamx-bf16/-mamx-tile) selected via ISA::AMX dispatch, accelerating QK/PV matmuls on Intel CPUs with AMX. This makes an always-slow path faster (adds a new hardware-accelerated kernel); it does not restore performance broken by a prior commit.

Under async scheduling, the model runner previously did a blocking GPU->CPU sync to materialize sampled_token_ids each step ("NOTE: GPU -> CPU Sync happens here"), stalling the GPU between iterations. This commit adds AsyncGPUModelRunnerOutput which launches the sampled_token_ids.to('cpu', non_blocking=True) copy on a separate async_output_copy_stream guarded by a CUDA event, and caches prev_sampled_token_ids on the GPU so the next step's _prepare_input_ids scatters them directly into input_ids on-device (avoiding the host round-trip). The worker also offloads get_output()/enqueue via a dedicated WorkerAsyncOutputCopy thread. Together these remove the CPU sync from the critical path so the next forward can be scheduled while the previous output copies, fully overlapping execution.

Adds a new opt-in all2all communication backend "flashinfer_all2allv" for MoE expert-parallel dispatch/combine using MNNVL (multi-node NVLink) FlashInfer trtllm kernels (FlashInferAllToAllManager + FlashInferAllToAllMoEPrepareAndFinalize calling MnnvlMoe.mnnvl_moe_alltoallv). This provides a faster/more scalable comm path than the default allgather+reducescatter for DP MoE. Separately, NaiveAll2AllManager.dispatch/combine are rewritten from naive_multicast + full all_reduce to all_gatherv/reduce_scatterv, replacing a full-tensor all-reduce with variable-sized collectives (less redundant communication/compute).

Mamba2 SSD Triton kernels (_state_passing_fwd, _chunk_scan_fwd) autotune lazily on first invocation, keyed by dtype and the HAS_INITSTATES constexpr. Without warmup this JIT autotuning ran during the first real inference request, causing a latency spike (and possible OOM since SSM cache is already allocated by then). The commit adds _warmup_ssd_kernels(), invoked during the V1 profile run (attn_metadata is None) while GPU memory is plentiful, running a minimal SSD forward for both HAS_INITSTATES=False/True code paths so neither triggers compilation during serving.

Adds "cpp.dynamic_threads": True to the CPU inductor_compile_config. This makes torch inductor's generated C++/OpenMP kernels query the OpenMP thread count at runtime instead of baking a fixed thread count in at compile time. Because vLLM's CPU path sets OMP_NUM_THREADS / thread binding around compilation, the compiled kernels would otherwise trigger repeated OpenMP thread-pool reallocation on invocation (the "thread reallocation" named in the subject), adding per-call overhead; the flag avoids that churn.

Adds a new fused Marlin MoE CUDA kernel (csrc/moe/marlin_moe_ops.cu, ~1740 lines, using m16n8k16 tensor-core MMA, cp.async pipelining, and int4->fp16 LOP3 dequant) plus a new Python entry point fused_marlin_moe() and compressed_tensors_moe integration. This provides a quantized (int4/GPTQ-Marlin) fused-MoE GEMM path so MoE expert matmuls run on Marlin's fast quantized kernels instead of dequantizing to a slower dense/Triton path — a throughput optimization for quantized MoE models. Also expands MoE weight loading to support the new layout.

Adds three autotuned Triton kernel launch-config JSON files (E=16/N=1344, E=8/N=1792, E=8/N=3584) for NVIDIA A100-SXM4-40GB. The fused MoE kernel selects BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K, GROUP_SIZE_M, num_warps, num_stages per batch size by looking up a file keyed on device_name + expert count (E) + intermediate size (N). Without an A100-40GB-specific file, the kernel fell back to generic/default heuristic configs; these tuned values improve MoE GEMM throughput/latency on that GPU for the covered shapes. No prior commit degraded this — the hardware simply never had specialized configs (latent inefficiency).

Adds a new opt-in FlashInfer attention backend (vllm/attention/backends/flashinfer.py) that uses flashinfer's BatchDecodeWithPagedKVCacheWrapper for the decode stage, plus a companion reshape_and_cache_flash CUDA kernel matching flashinfer's paged KV layout (num_blocks, 2, block_size, num_kv_heads, head_size). It's an alternative, faster paged-attention decode kernel path; the pre-existing FLASH_ATTN/XFORMERS backends remain the default. No prior commit degraded decode performance — this is a new kernel option, i.e. a latent-inefficiency optimization rather than a regression fix.

The `fused_qk_norm_rope` CUDA kernel (a fusion of QK RMSNorm + RoPE) was previously entirely gated out on ROCm via `#ifndef USE_ROCM` around the kernel body, the torch_binding registration, and the BF16 `_typeConvert` specialization, and the compilation pass `enable_qk_norm_rope_fusion` was force-disabled when `not current_platform.is_cuda()`. This commit makes the kernel ROCm-compatible (64-bit `FINAL_MASK` for AMD's 64-lane wavefront, enabling BF16 conversion for ROCm 7.0+, removing the `USE_ROCM` guards) and lets the fusion pass run on ROCm (`is_cuda_alike()`), so AMD GPUs now execute one fused kernel instead of separate norm + RoPE ops — a throughput/latency win. No earlier commit regressed ROCm; the fused path simply never existed there.

The AiterFlashAttention (ROCm) backend previously did the reshape_and_cache KV-cache write inside `forward`. This commit removes that block from `forward`, sets `forward_includes_kv_cache_update = False`, and moves the reshape_and_cache_flash / reshape_and_cache_shuffle_triton write into a standalone `do_kv_cache_update` method. This decouples the KV-cache update op from the attention compute so the runtime can schedule/overlap it separately, matching vLLM's existing framework used by other backends (FlashInfer, FlexAttention, Tree, CPU). It is applying an existing perf mechanism to a backend that always ran the update inline — a latent inefficiency, not a restored regression.

The Qwen2.5-VL ViT encoder CUDA graph sized cu_window_seqlens by token count (min(max_num_batched_tokens, max_model_len)) instead of by the actual number of non-empty window-attention sequences. This over-pads the window-attention metadata so FlashAttention launches many empty CTAs during graph replay (wasted GPU work / low effective util). The fix introduces get_encoder_cudagraph_max_window_seqs() which computes a tight geometry-driven bound (ceil(token_budget/window_side) + max_sequence_units, clamped to token_budget), and adds per-buffer padding_logics so replay pads cu_seqlens/cu_window_seqlens to the actual batch rather than a loose static upper bound.

On the CPU backend, top-k/top-p sampling was routed to the PyTorch-native `apply_top_k_top_p_pytorch` path, which for top-p sorts the full vocab logits tensor ("which can be slow for large batches" per its docstring). This commit routes CPU to `apply_top_k_top_p_triton` when `HAS_TRITON`, replacing the O(vocab·log vocab) sort with the Triton single-pass/binary-search kernel, and adds CPU-tuned tile sizes (BLOCK_SIZE 256 / BLOCK_SIZE_TRUNC 128 on CPU vs 8192/4096 on GPU) plus a CPU `num_compute_units` returning `torch.get_num_threads()` to size kernel parallelism. This removes the vocab sort from the CPU sampling hot path.

Adds one more triton.Config candidate (BLOCK_M=128, BLOCK_N=64, waves_per_eu=1, PRE_LOAD_V=False, num_warps=4) to the @triton.autotune config list of the ROCm/AMD Triton flash-attention forward kernel (attn_fwd). The autotuner benchmarks all candidate configs (keyed on IS_CAUSAL/dropout_p/BLOCK_DMODEL) and selects the fastest; supplying a previously-missing config lets the autotuner pick a better-performing tiling for certain shapes on AMD GPUs, reducing attention kernel latency. No prior commit removed this config, so it is a missing/latent tuning option rather than a restored regression.

The sparse Marlin (2:4) tensor-core matmul used the `mma.sp.sync.aligned.m16n8k32...` PTX instruction. On CUDA 12.5+ this non-ordered-metadata variant triggers a compiler advisory that it "is expected to have substantially reduced performance on some future architectures". The commit adds an `MMA_SP_INST` macro that selects `mma.sp::ordered_metadata...` when `CUDA_VERSION >= 12500`, keeping the plain `.sp` variant on older toolkits where the ordered-metadata form is unsupported. This selects the higher-performance instruction encoding on newer archs/toolkits.

Replaces the TPU V1 attention backend's separate prefill/decode paths (flash_attention, multi_queries_paged_attention, and a decode paged_attention path that split the batch into chunks and invoked the kernel multiple times to stay under a 512KB SMEM limit) with a single torch.ops.xla.ragged_paged_attention call. The old PallasMetadata forced batches to be all-prefill or all-decode (prefill_metadata/decode_metadata assert num_decode_tokens==0 / num_prefills==0); the new ragged kernel takes query_start_loc + num_seqs and a flattened [num_tokens, ...] layout, enabling mixed prefill+decode in one kernel launch and removing per-chunk relaunches and padding-to-[batch,seq_len]. This is a new-kernel integration/rewrite improving TPU attention efficiency, not a fix to a regression introduced by an earlier commit.

Reworks the CPU OpenMP thread-binding / NUMA-affinity mechanism that governs CPU core utilization for multiproc workers. The old path bound threads via a native C++ `init_cpu_threads_env` (sched_setaffinity in an OMP parallel region + numa_migrate_pages) plus a fragile LD_PRELOAD of PyTorch's libgomp to work around the "only one core will be properly utilized when we thread-bind" bug. The new `OMPProcessManager` instead computes per-worker CPU masks from `lscpu` and exports `OMP_PLACES`/`OMP_NUM_THREADS`/`OMP_PROC_BIND=TRUE` BEFORE each worker process is forked in the mp executor, so OpenMP binds each worker to distinct physical cores/NUMA node — the standard, robust way to get full multi-core CPU utilization. Improves/robustifies CPU throughput via correct thread affinity; not a fix that restores a specific prior perf level.

Adds a native AITER CK kernel backend (Mxfp4MoeBackend.AITER_MXFP4_MXFP4, W4A4) for quark OCP-MX MoE on ROCm GFX950. Previously the w_mxfp4_a_mxfp4 scheme had no native backend and fell to EMULATION, which does simulated weight dequant + activation QDQ with linear layers computed in high precision (the warning log removed in this commit describes it). The commit routes W4A4 via select_mxfp4_moe_backend(activation_key=kMxfp4Dynamic) to the CK kernel, adds e8m0_shuffle/shuffle_weights weight-format conversion and native float4_e2m1fn_x2 view, and permits (kMxfp4Static, kMxfp4Dynamic) in AiterExperts.is_supported_config — replacing the slow emulation path with a fast fused FP4 kernel.

Multi-LoRA optimizations for the V1 TPU backend along several axes: (1) the bgmv custom op is reimplemented from a hand-written Pallas TPU kernel (deleted vllm/lora/ops/xla_ops/pallas.py) to a JAX einsum with one_hot indexing (bgmv_jax), simplifying the LoRA batched matvec path; (2) buffer donation is enabled via torch.ops.xla.dynamo_set_buffer_donor_ on the LoRA index tensors and shrink/expand output y, allowing XLA to reuse/donate buffers and avoid copies; (3) torch.cat-based zero-padding in bgmv_expand/bgmv_expand_slice is replaced with F.pad, avoiding extra allocations/concats; (4) the shrink() path drops the y output arg and no_lora early-out, and prompt mapping is padded to a power-of-2 (>=MIN_NUM_SEQS) in _pad_prompt_mapping 'to avoid running into recompiles on the TPU', reducing XLA recompilation. It also activates the previously-disabled TPU LoRA tests.

In the LoRA prefill path, add_shrink/add_expand previously issued one Triton kernel launch per LoRA slice (e.g. 3 launches for fused QKV, 2 for gate_up) via sgmv_shrink / sgmv_expand_slice. This commit fuses them into a single grouped-GEMM kernel: the new _sgmv_expand_kernel uses a 3D launch grid (M-tiles, batches, len(lora_b_weights)) with slice_id = tl.program_id(axis=2), so all slices are computed in one launch. Per-slice weight pointers/strides are precomputed once and cached in _LORA_A_PTR_DICT/_LORA_B_PTR_DICT (LUT populated during profile_run), removing repeated pointer setup and reducing kernel-launch overhead on the prefill critical path.

The DeepGEMM fp8_mqa_logits/fp8_paged_mqa_logits kernels perform a "clean_logits" step that writes float(-inf) into all unfilled/invalid logit positions. Because the downstream top_k_per_row consumer already honors valid row boundaries (row_ends), this masking is redundant. The commit threads a clean_logits param through vllm/utils/deep_gemm.py and passes clean_logits=False in sparse_attn_indexer.py's two call sites, skipping that fill work on the sparse-attention runtime path.

Upgrades the ROCm AITER fused-MoE integration: replaces several specialized dispatch paths (rocm_aiter_ck_moe_2stages, rocm_aiter_asm_moe, rocm_aiter_fmoe_fp8_blockscale_g1u1) with a single unified aiter.fused_moe(...) custom op (rocm_aiter_fused_moe_impl) parameterized by QuantMethod/ActivationMethod IntEnums, and switches the weight-shuffle layout from (32,32) to the new default (16,16) in layer.py. This routes MoE through the newer/faster AITER kernel entry point for MI300-class GPUs rather than fixing a specific regression.

Adds an optional FlashInfer ragged-prefill path for MLA attention. A new `use_flashinfer_prefill()` gate and `FlashInferPrefillMetadata` route MLA prefill (main + chunked context) through `BatchPrefillWithRaggedKVCacheWrapper(..., backend="cutlass")` instead of the default flash-attn varlen kernel. On supported hardware this replaces the FA2 prefill kernel with a faster cutlass-based FlashInfer kernel for the compute-bound prefill phase of MLA (DeepSeek-style) models. The rest of the diff is supporting refactor: extracting `PerLayerParameters`/`get_per_layer_parameters`/`infer_global_hyperparameters` into shared utils, moving `validate_kv_sharing_target`, adding `debug_once` logging, and a test-connector refactor.

Adds a new tuned Triton config JSON for the fused MoE kernel keyed to E=256,N=384 (MiniMax-M2 expert shape), NVIDIA H100 80GB HBM3, fp8_w8a8, block_shape=[128,128]. Before this file existed the kernel would fall back to default/heuristic tile configs; the file supplies per-batch-size BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K, GROUP_SIZE_M, num_warps, num_stages so the kernel launches with tiling tuned to this shape/hardware, improving MoE GEMM throughput. No prior config was replaced, so this is a latent-inefficiency optimization, not a regression fix.

Adds an opt-in "fully sharded" LoRA scheme (new file vllm/lora/fully_sharded_layers.py, flag --fully-sharded-loras default False) that shards both the LoRA A (rank dim) and B computations across tensor-parallel ranks using all-gather/all-reduce, instead of the default scheme where only half the LoRA compute is TP-sharded. New bgmv CUDA kernels (FOR_INST_BGMV_ONESIDE / INST_BGMV_ONESIDE, and relaxing feat_in < feat_out to feat_in <= feat_out in bgmv_impl.cuh) support the narrow-rank one-sided matmuls needed. Reduces per-rank compute/redundant work at large TP/rank/seqlen.

Replaces the generic PyTorch `nn.GELU(approximate="tanh")` modules (used for both "gelu_new" and "gelu_fast" in the activation registry) with dedicated fused CUDA element-wise kernels (`gelu_new_kernel`/`gelu_fast_kernel`) launched one block per token, reading input via `__ldg` and writing output in a single kernel. This avoids PyTorch's per-op dispatch/launch overhead and provides a purpose-built activation kernel, reducing per-token activation latency in the MLP path.

Optimizes the vLLM CPU backend: adds an IPEX-based PagedAttention (vllm/attention/ops/ipex_attn.py) that routes reshape_and_cache and single_query_cached_kv_attention (decode) through Intel Extension for PyTorch's fused CPU kernels, selected by default in torch_sdpa.py when IPEX is importable on CPU. Also recommends TCMalloc via LD_PRELOAD 'for high performance memory allocation and better cache locality' and reshapes SDPA q/k/v to add a batch dim. These speed up an always-slow generic CPU path; no earlier commit regressed it.

Adds a specialized fast path for ROCm/AMD FP4 (mxfp4) GEMM: for small batch (M<=64) and a hardcoded allowlist of tuned (N,K) weight shapes (is_triton_gemm_afp4wfp4_presh_ws_tuned), the wrapper now routes to the Triton gemm_afp4wfp4_preshuffled_weight_scales kernel instead of the ASM gemm_a4w4 path, also skipping the (M+31)//32*32 output padding and, for M<32, skipping the activation-quant shuffle. This is a latent-inefficiency optimization for low-batch/decode FP4 matmuls, not a regression fix.

The dispatch function `cutlass_scaled_mm_supports_fp8` returned `false` unconditionally for Ada Lovelace (device capability 89), forcing FP8 GEMMs to fall back to torch.mm because "CUTLASS Kernels have not been tuned for Ada Lovelace systems and are slower than torch.mm." This commit re-enables the CUTLASS FP8 path (`return CUDA_VERSION >= 12040`) now that the kernels have been tuned for Lovelace, switching sm89 FP8 GEMMs onto the faster CUTLASS kernel.

On ROCm/AITER, RoPE is accelerated via a Triton kernel. This commit (1) promotes the AITER triton rope to a proper `direct_register_custom_op` (`rocm_aiter_triton_rotary_embedding`, mutates query/key in-place), so it can be pattern-matched and fused inside the compilation passes; (2) teaches the rope+kvcache fusion pass (MatcherRotaryEmbedding.match_rocm_aiter, ScatterSplitReplacementPass target_ops) and the +rotary_embedding custom-op enablement to work *with* AITER triton rope instead of being mutually exclusive — previously enable_qk_norm_rope_fusion/fuse_rope_kvcache forced `fuse_rope_kvcache=False` and logged "Cannot use VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_TRITON_ROPE with fuse_rope_kvcache"; (3) flips `VLLM_ROCM_USE_AITER_TRITON_ROPE` default from False to True. Net effect: the faster fused RoPE+KVCache path becomes usable and on-by-default on ROCm, reducing per-layer RoPE/kv-cache kernel launches/latency.

Adds an opt-in KV-cache "watermark": a fraction of total blocks kept free when admitting waiting/preempted requests. In kv_cache_manager.py, allocate_slots now computes watermark_blocks = int(watermark * num_blocks) and requires `num_blocks_to_allocate + watermark_blocks` free (applied only to WAITING/PREEMPTED requests when other reqs are already scheduled). This headroom prevents greedy over-admission on decode-heavy/high-concurrency workloads that later run out of KV and trigger preemption→recompute→re-prefill thrashing, which wastes throughput and inflates latency.

generate_block_hash_extra_keys() previously appended (mm_hash, mm_start) where mm_start = max(0, start_token_idx - offset) as the block-hash extra key for multimodal blocks. The commit changes this to append just mm_hashes[curr_mm_idx], dropping the intra-block offset. Since KV blocks are block_size-aligned and token IDs already participate in the hash, the offset was a redundant discriminator that could split hashes for identical mm content, reducing prefix-cache hit potential. Removing it simplifies and slightly broadens cache matching.

Adds three tuned Triton fused-MoE kernel config tables (E=512,N=128/256/512, device_name=NVIDIA_H200) mapping per-batch-size tokens to BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K, GROUP_SIZE_M, num_warps, num_stages. These override vLLM's generic fallback kernel heuristics for the Qwen3-Next MoE layer, giving better GPU occupancy/tiling for the fused expert GEMM on H200. Latent inefficiency: without a matching file the code silently uses slower default configs; nothing regressed.

In the VllmBackend AOT-compile path, fake args were previously regenerated by calling `fake_mode.from_tensor(t)` for each example input, which re-creates fake tensors and re-invokes torch's symbolic-shape machinery (`make_symbol`). The change instead extracts pre-existing fake values from each placeholder node's `meta["example_value"]` (`all_fake_values.append(i.meta["example_value"])`) and passes those to `PiecewiseCompileInterpreter(...).run(*fake_args)`, so a cache hit reuses existing symbolic shapes rather than re-deriving them, avoiding redundant symbol creation during (re)compilation/cache load. This reduces compile/startup-time work on the new `VLLM_USE_AOT_COMPILE` path.

Adds a new inductor compilation pass (SequenceParallelismPass) that pattern-matches `tensor_model_parallel_all_reduce(x) -> RMSNorm` and rewrites it to `reduce_scatter(x, dim=0) -> RMSNorm(shard) -> all_gather`. This distributes the RMSNorm/residual computation across the sequence dimension over TP ranks, so each rank normalizes only its shard, reducing redundant per-rank compute and memory, while the reduce_scatter+all_gather pair moves the same total data as the original all_reduce. New reduce_scatter/all_gather custom ops and communicator methods support it; cudagraph capture sizes are filtered to multiples of tp_size.

The OpenAI API server's frontend-multiprocessing backend previously ran the LLMEngine in a separate process wrapped in an asyncio event loop (AsyncLLMEngine + AsyncEngineRPCServer, both deleted here), incurring per-step/per-request asyncio coroutine scheduling overhead. This commit replaces it with MQLLMEngine, a synchronous ZMQ busy-loop (run_engine_loop) that calls self.engine.step() directly with no asyncio in the engine process, and optionally overlaps socket send/recv with GPU compute via process_request_outputs_callback when use_async_sockets is set. Removing the asyncio overhead ('OH') from the hot serving loop improves online-serving throughput/latency.

Adds a new SM100 (Blackwell) CUTLASS MLA decode kernel (ported from SGLang PR #6929) plumbed through csrc + vllm/_custom_ops.py + cutlass_mla.py. The new `_sm100_cutlass_mla_decode` path pads Q to MAX_HEADS=128 (`if H < MAX_HEADS: q_nope_padded = q_nope.new_empty((B_q, MAX_HEADS, D_q_nope))`) and slices back with `out[:, :H]`, so it works for any num_heads < 128. The previous CUTLASS MLA kernel (retained as `_old_forward_decode`/`ops.cutlass_mla_decode`) effectively required 128 heads; under DeepSeek tensor parallelism num_heads-per-rank is < 128 (TP=2→64, etc.), which previously blocked using the fast Blackwell CUTLASS decode kernel and forced a slower fallback backend. This enables the fast kernel for that config.

Enables Expert Parallel Load Balancing (EPLB) in the Transformers backend by removing the `raise NotImplementedError("Transformers backend does not support expert parallel load balancing yet.")` guard and wiring the model into the `MixtureOfExperts` mixin (set_eplb_state / update_physical_experts_metadata, num_physical/local/redundant_experts, per-layer expert_weights). EPLB redistributes physical expert replicas across EP ranks to even out per-expert load, reducing GPU-util imbalance and bubbles in expert-parallel MoE serving. This is feature enablement for a new backend rather than a fix to an existing regression.

Adds a new Marlin tensor-core GEMM kernel (csrc/quantization/gptq_marlin/gptq_marlin.cu + gptq_marlin_repack.cu) and a GPTQMarlinLinearMethod that repacks AutoGPTQ int4 weights into Marlin tile layout at load time (ops.gptq_marlin_repack) then runs the fast fused dequant+mma kernel (ops.gptq_marlin_gemm), including support for act_order/desc_act models. config.py._verify_quantization now auto-selects quant_method='gptq_marlin' for any GPTQ checkpoint that is_marlin_compatible, so standard AutoGPTQ models transparently take the faster kernel path instead of the slower generic GPTQ GEMM.

Implements a chunked-prefill scheduler (part 4/n of the chunked prefill series). The Scheduler is rewritten with SchedulingBudget/token_budget and _schedule_running/_schedule_swapped/_schedule_prefill paths that split a long prefill into token chunks (ScheduledSequenceGroup.token_chunk_size) so prefill chunks can be batched together with decodes in one step. This raises GPU utilization / batched-token efficiency and reduces the inter-token-latency bubble that arises when a large prefill monopolizes a step. Gated on scheduler_config.chunked_prefill_enabled (opt-in; prompt_limit branches on it), so it changes behavior only when enable_chunked_prefill=True — a new capability, not a fix restoring previously-working performance.

Adds a new hand-written CuteDSL/tcgen05-based GatedDeltaNet (GDN) chunk prefill kernel for NVIDIA Blackwell (SM100), plus supporting cute_utils (tcgen05 MMA, TMA copy, bf16 conversion helpers). It is wired in as a third GDN prefill backend ('cutedsl') alongside the existing 'triton'/'flashinfer' paths via _resolve_gdn_prefill_backend and ChunkGatedDeltaRule.forward_cutedsl. On SM10x with head_k_dim==128 it provides a native SM100 tensor-core prefill path instead of the generic Triton/FLA kernel, targeting higher prefill throughput on Blackwell. The kernel also computes chunk metadata on-GPU (prepare_metadata_cutedsl) to avoid a GPU->CPU sync present in the Triton path (prepare_chunk_indices via .tolist()).

Hybrid SSM/attention models on V1 previously required VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=FLASHINFER because _verify_hybrid_attention_mamba_layout raised a ValueError unless the attention KV-cache shape was (num_blocks, 2, ...). This commit replaces that hard restriction with _update_hybrid_attention_mamba_layout, which uses kv_cache.as_strided_() to re-express a (2, num_blocks, ...) FlashAttention-layout tensor as (num_blocks, 2, ...) in place. This unblocks the FlashAttention backend (and others) for hybrid models rather than pinning them to FlashInfer, letting users select a backend better suited to their workload/hardware. It removes a functional restriction present since V1 hybrid support was first added, so it enables a faster path rather than restoring a regressed one.

Adds a persistent InductorHashCache that serializes (runtime_shape, graph_index -> inductor graph hash) to disk and sets TORCHINDUCTOR_CACHE_DIR/TRITON_CACHE_DIR. On a cache hit, wrap_inductor bypasses the expensive compile_fx() call and instead directly retrieves the already-compiled artifact via FxGraphCache._lookup_graph(hash_str, ...), using AlwaysHitShapeEnv and patched _check_can_cache/_get_shape_env/compiled_fx_graph_hash to force Inductor caching outside Dynamo's tracing context. Config compute_hash() methods provide the factors that key the cache safely. Net effect: skips redundant Inductor recompilation across runs/shapes, reducing torch.compile startup/compilation time.

Four pointwise/fusion helper functions (_multinomial, get_masked_input_and_mask, quick_gelu, gegelu) are switched from @torch.jit.script to @torch.compile(dynamic=True). These functions rely on op fusion into a single kernel (per the in-code comment 'will fuse all of the pointwise ops below into a single kernel, making it very fast'); torch.compile's inductor backend replaces the legacy TorchScript fuser as the mechanism for generating those fused kernels, part of a project-wide N-step migration off torch.jit.script.

On TPU/XLA, the V1 sampler path recompiled graphs at runtime because sampling tensors had dynamic shapes not covered by warmup. The fix (a) precompiles the actual `max_num_reqs` upper-limit shape by replacing the `num_reqs_to_sample *= 2` doubling with `_get_padded_num_reqs_with_upper_limit(...)` so the runtime shape is warmed up, (b) removes commented-out penalty `copy_slice` calls so the CPU-TPU sync graph is consistent ('We can't have flags to skip copies or we'll end up recompiling'), and (c) drops a stale `@torch.compile(backend='openxla', ...)` decorator on `sample_from_hidden`. Each XLA recompilation stalls inference with a fresh compile.

Adds an opt-in data-parallel (DP) execution path for the Qwen2.5-VL vision encoder. With `use_data_parallel=True`, TP linear layers (QKVParallelLinear/RowParallelLinear/ColumnParallelLinear/MergedColumnParallelLinear) are replaced by replicated variants (ReplicatedLinear/MergedReplicatedLinear), setting `self.tp_size = 1` so the per-attention-block `all_gather_interleave` collective in `split_qkv` (guarded by `if self.tp_size > 1`) is skipped. Instead, whole images are load-balanced across ranks by patch count via `get_load_balance_assignment` (greedy by total size) in the new `run_dp_sharded_mrope_vision_model`, so each rank runs full images independently, avoiding per-layer TP communication overhead on the small ViT.

Adds a new MXFP8 MoE backend (AITER_MXFP8) that routes gfx950 MXFP8 (1x32 E8M0) MoE through AITER's FlyDSL two-stage grouped GEMM instead of the native Triton dot_scaled path. The backend is inserted into _SUPPORTED_BACKENDS so it is auto-selected (higher priority than the Triton fallback) whenever the device is gfx950 and the flydsl package + aiter #3811 tuned config are present; weights/E8M0 scales are preshuffled at load time into FlyDSL's gate/up-interleaved layout, and the call goes through the graph-safe rocm_aiter_fused_moe custom op so it is captured under HIP graphs/torch.compile. A faster kernel replaces an always-slower generic path — no regression is being repaired.

Adds a "naive block assignment" fast path to the Triton fused-MoE kernel that bypasses the separate moe_align_block_size sort/pad kernel launch. For small, sparse batches (expert_map is None and tokens_in_chunk*top_k*SPARSITY_FACTOR <= global_num_experts) it sets sorted_token_ids=None and assigns one token per block directly inside the kernel via `offs_token = tl.where(offs==0, pid_m, num_valid_tokens)`, avoiding the token-sorting/padding overhead that dominates when M < BLOCK_SIZE_M and expert count is large (e.g. 128/256 experts, topk 1-2, m=1-2).

During model loading, weight tensors of varying sizes are allocated, and PyTorch's CUDA caching allocator (default max_split_size_mb = SIZE_MAX, i.e. no limit) splits large free blocks to satisfy small allocations, causing heap fragmentation that can OOM even when total free memory is sufficient. The fix wraps `model_runner.load_model` in a `_scoped_allocator_max_split(max_split_size_mb=20)` context manager that calls `torch._C._accelerator_setAllocatorSettings("max_split_size_mb:20")` (20 MiB is PyTorch's minimum) so large blocks are not split during load, then restores the original value on exit. Cost is more cudaMalloc calls, stated as 'negligible in practice'.

Adds hardware-specific tuned Triton GEMM configs (BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K, GROUP_SIZE_M, num_warps, num_stages, indexed by batch size M) as JSON lookup files for FP8 block-wise quantization matmul and fused-MoE kernels on H100/H200, and adds a dedicated fp8_w8a8 branch in get_default_config replacing the generic 64/64/32/GROUP=8 fallback. This lets the kernel pick tile shapes and pipelining that better saturate the GPU for the block=[128,128] fp8 path, improving throughput/GPU utilization. No prior commit degraded these kernels; they were always using a suboptimal generic default, so this is a latent-inefficiency optimization, not a regression fix.

On the CPU backend, OMP thread affinity previously defaulted to "all" (no binding), letting a rank's OpenMP threads spread across all logical CPUs including remote NUMA nodes, incurring slow cross-NUMA memory access. This commit adds get_cpus_id_binding_based_on_numa_nodes() which, via py-libnuma + psutil, computes the CPU ids of the NUMA node for each rank and binds that rank's OMP threads to node-local cores (init_cpu_threads_env), and flips the VLLM_CPU_OMP_THREADS_BIND default from "all" to "auto" so this happens automatically. Keeping compute and memory NUMA-local improves CPU inference throughput.

Adds a native CUDA kernel for INT8 per-token-group quantization by generalizing the existing FP8 8-bit kernel: per_token_group_quant.cu now also dispatches on at::ScalarType::Char to instantiate the kernel with int8_t, and a new per_token_group_quant_int8 binding is exposed. The Python per_token_group_quant_int8 now prefers this CUDA kernel (torch.ops._C.per_token_group_quant_int8) over the pre-existing Triton kernel when running on CUDA, replacing a per-row Triton launch with a fused native kernel for activation quantization in W8A8 block-INT8 linear layers.

Enables multiprocessing-based tensor parallelism on the CPU backend and adds CPU-perf-critical machinery: a new `init_cpu_threads_env` (csrc/cpu/utils.cpp) that pins OpenMP threads to specific CPU cores via sched_setaffinity and binds/migrates memory pages to the local NUMA node (numa_migrate_pages + numa_set_membind + numa_set_strict), avoiding cross-NUMA remote memory access and CPU oversubscription; controlled by a new env var VLLM_CPU_OMP_THREADS_BIND. It also routes CPU all_reduce through IPEX (ipex.distributed.all_reduce) instead of stock torch.distributed. This is new capability + thread/NUMA affinity tuning to improve CPU inference throughput, not a fix to a prior regression.

Adds a shared-memory (SHM) based send_tensor_dict/recv_tensor_dict fast path for CPU pipeline-parallel communication. In parallel_state.py, when use_cpu_custom_send_recv is set (CPU + init_shm_manager available), send/recv_tensor_dict short-circuit into the SHM device_communicator instead of the generic pickle-based recv_object/send_object metadata path. The C++ ThreadSHMContext is extended to double-buffer stamps (2 indices for local/remote) via reset_threads_stamp_buffer_idx so bidirectional PP send/recv don't contend on a single stamp buffer, and the per-thread SHM buffer is doubled from 2MB to 4MB. cpu_communicator now activates SHM for 'pp' groups (previously only 'tp'). This replaces the slow serialization-based inter-stage transfer for CPU PP with a fast SHM path.

Adds tensor-parallel support to the ViT vision encoders (CLIP/Blip/InternViT/SigLIP) used by VLMs. Replaces plain nn.Linear / HF eager attention (BlipAttention, CLIPAttention, InternAttention's nn.Linear qkv) with vLLM's QKVParallelLinear + RowParallelLinear, sharding attention heads across ranks via num_heads_per_partition = divide(self.num_heads, self.tp_size), and routes attention through xformers memory_efficient_attention_forward. Previously the vision model was not sharded at all (blip2 comment 'We only do sharding for language model and not vision model for now' is removed), so this distributes ViT compute/weights across TP ranks (per-GPU memory reduction) and uses a more efficient attention kernel.

Adds Triton kernels (_dequantize_nvfp4_kernel, _nvfp4_quant_dequant_kernel with a binary-tree E2M1 decode and 2D tiled/coalesced access) that replace the pure-PyTorch reference-op NVFP4 emulation path. dequantize_to_dtype and ref_nvfp4_quant_dequant now dispatch to the Triton implementation when `not swizzle and current_platform.is_cuda_alike()`, avoiding the element-wise torch ops (break_fp4_bytes gather + broadcast multiplies) used in the emulated dequant/QDQ path on Hopper/AMD Instinct hardware.

The RMSNorm+quant compilation fusion pass fuses rms_norm and per-block fp8 quant into a single fused CUDA kernel. When quantization uses TMA-aligned (padded) scale tensors — as DeepGEMM requires on Hopper — the fused kernel indexed scales using `gridDim.x` as the scale-row stride instead of the TMA-aligned padded row count, so the pattern could not be matched/fused. The commit threads an `outer_scale_stride`/`tma_alignment` through the kernel (`scale_rows = ceil(gridDim.x/stride)*stride`) and the fusion matcher (`is_tma_aligned`), enabling the fused kernel on the DeepGEMM+TMA-aligned path where previously the unfused (slower) rms_norm + separate quant path ran.

In LMCacheMPRequestMetadata.GetStoreMetadata, the upper bound computed_blocks was set to `num_scheduled_tokens // block_size + num_lmcache_hit_blocks`. Because LMCache stores in chunk units, num_lmcache_hit_blocks is rounded DOWN to a chunk boundary, so when vLLM APC (prefix cache) hits more blocks than that rounded value, the upper bound is too low and the APC-hit blocks between the two values are silently skipped and never stored to LMCache (under-storing). Those un-stored KV blocks cause future prefix-matching requests to miss the external cache and recompute prefill, degrading throughput. The fix uses max(num_vllm_hit_blocks, num_lmcache_hit_blocks) so the tighter/larger hit count is used.

In a disaggregated prefill/decode (P/D) deployment using the NIXL KV connector, a request with do_remote_prefill=True causes the prefill (P) node to allocate/pin KV blocks. If the request is rejected at the D-side OpenAI serving layer *before* engine admission (validation error / ErrorResponse / exception), the connector's request_finished hook never runs, so the P node's KV blocks are never freed — they are stranded/leaked. The fix adds notify_kv_transfer_request_rejected, which submits a pre-aborted EngineCoreRequest (abort_immediately=True) so the scheduler adds then immediately aborts it, triggering the connector's request_finished path which enqueues an empty recv (local_block_ids == []) telling the P node to free the pinned blocks.

Under data parallelism (DP>1) with MTP/EAGLE-style speculative decoding, DP ranks can independently evaluate `input_fits_in_drafter` and reach different results. When a rank's input does not fit, the old code skipped the drafter's GPU model forward entirely — but that forward carries TP/EP/DP collectives. Ranks that skipped never entered the collective while ranks that ran the drafter blocked in it, causing a distributed deadlock/hang. The fix adds `self.drafter.dummy_run(num_tokens=1)` on the skipping DP ranks (guarded by `data_parallel_size > 1`) so every rank participates in the collective.

vLLM monkey-patches `_get_shape_env` to return `AlwaysHitShapeEnv` so the Inductor Fx graph cache lookup always hits, letting it reuse a compiled graph across many runtime shapes instead of recompiling (per the class docstring: "make the Inductor code cache lookup always hit, and we can compile the graph for different shapes as needed"). torch 2.8+/main moved the shape-env fetch into `AOTAutogradCache._get_shape_env`, which vLLM was not patching; the commit adds `patch(... AOTAutogradCache._get_shape_env ...)` in both `compile()` and `load()` (guarded by `hasattr(AOTAutogradCache, "_get_shape_env")`). Without it, the autograd/fx cache lookup would miss on newer torch, forcing recompilation and losing the always-hit optimization.

On TPU, routing FusedMoE.forward through the torch.ops.vllm.moe_forward custom op wrapper caused an out-of-memory condition (likely because the opaque custom-op boundary defeats the XLA compiler's memory planning/buffer aliasing). The fix special-cases current_platform.is_tpu() to call forward_impl directly, bypassing the custom op so the TPU/XLA graph can compile within memory. The inline TODO ("Once the OOM issue for the TPU backend is resolved, we will switch to using the moe_forward custom op") confirms it is a temporary memory workaround.

Reverts #13721, which had added a `_dummy_sampler_run` warm-up call in gpu_worker.py invoked after `capture_model()` to "preallocate memory buffer for logits and other sampling related tensors of max possible shape to avoid memory fragmentation issue". This revert removes that pre-allocation/warm-up (and folds the sampler dummy run back inline into profile_run), re-exposing GPU memory fragmentation for logits & sampling tensors. It is a transient rollback in a revert/re-apply churn cycle: the same fix was re-applied the same day in #13776.

In V1's SyncMPClient, the output-processing thread blocked on `output_socket.recv_multipart(copy=False)` and shutdown relied on `self.ctx.destroy(linger=0)` raising `zmq.error.ContextTerminated` to unblock it. This was racy/unreliable and could hang on shutdown (or error) when using multiprocessing. The fix adds an explicit `inproc://` PAIR shutdown socket and a `zmq.Poller` registering both the output and shutdown sockets, so shutdown sends a signal (`shutdown_sender.send(b'')`) that cleanly breaks the recv loop and closes sockets in their own thread instead of tearing down the whole context.

PR #42585 (812e7e736) switched multi-API-server DP startup to deferred port allocation (defer_api_server_ports=not rust_frontend_path), leaving addresses as tcp://host:0 placeholders resolved post-bind via gather_actual_addresses(). On the Ray DP backend, launch_core_engines pickles the addresses snapshot into each Ray engine actor at .remote() time and EngineCoreActorMixin._perform_handshakes is a no-op, so actors keep the placeholder tcp://host:0 forever and DEALER-connect to port 0; ZMQ connect is async and does not raise, producing a deterministic startup hang. The fix carves out Ray DP (defer_api_server_ports=not (rust_frontend_path or is_ray_dp)) to pre-allocate real ports driver-side and skip the post-bind rebind, so actors are pickled with real endpoints.

The multimodal processor and the AsyncMicrobatchTokenizer shared the same underlying HuggingFace Rust fast-tokenizer instance. Under concurrent load, simultaneous access from call_hf_processor and the async tokenizer triggered a Rust RefCell "RuntimeError: Already borrowed", causing request failures/contention that degraded VLM serving throughput. The fix `mm_tokenizer = copy.deepcopy(tokenizer)` gives the mm processor its own tokenizer backend, eliminating the cross-path borrow conflict.

The tuned fused-MoE Triton kernel config JSON for Qwen3-Coder-480B-A35B FP8 (E=20,N=2560, H20-3e, block_shape=[128,128]) shipped in the introducing commit was produced incorrectly, giving suboptimal GEMM tiling (BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K, GROUP_SIZE_M, num_warps, num_stages) for many token-count buckets. This commit re-tunes those entries to faster values, and adds a guard in benchmark_moe.py that raises ValueError when tuning with --use-deep-gemm (which only tunes Triton kernels), indicating the original config was mis-tuned via that flag.

The full-cuda-graph static-buffer scheme for FlashMLA decode assumed the per-SM tile_scheduler_metadata tensor had a fixed size equal to the capture-time size (assert cg_buf_tile_scheduler_metadata.size() == tile_scheduler_metadata.size()) and zero-filled the trailing num_splits. But sm_parts (dim 0 of tile_scheduler_metadata) varies across decode steps and with DP, so on cuda-graph replay this produced an illegal memory access (IMA), breaking full cuda-graph capture for FlashMLA + DP. The fix pre-allocates an upper-bound buffer (num_sms, 8), copies into a correctly-sized view (cg_buf_tile_scheduler_metadata[:sm_parts]), and fills trailing num_splits with num_splits[-1] to satisfy the monotonic-increasing requirement. Full cuda graphs cut per-launch overhead / GPU bubbles, so the fix re-enables that perf path for FlashMLA under DP.

The CUDA-graph memory-profiling path introduced in PR #30515 (commit ebb9cc5f2) reads the CudagraphDispatcher's `cudagraph_keys` via `get_capture_descs()` and captures graphs against a *minimal* KV cache built only for profiling, then calls `CUDAGraphWrapper.clear_all_graphs()` and restores the real graph pools. However it never cleared the dispatcher's `cudagraph_keys` or reset `keys_initialized`, leaving stale batch-descriptor keys populated during profiling. This fix adds `for key_set in self.cudagraph_dispatcher.cudagraph_keys.values(): key_set.clear()` and `self.cudagraph_dispatcher.keys_initialized = False` so the dispatcher is re-initialized cleanly for the real capture/runtime. Stale keys marked as `keys_initialized=True` would make runtime `dispatch()` match descriptors whose underlying graphs were cleared / captured against the wrong (minimal) KV cache, corrupting the CUDA-graph capture/replay path (a perf path) and the memory-estimate accounting.

For text-only V1 models the runner now passes token `input_ids` (with `inputs_embeds=None`) to the model instead of always computing embeddings on-host and copying them into a persistent buffer. The prior always-embeddings path excluded the embedding lookup from the captured CUDA graph and forced an extra per-step device copy `self.inputs_embeds[:num_scheduled_tokens].copy_(inputs_embeds)`. Feeding input_ids lets the embedding layer be captured inside the CUDA graph and removes the redundant copy.

ParallelSampleSequenceGroup.add_request created per-sample params via copy.deepcopy(original_params), which for guided decoding deep-copies each SamplingParams' logits_processors — recompiling/reallocating heavy grammar state (compiled grammar ctx, tokenizer_info, bitmasks) once per parallel sample (n copies). The fix replaces `params = copy.deepcopy(original_params)` with `params = params.clone()`, and adds a `clone()` method to the outlines/guidance/xgrammar logits processors that shares the immutable compiled grammar (`new_processor.ctx = self.ctx`, reuses tokenizer_info) while only copying the small mutable FSM/matcher state; xgrammar `__post_init__` also now skips re-deriving tokenizer_info when already provided.

async_copy_to_gpu() called x.pin_memory() on every invocation, which allocates a fresh pinned host buffer via the CUDA driver's pinned allocator (cudaHostAlloc) and does an extra CPU-to-CPU copy before the CPU->GPU transfer. Under high concurrency, repeated per-call pinned allocation contends on the CUDA driver lock, causing sporadic stalls. The fix removes pin_memory() and copies directly from the pageable tensor with out.copy_(x, non_blocking=True), eliminating the per-call allocation/contention.

Re-enables CUDA graph memory profiling/reservation on ROCm by changing the guard from `current_platform.is_cuda()` to `current_platform.is_cuda_alike()` in `determine_available_memory` (gpu_worker.py). Previously (since #36720 / e8f9dbc36) ROCm forced `cudagraph_memory_estimate = 0`, so the startup memory profiler did not reserve space for CUDA graphs; the KV-cache sizing then over-committed VRAM and the engine OOM'd at startup on ROCm (the "startup memory guard"). The commit also adds a test-side `wait_for_rocm_memory_to_settle()` since ROCm frees VRAM lazily, so a freshly-started engine would OOM against stale accounting.

On ROCm, DeepSeek V3.2 sparse MLA uses two attention groups: the sparse MLA attention (ROCMAiterMLASparseMetadata) and the DeepseekV32Indexer (DeepseekV32IndexerMetadata). Commit a9e532afe (#36681) enabled MTP>1 for sparse MLA by adding ROCMAiterMLASparseMetadata to self.allowed_attn_types, but it did NOT add DeepseekV32IndexerMetadata. The validation loop iterated over per_layer_attn_metadata.values() and raised ValueError for any type not in allowed_attn_types when num_speculative_tokens > 1, so the indexer group's metadata caused the check to fail and blocked the accelerated Aiter sparse MLA path under MTP>1. This fix adds DeepseekV32IndexerMetadata to allowed_attn_types and refactors build_per_layer_attn_metadata into build_per_group_and_layer_attn_metadata so the type check runs once per attention group instead of once per layer.

In Fp8MoEMethod weight post-processing, the weight_scale_inv tensors are passed through get_col_major_tma_aligned_tensor() to produce the column-major TMA-aligned layout DeepGEMM expects. A trailing .contiguous() call forced that result back into a standard (row-major) contiguous layout, undoing the alignment and pushing DeepGEMM into a slower path / extra runtime layout conversion. Removing .contiguous() preserves the col-major TMA alignment, recovering 5.5% throughput.

The KV-sharing "fast prefill" optimization (skips redundant work on shared-KV layers) was gated by an assert in create_fast_prefill_custom_backend requiring common_attn_metadata.logits_indices_padded/num_logits_indices to be non-None. During cudagraph capture / dummy runs logits_indices is None, so num_logits_indices was 0 and logits_indices_padded None, tripping the assert and making the fast-prefill path unusable whenever cudagraph was enabled (only enforce_eager worked). The fix removes the assert, makes the metadata fields Optional(None), and sets num_logits_indices' initial value to None instead of 0, letting fast prefill run under cudagraph.

Commit cc4325b66 (#8558) rewrote the custom all-reduce multi-GPU barrier to use `st.release.sys.global.u32` / `ld.acquire.sys.global.u32` PTX, which are only valid on sm_70+ (Volta+). On Pascal (sm_60/sm_61, e.g. P40/P100) these instructions don't compile/work, disabling vLLM's fast custom peer-to-peer all-reduce and forcing the slower NCCL fallback for the small-tensor regime it targets. This commit adds `#if __CUDA_ARCH__ >= 700` guards with a fallback using `membar.sys; st.volatile.global.u32` and `ld.volatile.global.u32; membar.gl`, restoring the custom all-reduce path on Pascal.

In the torch.compile profiling/warmup pass, dummy kv_caches were built as `[torch.tensor([], ...)] * num_layers`, which makes every list element the same tensor object. Dynamo then treats the per-layer kv_caches as aliasing each other, which mis-specializes/incorrectly traces the compiled graph relative to the real runtime where each layer's kv_cache is a distinct tensor. The fix uses a list comprehension `[torch.tensor([...]) for _ in range(num_layers)]` so each layer gets a distinct tensor, as the added comment states: 'important to create tensors inside the loop, rather than multiplying the list, to avoid Dynamo from treating them as tensor aliasing.'

In swap_in/swap_out, when a physical block is shared across multiple sequences in a group (ref_count>1, e.g. beam search / parallel sampling with shared prompt), the buggy code did `if cpu_block in mapping: new_block_table.append(mapping[cpu_block]); continue`, which skipped the subsequent `self.cpu_allocator.free(cpu_block)` (symmetrically `gpu_allocator.free` in swap_out). Thus the block's ref_count was decremented only once instead of once per referencing sequence, never reaching 0, so it was never returned to free_blocks — a KV-cache block leak that progressively exhausts the CPU/GPU block pool. The fix moves `new_block_table.append(...)` and the `free(...)` call outside the branch so free is invoked for every sequence's every block.

`_set_compile_ranges()` was invoked before `current_platform.check_and_update_config(self)`, so the torch.compile compile_ranges were computed from a stale `max_num_batched_tokens`. On XPU with MLA enabled, `check_and_update_config` lowers `max_num_batched_tokens`, leaving the compile ranges (which are bounded by that value, per get_compile_ranges() in #24252) misaligned with the real runtime shape space — causing incorrect/extra compilation partitioning. The fix moves the call to after the platform config update so ranges reflect the final max_num_batched_tokens.

Commit bfdb512f1 (#34127) added attention-mask logic to the shared Idefics2VisionTransformer.forward: when patch_attention_mask has padding it builds a 4D additive mask, which in Idefics2VisionAttention.forward diverts execution from the fused MMEncoderAttention (Flash Attention) path to plain F.scaled_dot_product_attention. This regressed all models sharing this encoder (e.g. Aria, Phi4) that don't need masking. This fix adds an apply_encoder_attention_mask flag (default False) so only MiniCPM variants build the mask; other models keep attention_mask=None and stay on the fast fused kernel.

In PiecewiseBackend, `to_be_compiled_sizes` was initialized as `compile_sizes.union(capture_sizes)`, but the compilation loop only removes entries when a size is actually *compiled* (compile_sizes), not merely cudagraph-captured. When any capture-only size existed, the set `to_be_compiled_sizes` never became empty, so the guard `if self.is_last_graph and not self.to_be_compiled_sizes:` never fired and `inductor_hash_cache.save_to_file()` was never called. The Inductor graph-hash cache therefore was never persisted to disk, forcing full torch.compile/Inductor recompilation on every subsequent startup instead of a fast cache lookup. The fix changes it to `compile_sizes.copy()` so the set drains and the cache is saved (via the new `check_for_ending_compilation` helper).

In rocm_aiter_mla.py, `AiterMLAHelper._AITER_UNSUPPORTED_HEADS` is reset from `[32]` back to `()`. The `[32]` entry was added in bc635fad2 ("dsv3.2 further optimization") together with an `assert num_heads not in _AITER_UNSUPPORTED_HEADS` in `get_mla_padded_q`. That assert fired for DeepSeek at TP4 (128 attention heads / 4 = 32 heads), which blocked the fast AITER MLA decode kernel for the TP4 configuration (either raising or forcing a slower fallback MLA backend). Removing the restriction re-enables the AITER MLA attention path for DeepSeek V3.2 TP4. The deepseek_v2.py hunk additionally routes fp32 router logits into FusedMoE (`router_logits_dtype=self.gate.out_dtype` + `set_out_dtype(torch.float32)`) so the AITER biased_grouped_topk routing kernel is used with matching bias dtype rather than being bypassed.

In the SM100 CUTLASS MLA warp-specialized kernel, the final epilogue NamedBarrier used only `.arrive()` instead of `.arrive_and_wait()`. Under load with the persistent tile scheduler (multiple tiles per CTA), a warp that signals arrival but does not wait can advance to the next tile and overwrite shared Q/pipeline state before peer warps finish, desynchronizing the named barrier's arrival count and deadlocking the kernel — observed as a hang under DP/EP load. Changing to `.arrive_and_wait()` forces all participating warps to synchronize before proceeding. (The remaining diff hunks are pure tab→space whitespace normalization.)

The MLA attention impl bypasses torch's custom-op/nn.Module dispatch overhead by pulling out the rotary embedding's forward method and calling it directly (`self.rotary_emb = rotary_emb._forward_method`). An earlier perf commit (#14384) did this by hardcoding `rotary_emb.forward_cuda`, which produced wrong results for DeepSeek because `DeepseekScalingRotaryEmbedding` (YaRN) needs its own dispatched forward, not the generic CUDA op; that commit was reverted (#14471), removing the optimization. This commit restores the optimization safely by using `_forward_method` — the value already selected by `CustomOp.dispatch_forward()` per op subclass — regaining the CPU-overhead reduction without breaking accuracy (accuracy silently switching the rope path).

The RMSNorm+FP8-group-quant fusion pass was silently failing to fire on ROCm gfx950 (non-fnuz FP8). In matcher_utils.py the quant matcher used `torch.ops.vllm.triton_per_token_group_quant_fp8` for non-fnuz but the aiter group quant op (`get_group_quant_op`) for fnuz; on non-fnuz this made the pattern's QUANT_OP diverge from the op actually in the graph, so the pattern never matched and rms_norm + quant stayed unfused (extra 16-bit materialization + separate kernels). The fix makes the matcher always use the aiter group quant op, and adds DoubleAiterRMSFp8GroupQuant(View)Pattern to fuse the 1-rms->2-quant fan-out (incl. a view-tolerant variant for DSv3.2 MLA indexer q_c norm) into `rocm_aiter_rmsnorm_fp8_group_quant`.

The removed block in `_load_adapter` iterated over every module in `lora.loras` and, per module, called `is_supported_lora_module(module_name, expected_lora_modules_lst)` (and `is_in_target_modules`). `is_supported_lora_module` builds and runs an `re.match(r".*\.{target_module}$")` against each entry of the supported-modules list, i.e. an O(n_lora_modules × n_supported_modules) regex scan run synchronously during adapter loading. For a large model like Nemotron Super with many modules, this loop takes long enough to exceed the LoRA-load timeout. Removing the warning loop restores prior load latency.

Commit 36e74c9ea added `get_configured_worker_rnic`/`_get_explicit_worker_rnic`, which on a multi-NIC host used the physical GPU index to pick a single RNIC from the CSV device list (`device_name = entries[gpu_index]`) and overwrote `store_config.device_name` in the worker. This pinned each Mooncake RDMA worker to one NIC, capping KV-transfer bandwidth at a single HCA's capacity. This commit deletes that selection logic and stops overwriting `device_name`, passing the full config through to Mooncake's TransferEngine so it can use all HCAs on multi-NIC hosts, restoring aggregate RDMA bandwidth for KV-cache transfer.

The fused MoE kernel selects a tuned block config (BLOCK_SIZE_M/N/K, GROUP_SIZE_M) based on the token count M via try_get_optimal_moe_config. When inputs exceed VLLM_FUSED_MOE_CHUNK_SIZE (65536), execution is split into chunks; the last chunk has fewer tokens (tokens_in_chunk < CHUNK_SIZE) but was reusing the config picked for the full chunk size, giving a mismatched/suboptimal tiling for that smaller trailing chunk. The fix adds `config = get_config_func(tokens_in_chunk)` inside the `if tokens_in_chunk < CHUNK_SIZE` branch so the last chunk gets a config appropriate to its actual size, improving kernel efficiency.

On TPU, MoE had been switched to a fallback Python loop implementation (moe_torch_iterative) that runs a full dense F.linear over ALL tokens for EVERY expert (O(num_experts) dense matmuls), instead of a single grouped/sparse matmul. This commit re-enables the Pallas MoE kernel by importing `fused_moe` from `.moe_pallas` (which uses `torch.ops.xla.gmm` grouped matmul), inlining the `_histogram` helper whose torch_xla import had broken and bumping torch_xla to dev20250518. This restores the fused sparse MoE path, avoiding the O(experts) dense compute of the iterative fallback.

PyTorch 2.9.0 disabled CUDNN's Conv3D, causing a "significant performance regression" for Conv3d layers (per the in-source comment and issue #27406 / pytorch#166122). vLLM works around this by routing eligible Conv3d ops (enable_linear: kernel==stride, no padding, groups==1) through a matmul-based _forward_mulmat path instead of F.conv3d. This commit, part of the 2.9.1 release bump, extends the version guard so the workaround also fires on torch 2.9.1 (which still has CUDNN Conv3D disabled): `is_torch_equal("2.9.0") or is_torch_equal("2.9.1")`.

PR #33110 added a call to `mm_registry.get_max_tokens_per_item_by_modality(self.model_config)` in `InputProcessor.__init__` (early-reject MM budget feature).
