That call runs dummy multimodal profiling with torch ops, which without a thread cap lets PyTorch spawn threads across all cores (thread oversubscription), and it was reverted as "[Critical] Revert #33110". This redo re-adds the identical feature but wraps the call in `set_default_torch_num_threads()`, which now defaults to `OMP_NUM_THREADS` or 1, capping thread usage during init — matching the existing thread cap already applied to the per-request `preprocess()` path.

The `uniform_decode` classification decides whether a batch takes the specialized uniform-decode CUDA graph / attention path. Commit 56539cdda ("Refactor padding logic and pad for CUDA graphs before attention metadata building") changed the check to compare the SP-padded token count: `num_tokens_padded == max_num_scheduled_tokens * num_reqs`. When sequence-parallelism padding is active, `num_tokens_padded` no longer equals the exact `max_num_scheduled_tokens * num_reqs` product, so a genuine uniform-decode batch is misclassified as non-uniform and misses the specialized/CUDA-graph decode path, degrading decode efficiency. The fix reverts the comparison to use the raw, unpadded `num_tokens` (extracted into `_is_uniform_decode`) and moves `num_tokens_padded = self._pad_for_sequence_parallelism(num_tokens)` to after the uniform-decode determination.

Eliminates numerous implicit GPU↔CPU synchronization points on the model-execution / multimodal input path. Blocking host-to-device tensor creation (`torch.tensor(tensor_ptrs, device=device)`) in LoRA triton ops and punica `convert_mapping` is replaced with pinned, non-blocking `async_tensor_h2d(...)`. Device-to-host syncs from `.item()`/`.tolist()` are removed or deferred: `torch.max(audio_embed_sizes).item()`→`int(torch.max(...))` building the arange mask on CPU (granite_speech), small metadata fields marked `MultiModalFieldConfig.batched(..., keep_on_cpu=True)` so downstream indexing/`.item()` doesn't force a D2H stall, and mrope/pos_id/window_index tensors moved with explicit `.to(device, non_blocking=True)`. bert `_decode_token_type_ids` replaces a device `torch.ones_like(...) << shift` mask with plain Python-scalar bitwise ops. thinking_budget_state builds active-index/forced-token lists entirely on CPU (comment: 'avoid per-iteration scalar sync writes to GPU tensors').

The nvfp4 quant kernel (scaled_fp4_quant_sm100a) was being launched on a stream taken from PyTorch's stream pool (at::cuda::getStreamFromPool(false, ...)) rather than the current CUDA stream. Because the pool stream has no ordering relationship with the current/default stream that surrounding ops run on, the kernel does not correctly overlap/order with adjacent compute and requires implicit synchronization, creating pipeline bubbles (and a correctness hazard). The fix uses at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream(input.get_device()) so the kernel enqueues on the active stream, restoring proper stream ordering and overlap.

In the attn+quant fusion replacement graph, the attention output buffer was allocated with torch.ops.aten.full.default([...], 0.0, ...), which materializes a redundant zero-fill (memset) device kernel on every forward pass. The buffer is passed as output= to unified_attention_with_output and fully overwritten by the attention kernel, so the initialization is wasted work. Switching to torch.empty allocates uninitialized memory and drops the per-step fill kernel, reducing per-layer attention latency/overhead.

In `_validate_and_reshape_mm_tensor`, when a single multimodal input tensor has ndim==3 (shape [N, S, H]), the old code did `torch.concat(list(mm_input))`: `list(mm_input)` iterates dim-0 producing N Python tensor slices, and `torch.concat` then allocates a fresh contiguous tensor by copying them all back together. The new code `mm_input.reshape(-1, mm_input.shape[-1])` collapses the leading dims as a (typically zero-copy) view, eliminating the Python-level iteration and the redundant concat allocation/copy. This runs on the input-validation/preprocessing path per multimodal request.

The old apply_logits_bias did a Python double-loop over sampling_metadata.logit_bias and, per (request, token) entry, executed `logits[i, token_id] += bias` — a separate scalar GPU indexing/scatter op with an implicit host-device interaction for each bias term. The commit collects all rows/cols/vals into host lists, does two batched async H2D transfers (async_tensor_h2d with pinned memory), and applies a single vectorized `logits.index_put_(indices, values, accumulate=True)` on device, replacing N small GPU launches with one.

Replaces the Python stdlib `base64` module with the SIMD-accelerated drop-in `pybase64` package across all base64 usage, including runtime hot paths: multimodal video decode (`vllm/multimodal/media/video.py` now calls `pybase64.b64decode(data)`), `audio.py`, the pooling embed protocol serializer, the IPC weight-transfer engine, run_batch, and the realtime connection. pybase64 accelerates base64 encode/decode of large multimodal/embedding payloads, reducing per-request CPU/latency versus the pure-Python stdlib implementation. A pre-commit `check_forbidden_imports` rule is also added to forbid `import base64` and require `pybase64`.

In TritonMLAImpl.forward_mqa the decode attn-logits scratch tensor was allocated per-call via torch.empty((B, q_num_heads, num_kv_splits, kv_lora_rank+1), float32) on the decode hot path. The commit moves this to a shared workspace buffer pre-reserved at worst case in TritonMLAMetadataBuilder.__init__ (_reserve_attn_logits_workspace), so forward_mqa fetches via current_workspace_manager().get_simultaneous() with no per-call GPU allocation, and no runtime buffer growth after the workspace is locked (needed before cudagraph capture).

In the V1 LLMEngine, abort_request() only called scheduler.finish_requests() but never removed the request from the engine-side tracking dicts (self.requests, self.num_lagged_steps, self.request_outputs). Aborted requests therefore accumulated indefinitely in these dicts — a memory leak. The fix adds a _free_request() helper and calls it from abort_request(), and also uses it in the finished-request path. (A secondary guard `if req_id not in self.requests: continue` fixes a KeyError when a request is aborted mid-detokenization — a functional crash fix.)

In AiterMLAImpl.forward_mqa, the decode output tensor `o` was allocated with torch.zeros then immediately passed as the output buffer to rocm_aiter_ops.mla_decode_fwd(q, kv_buffer, o, ...), which fully writes it. The zero-fill was a redundant memset kernel launched every decode step; changing torch.zeros->torch.empty removes that wasted work in the per-token decode hot path.

The chunk-mask builder looped `for i in range(size)` computing scalar start/end indices in Python and writing a tensor slice `ret[i, start:ending] = True` each iteration — O(size) Python iterations plus O(size) small device writes. The commit replaces it with a fully vectorized implementation using `torch.arange`, `torch.clamp`, and a single broadcast comparison `(col_indices >= start_indices) & (col_indices < end_indices)`, eliminating the Python loop and per-row kernel/slice overhead.

The AWQ GEMM CUDA kernels (gemm_forward_4bit_cuda_m16n128k32 / m16n64k32) were launched with `<<<num_blocks, threads_per_block>>>`, i.e. on the default stream (0). The fix captures `stream = at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream()` and launches with `<<<..., 0, stream>>>` so they run on the current PyTorch CUDA stream. Launching on the default stream forces implicit synchronization with all other streams, serializing the AWQ GEMM against the rest of the model's ops and creating bubbles / breaking async overlap (as well as risking incorrect ordering vs. current-stream work).

In MLA's chunked-prefill context attention loop (compute_prefill_context / the sparse variant), each merge iteration previously called `output_tmp = torch.empty_like(output)` and `output_lse_tmp = torch.empty_like(output_lse)`, allocating two fresh temp tensors per chunk iteration. The fix hoists allocation out of the loop: it allocates `merge_output`/`merge_output_lse` once on first merge (`if merge_output is None`) and then ping-pongs buffers via `output, merge_output = merge_output, output`, so the loop reuses two buffers instead of allocating new ones every iteration. This reduces allocator churn/fragmentation across chunks.

The ViT torch-SDPA attention path looped `for i in range(1, len(cu_seqlens))` and sliced `q[:, cu_seqlens[i-1]:cu_seqlens[i]]` using GPU-tensor scalars as Python slice bounds, which forces an implicit device→host synchronization per iteration (one sync per image/sequence chunk). The commit replaces this with a single `(cu_seqlens[1:] - cu_seqlens[:-1]).tolist()` sync followed by `torch.split(...)` on all of q/k/v, collapsing N per-chunk syncs into one and removing the sync from the inner loop.

Adds a new W4A8 path to the Marlin mixed-precision GEMM: 4-bit weights combined with 8-bit activations (INT8 or FP8-e4m3), controlled by the new env var VLLM_MARLIN_INPUT_DTYPE (default None = old W4A16 path). Activations are dynamically quantized to 8-bit (marlin_quant_input) and fed to a new kernel variant with an extra `a_type` template axis, giving faster low-precision GEMM on supported archs. This is a new capability / latent-throughput optimization, not a restoration of previously-broken performance — no prior W4A8 path existed to regress.

Adds a new torch.compile post-grad pass (AllReduceFusionPass) that pattern-matches the sequence `tensor_model_parallel_all_reduce` -> `rms_norm`/`fused_add_rms_norm` and replaces it with a single FlashInfer TRT-LLM fused kernel `flashinfer_trtllm_fused_allreduce_norm`. This fuses the TP allreduce and the RMSNorm into one one-shot allreduce kernel, avoiding a separate collective + norm launch/round-trip (with a size-gated fallback to the unfused path via `_FI_MAX_SIZES`). This is a latent inefficiency (the unfused allreduce+norm was always the path); no prior commit regressed it.

Integrates the FlashInfer trtllm-gen MLA decode kernel as a new opt-in attention backend (FLASHINFER_MLA). FlashInferMLAImpl._forward_decode routes DeepSeek-style MLA decode to trtllm_batch_decode_with_kv_cache_mla, providing an alternative (faster on Blackwell) decode path vs the existing CUTLASS/FlashMLA/Triton MLA backends. It adds max_seq_len plumbing through MLACommonMetadata needed by the kernel. No prior commit degraded this path; it is a new fast kernel for an always-present workload, i.e. a latent-inefficiency optimization rather than a regression fix.

Adds a new "AllSpark" W8A16 dequant+GEMM CUDA kernel specialized for Ampere (sm80-89) int8 GPTQ (uint8b128, group_size=-1, no act-order, no zero-points) with N32K16 weight repacking, multistage cp.async pipelining, split-K, and a cuBLAS fallback above M threshold. It is registered in _POSSIBLE_KERNELS ahead of MarlinLinearKernel ("in priority/performance order"), so eligible layers now dispatch to AllSpark instead of Marlin, reducing per-GEMM latency for W8A16 quantized linear layers on Ampere.

Adds a FlashInfer-backed implementation of the Mamba selective_state_update (SSU) kernel as an alternative to the default Triton kernel. A new dispatch layer (ssu_dispatch.py) with TritonSSUBackend/FlashInferSSUBackend is selected via a new MambaConfig.backend / --mamba-backend flag (default TRITON), initialized once per runner via initialize_mamba_ssu_backend(). mamba_mixer.py/mamba_mixer2.py now call the dispatched selective_state_update instead of importing the Triton kernel directly, letting mamba decode-step state updates run through the faster FlashInfer kernel when opted in. This is an alternative-kernel optimization for an always-slow(er) path, not a regression fix.

Adds a new INT3 (3-bit) symmetric block-scaled quantization codec (CodecQ3) to the ROCm quickreduce two-shot all-reduce. Quantizing the exchanged tensor to 3 bits reduces inter-GPU communication volume for the all-reduce, speeding it up. INT3 is gated to TP2 (world_size==2) because on TP4/TP8 the codec's pack/unpack overhead outweighs the reduced communication volume (per the added dispatch macro comment and the Python guard warning).

Introduces the Dual-Batch Overlap (DBO) mechanism: a batch is split into two microbatches (UBatchSlice/ubatch_splitting) run on separate threads, each with its own forward context, using a dedicated comm_stream vs compute_stream and CUDA events (yield_and_switch_from_compute_to_comm / comm_to_compute) to overlap MoE expert-parallel all2all communication with computation, hiding communication latency and filling GPU bubbles. Gated by new opt-in flags enable_dbo (default False) and dbo_decode_token_threshold=32; requires the deepep_low_latency all2all backend.

Adds a new hardware-specialized CUTLASS MLA decode kernel (`cutlass_mla_decode` / `cutlass_mla_decode_sm100a`) for Blackwell SM100 GPUs, using a TMA warp-specialized FMHA MLA kernel (`Sm100FmhaMlaKernelTmaWarpspecialized`) with an automatic split-KV tile scheduler to balance workload across SMs. It provides a new faster attention path for DeepSeek-style MLA decode on Blackwell; it does not restore behavior an earlier commit broke.

Flips the default of VLLM_USE_STANDALONE_COMPILE from "0" to "1", so make_compiler() selects InductorStandaloneAdaptor (Inductor standalone_compile path) instead of the plain InductorAdaptor for torch >= 2.8/2.9. This changes the torch.compile / Inductor caching-and-codegen path used during model compilation, affecting compile/startup time and cache reuse rather than runtime step throughput.

The commit removes a per-model override in MambaModelConfig.verify_and_update_config that, when compilation_config.cudagraph_mode was unset (None), forced it to CUDAGraphMode.FULL_AND_PIECEWISE for hybrid/mamba models (excluding Lfm2/MiniMaxText01). CUDA graph mode governs how much of the decode/prefill path is captured into CUDA graphs, directly affecting launch overhead / GPU bubbles. The override was originally added (#22594) "to optimize performance"; it is now removed because the framework's default cudagraph-mode resolution (auto-resolve based on attn backend, cf. #32936) makes the explicit override redundant. This is a config-default cleanup, not a fix for a regression nor a new optimization.

On ROCm, the high-throughput DeepEP DBO (dual-batch overlap) path was corrupting DP+EP generation accuracy. The fix stabilizes it by degrading three throughput mechanisms: (1) forces `async_scheduling=False` for the rocm+enable_dbo+deepep_high_throughput combo, (2) inserts a hard host-side `torch.cuda.current_stream().synchronize()` after HT dispatch and combine (via `_sync_dbo_comm_if_needed`, gated on `sync_dbo_comm=is_rocm()` and `dbo_enabled()`) to stop the next DBO ubatch from reusing the Buffer-owned comm workspace before the prior HT kernel finishes, and (3) sets `comm_sms=0` so no CUs are reserved for DeepEP comm under DBO. The synchronize serializes compute/comm, defeating DBO overlap; this trades throughput for correctness on the ROCm HT path.

On Blackwell (device capability family 100 / SM100) the default MLA attention backend is switched from CUTLASS_MLA to FLASHINFER_MLA, and the default MLA prefill path is switched to TRTLLM ragged DeepSeek prefill (use_trtllm_ragged_deepseek_prefill default flips False->True, and it is now checked before FlashInfer/CUDNN/FlashAttention in MLACommonImpl.__init__). This selects different attention kernels for MLA models (e.g. DeepSeek) on Blackwell, changing decode/prefill throughput and latency without any functional change.

On Intel XPU, XPUWorker.init_device previously forced os.environ["CCL_ZE_IPC_EXCHANGE"]="pidfd" (originally "drmfd" in b69781f10) before initializing the oneCCL distributed backend. This env var selects the Level-Zero IPC handle exchange mechanism used by oneCCL collectives (all_reduce, etc.) across ranks in tensor/pipeline parallelism. The commit removes the forced default, letting oneCCL use its own built-in default, which affects the IPC exchange path (and thus collective-communication latency/throughput, and startup viability when pidfd is unsupported by the kernel/driver).

Introduces VLLM_RAY_DP_PACK_STRATEGY controlling how DP ranks are placed on Ray nodes ("strict": exactly data_parallel_size_local ranks per node; "fill": as many as fit on non-master nodes). Placement locality directly affects expert-parallel/all2all communication performance: the new guard raises when strategy="fill" with deepep_high_throughput/deepep_low_latency because "DeepEP kernels require EP ranks [0,7] ... to be on the same node". The default becomes "strict", changing the prior fill-like allocation of available_engine_count ranks per non-master node.

Introduces the piecewise-cudagraph torch.compile backend: VllmBackend splits the fx graph at non-capturable ops (e.g. attention) via split_graph, and PiecewiseBackend captures a torch.cuda.CUDAGraph around each compilable subgraph and replays it (entry.cudagraph.replay()) to eliminate per-kernel launch overhead / CPU-side bubbles, while leaving the attention op running eagerly. Mechanism targets launch-overhead/gpu-util, but it is new opt-in infrastructure (CompilationConfig.use_cudagraph defaults to False, gated behind VLLM_TORCH_COMPILE_LEVEL=PIECEWISE), plus a benchmark() harness comparing eager/full-cudagraph/piecewise and new tests — not a change to an already-active hot path.

Introduces the chunked-prefill feature end-to-end: a new SchedulingBudget/Scheduler path that mixes chunked prefill chunks with decode tokens in one batch (ScheduledSequenceGroup.token_chunk_size, _schedule_running/_schedule_swapped with enable_chunking), attention-backend support for mixed prefill+decode metadata, plus benchmark flags. It is an opt-in performance feature (gated by enable_chunked_prefill, default off) that improves prefill/decode batching efficiency; it does not fix a prior regression nor optimize the existing default codepath.

In `_resolve_fi_ar_backend`, the single-node `auto` branch previously forced `backend = "trtllm"` as a workaround for an mnnvl cudagraph hang (issue #35772). This commit changes it to `backend = "mnnvl"` (with a trtllm fallback when NVSwitch multicast is absent), because the upstream FlashInfer hang was fixed (>=0.6.12). mnnvl is the faster fused-allreduce path for single-node TP, so the default change re-enables the better-performing backend that had been intentionally disabled.

Reverts PR #28971, which had made get_mxfp4_backend_with_lora() prefer the Triton matmul_ogs-based UnfusedOAITritonExperts kernel (on SM90/SM100 with triton_kernels available) for mxfp4 FusedMoE-with-LoRA, falling back to Marlin. The revert restores the unconditional 'return Mxfp4Backend.MARLIN' and deletes UnfusedOAITritonExperts, so mxfp4 MoE LoRA now always runs the Marlin expert path instead of the (typically faster) fused Triton path. This changes which MoE GEMM backend executes for that configuration; net effect on throughput depends on relative kernel speed on the target GPU.

Enabling batch invariance (TRITON_ATTN backend) now forces the unified attention 2D kernel and disables the 3D split-KV / parallel-softmax kernel: the launch condition gains `or is_batch_invariant`, where `is_batch_invariant = vllm_is_batch_invariant()`. The 3D kernel exists to parallelize the softmax reduction across segments (num_par_softmax_segments/segm_output/segm_max/segm_expsum) — a flash-decoding-style optimization that improves decode GPU utilization for few-sequence/long-context batches. Under the batch-invariant config this speedup is deliberately given up (the split-softmax reduction is not batch-invariant), so it is a config-gated perf tradeoff to gain determinism, not a regression fix or a general optimization. Also relaxes backend gating so TRITON_ATTN is treated as a decode-invariant backend.

Exposes a new config flag `DynamicShapesConfig.assume_32_bit_indexing` (default True) that controls whether Inductor's `assume_32bit_indexing` patch is applied during torch.compile. That inductor setting (added in #30459/763963aa7, torch 2.10.0.dev+) lets generated GPU kernels use faster 32-bit index arithmetic instead of 64-bit; this commit makes it disableable for tensors large enough that 32-bit indexing would be incorrect. It also adds `dynamic_shapes_config` to the compilation hash factors so the flag participates in the compile cache key.

The DeepEP low-latency internode all2all kernel takes an `allow_nvlink_for_low_latency_mode` flag driven by env var VLLM_DEEPEP_LOW_LATENCY_ALLOW_NVLINK. The default was "0"/False, so the NVLink transport path for the low-latency MoE dispatch/combine kernel was disabled by default; traffic fell back to RDMA even on systems with NVLink (GB200-like). This commit flips the default to "1"/True, enabling the faster NVLink path by default for lower dispatch/combine latency.

For V1, the per-modality default of `limit_mm_per_prompt` changes from 1 to 999 via the new `MultiModalConfig.get_default_limit_per_prompt()` returning `999 if envs.VLLM_USE_V1 else 1`. The effective limit is `min(user_limit, supported_limit)` (new `get_allowed_mm_limits`), so profiling now allocates dummy inputs for the model's full supported number of items per modality (all enabled modalities) rather than 1. This enlarges the worst-case multimodal memory profiling footprint used to size KV cache/activation budget during startup, which is why the examples were edited to disable unused modalities to save memory.

The auto-enable heuristic in EngineArgs (introduced in #6666, "Chunked prefill is enabled by default for long context models") sets self.enable_chunked_prefill=True for GPU models meeting a set of conditions. This commit adds `and not current_platform.is_rocm()` so that on AMD/ROCm GPUs chunked prefill is NOT auto-enabled by default, because the chunked-prefill attention path was not performant on ROCm and defaulting it on degraded throughput/behavior there.

The headline change flips the default of VLLM_FLASHINFER_MOE_BACKEND from "throughput" (TensorRT-LLM CUTLASS kernels) to "latency" (TensorRT-LLM low-latency MoE kernels) for FlashInfer FP8/FP4 MoE on SM100. The diff also reveals the throughput/CUTLASS path "does not support block quantization" (new ValueError in fp8.py get_fp8_moe_backend), so the previous default was non-functional for block-quantized FP8 MoE; the new default routes to the latency kernels that work and are optimized for low-latency inference. A secondary genuine micro-optimization changes scaled_fp4_quant's output_scale allocation from torch.zeros to torch.empty and instead zeros only the padding region inside the CUDA quant kernel (new round_up padding loop), avoiding a full-buffer memset per call.

Previously `set_inductor_config` unconditionally set `config["max_autotune"]=True` and `config["coordinate_descent_tuning"]=True` for specific batch sizes. These inductor tuning passes are expensive at compile time (they benchmark many kernel configs). The commit replaces the hardcoded `True` with two new env vars (VLLM_ENABLE_INDUCTOR_MAX_AUTOTUNE, VLLM_ENABLE_INDUCTOR_COORDINATE_DESCENT_TUNING), both defaulting to 1/True. This does not change default runtime behavior but lets users disable the slow autotuning to reduce torch.compile time. The vars are also added to compute_hash so the compile cache key reflects them.

The commit reverts PR #35271, removing the pure-PyTorch CUDA fallbacks `fp8_mqa_logits_torch`/`fp8_paged_mqa_logits_torch` from the DeepseekV3.2 sparse attention indexer and hard-requiring the fused DeepGEMM FP8 kernels. The removed fallbacks were explicitly labeled "less efficient PyTorch implementation" and used Python loops over batch/blocks with per-iteration `.item()` device syncs plus unfused bf16/fp32 einsum/matmul — dramatically slower than the DeepGEMM fused FP8 path. The revert also changes the selection gate from `is_deep_gemm_supported()` back to `has_deep_gemm()` and replaces the silent `logger.warning_once` slow-path with a hard `RuntimeError`. Net effect: eliminates a silent slow-path (perf-cliff escape hatch) rather than speeding up a live hot path when DeepGEMM is present.

For MLA models, backend selection previously only chose FlashMLA when the user explicitly set VLLM_ATTENTION_BACKEND=FLASHMLA; otherwise it fell through to the TritonMLA backend (`return "vllm.attention.backends.triton_mla.TritonMLABackend"`). This commit makes FlashMLA the default whenever no env var is set and `is_flashmla_supported()[0]` is true, and correspondingly forces `cache_config.block_size = 64` (FlashMLA's required block size) for `model_config.use_mla`. Net effect: MLA models (e.g. DeepSeek) now run the faster FlashMLA attention kernel by default instead of the slower Triton MLA kernel.

The multi-modal preprocessor cache (a perf feature that avoids re-running expensive image/video preprocessing) is auto-disabled when data_parallel_size>1 and not external-LB, i.e. when there is no one-to-one correspondence between API process and EngineCore process. In that many-to-many topology (introduced by the API-server scaleout work) the cache cannot be shared consistently, so it must be turned off for correctness — at the cost of losing the caching speedup for MM+DP deployments. This is a correctness fix that deliberately disables a performance path, not an optimization.

This reverts PR #34492 (3bb4e4311), which fused Qwen3.5 GatedDeltaNet's four separate input projection GEMMs into two fused MergedColumnParallelLinear modules (in_proj_qkvz = [key_dim,key_dim,value_dim,value_dim] and in_proj_ba = [num_v_heads]*2, loaded via tuple shard ids). Fusing these projections into fewer, larger matmuls reduces kernel-launch overhead and improves GEMM efficiency per GDN layer. The revert re-splits them back into four independent ColumnParallelLinear/MergedColumnParallel calls (in_proj_qkv, in_proj_z, in_proj_b, in_proj_a) in Qwen3_5GatedDeltaNet.forward, and removes the tuple-shard-id support (`isinstance(loaded_shard_id, tuple)` + per-index output_sizes) added to MergedColumnParallelLinear. Net runtime effect: undoes the projection-fusion speedup. The fusion was re-landed shortly after in #34697 ('[Bugfix] Redo Qwen3.5/Qwen3-Next GDN projector fusion'), indicating the original had a functional/loading bug prompting a temporary revert.

The CUTLASS w8a8 scaled_mm_dq GEMMs were invoked as `gemm_op(args, workspace.get())` / `gemm_op.run(args)` without passing a CUDA stream, so they launched on the (legacy) default stream. Under CUDA graph capture (torch.cuda.graph), work must be issued on the capture/side stream; a launch on the default stream is not captured into the graph correctly and breaks/serializes CUDA-graph replay. The fix fetches `at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream(a.get_device())` and threads it into the GEMM call so the kernel is enqueued on the active capture stream, preserving the CUDA-graph fast path (a major latency/util optimization in vLLM).

`create_engine_configs` stopped passing `self.enable_prefix_caching` to `CacheConfig`, so `CacheConfig.enable_prefix_caching` fell back to its default `False`. This forced `BlockSpaceManager` down the `else` branch using `UncachedBlockAllocator` instead of `CachedBlockAllocator`, meaning `--enable-prefix-caching` was silently a no-op and shared prompt prefixes were re-prefilled every request instead of being cache-reused. The fix re-adds `self.enable_prefix_caching` to the `CacheConfig(...)` call (plus info logs of the active mode).

In TritonAttentionImpl.forward (fp8 kv-cache path), the assertion compared a GPU tensor `layer._q_scale` against a Python float 1.0 on every forward call. Inside a method the file itself flags as executed in eager mode under piece-wise CUDA graphs (with a NOTE to minimize PyTorch/CPU overhead), a `torch.Tensor == float` comparison forces a data-dependent GPU→CPU sync and causes a torch.compile graph break. The fix precomputes `layer._q_scale_float = q_scale.item()` once at weight-load time and asserts against that Python scalar, eliminating the per-forward tensor op / sync and the compile break.

In FlashAttnMLAMetadataBuilder._build_decode, max_seq_len was computed as `seq_lens_device.max().item()` — a `.item()` on a GPU tensor, which forces a blocking device→host synchronization every decode step. Under async scheduling (whose purpose is to overlap CPU metadata prep with GPU compute), this sync both stalls the overlap (creating a GPU bubble) and reads a device seq_lens value that is not yet valid, breaking FlashAttn MLA. The fix threads a host-side `dcp_local_seq_lens_cpu` tensor through CommonAttentionMetadata and switches to `seq_lens_cpu.max().item()`, removing the per-step host-device sync while keeping the device tensor only for the actual kernel/scheduler inputs.

FusedMoE allocated the dp-chunking scratch buffers (batched_hidden_states/batched_router_logits) in __init__ gated on self.use_dp_chunking, but that enablement was evaluated too early/incorrectly, so the chunked FlashInfer-Cutlass MoE forward path (used under DP with pplx/deepep_ll kernels) could be mis-enabled. The fix defers buffer allocation to a lazy ensure_dp_chunking_init() called from apply()/forward, guarded by `if not self.use_dp_chunking or self.batched_hidden_states is not None: return`, so the chunked perf path is entered only when actually applicable. Note the use_dp_chunking boolean itself is unchanged (only a stale comment was removed); the fix is about *when/whether* buffers are set up, controlling entry into the chunked kernel path.

Refactors the "keep multimodal field on CPU" mechanism: replaces the model-class-level `multimodal_cpu_fields = {"image_grid_thw", ...}` set with a per-field `keep_on_cpu=True` flag on `MultiModalFieldConfig.batched/flat/flat_from_sizes` (stored on `BaseMultiModalField`). `reduce_data` now overrides device to "cpu" and disables pin_memory when `keep_on_cpu` is set. The underlying perf purpose (unchanged) is to keep small index tensors like `grid_thw` on CPU so model code can call `.tolist()`/`.prod()` without forcing a GPU->host CUDA sync stall in the vision path. Which fields stay on CPU is identical before/after (qwen2_vl, qwen2_5_vl, qwen3_vl, hunyuan_vision, opencua), so runtime behavior is preserved — this is a plumbing/config refactor of a perf-sensitive path, not a new fix or optimization.

Adds a new `--prefix-caching-hash-algo` config knob (choices: 'builtin' | 'sha256') threaded through EngineArgs → CacheConfig → KVCacheManager → block_pool/kv_cache_utils, so `hash_block_tokens`/`hash_request_tokens` now take a `hash_function` callable instead of hardcoding builtin `hash`. The default stays 'builtin' (the fast path); selecting 'sha256' is an opt-in collision-resistant path that pickles inputs and runs hashlib.sha256 per block, adding per-token latency. No pre-existing slowness is fixed and no regression is restored — it introduces a performance/safety tradeoff knob.

The DeepEP low-latency all2all manager passes allow_nvlink_for_low_latency_mode to Buffer(). This commit removes the VLLM_DEEPEP_LOW_LATENCY_ALLOW_NVLINK env var and hardcodes that argument to True, making NVLink usage for the internode_ll kernel permanent. Per the removed code comment, NVLink gives "better latency on GB200 like system", so the setting affects the latency of the MoE expert-parallel all2all communication. At this commit the runtime default was already "1" (True), so effective behavior is unchanged; the commit just deletes the ability to turn it off. The actual perf change (NVLink on by default) happened earlier when the env default flipped from "0" to "1".

This commit reverts PR #30746 (b10f41c89), which had enabled FP8 compute for prefill MLA on SM100/Blackwell. The reverted code cast q/k/v to `current_platform.fp8_dtype()` and passed `q_data_type=self.q_data_type` (fp8 when cache_dtype is fp8) into the FlashInfer/TRT-LLM ragged prefill kernels (`_run_prefill_new_tokens_fi`, `_run_prefill_context_chunk_fi`, `_run_prefill_new_tokens_trtllm_ragged`, etc.). This revert removes the `fp8_attention` threading and forces `q_data_type=self.model_config.dtype`, so prefill again runs in the model (bf16) dtype rather than the faster fp8 path. Because it's a same-day revert of a perf feature, it likely backs out an optimization that caused a correctness/functional issue; the revert's own motivation is not documented in the offline diff, so intent is unclear.

Extends `reorder_batch_to_split_decodes_and_prefills` to sort the persistent batch into three contiguous regions (decode → extend → prefill) instead of two, distinguishing "extend" requests (non-decode with existing context, num_computed_tokens > num_scheduled_tokens) from fresh "prefill" (num_computed_tokens == num_scheduled_tokens). This grouping is an enabler so attention backends can dispatch memory-bound vs compute-bound request classes to specialized kernel paths; but this commit only changes the reordering primitive and adds a unit test — no backend consumer is wired to use the new extend region here and no perf number is claimed.

Adds a new `nobind` value for the `VLLM_CPU_OMP_THREADS_BIND` env var so users can disable per-core CPU affinity binding and inherit user-defined OpenMP variables (e.g. `OMP_NUM_THREADS`). When `nobind` is set, vLLM skips `torch.ops._C_utils.init_cpu_threads_env(...)` and does not overwrite `OMP_NUM_THREADS` with `torch.get_num_threads()`, leaving OpenMP thread count/affinity fully to the user. This is a CPU threading-affinity tuning knob that affects throughput; the built-in default remains `auto`, so it is opt-in.

Adds a new `moe_backend` field to `SpeculativeConfig` and a `_create_draft_vllm_config()` hook so the speculative/draft model can select a different MoE expert-kernel backend (e.g. triton vs flashinfer_trtllm/cutlass) than the target model. Since moe_backend picks which MoE kernel implementation executes, it directly affects MoE-layer throughput; the feature lets users tune the drafter's kernels independently (e.g. quantized generator with unquantized drafter). Default (None) preserves prior behavior by inheriting the target's backend, so it is a new perf-tuning knob rather than a default change.

Introduces max_num_scheduled_tokens = max_num_batched_tokens - (max_num_new_slots_for_drafting * max_num_seqs) so the scheduler issues fewer tokens per iteration under speculative decoding, keeping the post-drafting batch within the target model's configured max_num_batched_tokens. This replaces the prior approach (from af3162d3a) that instead *grew* the eagle buffers by net_num_new_slots_per_request*max_batch_size and *extended* the torch.compile compile_range_end by multiplier*max_num_seqs. Net effect: correctly caps the batched-token budget for drafting while shrinking compile-range endpoints and persistent buffer allocation, at the cost of a slightly smaller effective schedulable batch.

Under ZeRO-3 with torch autocast, model weights are already fp32 and can be updated directly by the optimizer, but the code unconditionally allocated a separate fp32 master-weight copy via `.clone().float().detach()`. The fix adds a branch that, when `fp16_partitioned_groups_flat[i].dtype == torch.float32` and no optimizer offloading/swapping is active, aliases the existing fp32 param as the master weight (`fp32_partitioned_groups_flat.append(self.fp16_partitioned_groups_flat[i])`), eliminating a redundant full-size fp32 accelerator-memory allocation. The corresponding `.data.copy_` in `_reassign_or_swap_out_partitioned_parameters` becomes a no-op self-copy in that case.

In ZeRO stages 1/2/3, `_overflow_check_and_loss_scale_update` / step unconditionally called `self.check_overflow()` every optimizer step, which scans all gradients for inf/nan and performs a distributed all-reduce (plus a GPU→CPU sync of the overflow flag) to agree across ranks. The commit guards this behind `if self.dtype == torch.float16`, so bf16 (and fp32) training skips the per-step overflow scan+collective entirely. Rationale in the message: "bf16 dynamic range is similar to fp32. don't check overflow by default" — bf16 essentially never overflows, so the check is wasted work.

In ZeRO-3 param init, the all-gather paths issued an unconditional device-wide get_accelerator().synchronize() ("guarantee the communication to be completed") plus per-handle current_stream().synchronize() in NoGather(Coalesced)Handle.wait(). These are hard host<->device stalls that serialize the stream and create bubbles during parameter gathering. The commit gates them behind get_accelerator().resolves_data_dependency(), so accelerators whose runtime natively tracks cross-stream data dependencies skip the redundant explicit sync. Latent inefficiency, not a regression: the synchronize() calls were always present.

ZeRO stage 3 registered forward/backward module hooks (_pre_forward_module_hook, _post_forward_module_hook, etc.) but never stored their handles, so they could never be removed. Each hook closure captures the optimizer/module state, so a destroyed engine's hooks (and everything they reference) stayed alive. The commit records handles in self.forward_hooks/self.backward_hooks and adds destroy()/_remove_module_hooks() (surfaced via DeepSpeedEngine.destroy()) so hooks can be removed and the referenced memory reclaimed on teardown.

The `instrument_w_nvtx` decorator wraps functions with `get_accelerator().range_push/range_pop` (NVTX markers). These are sourceless calls Dynamo cannot trace, so each wrapped function forces a graph break under torch.compile. Since the decorator is applied widely across the codebase, this fragments the compiled graph and reduces throughput. The fix guards both range_push and range_pop with `not is_compiling()`, so NVTX instrumentation is skipped while torch.compile is tracing, eliminating the graph breaks.

In the DeepSpeed inference kernel, `ds_linear_layer` (used for the non-fused QKV projection on HF Bert/encoder models) allocated its output with `at::empty({...}, options)` on every forward call. This commit changes it to lazily allocate the persistent inference workspace (`allocate_workspace<T>(...)` if not yet allocated) and return the output via `at::from_blob(workspace, {...}, options)`, reusing the preallocated buffer instead of doing a fresh per-call allocation — matching the pattern already used by `ds_softmax_context`/`einsum_sec_sm_ecm`. The Python side passes `layer_id` so the workspace can be sized for `num_layers`.

Before this commit ZeRO3 built fp16/fp32 partitions and initialized optimizer states (Adam momentum+variance master copies) over `self.optimizer.param_groups`, i.e. ALL parameters including frozen ones (requires_grad=False). The commit adds `_get_trainable_parameter_groups()` filtering to `p.requires_grad`, and threads `self.trainable_param_groups` through `_create_fp16_partitions_with_defragmentation(...)`, `_create_fp32_partitions` (`for j, param_group in enumerate(self.trainable_param_groups)`), and optimizer-state init. Frozen params no longer get fp32 master copies or optimizer state allocated, saving memory; it also enables frozen-weight training under ZeRO3 which previously mis-handled such params.

PartitionedTensor.from_meta reads its meta tensor via meta.tolist(), which forces a device-to-host synchronization. In pipeline+tensor(slice)-parallel training this happened on every activation and gradient exchange between stages. The commit caches a CPU copy of the meta tensor (inputs[0].to('cpu'), outputs[0].to('cpu'), grad_layer[0].to('cpu')) in pipe_partition_*_meta_cache fields, reusing it across microbatches so the GPU->CPU sync happens once instead of every communication, removing per-step pipeline bubbles.

Adds experimental torch.compile support for ZeRO stages 1/2/3 via a new CompiledModuleWrapper that lazily calls torch.compile(self.wrapped, backend=..., **kwargs) on the first forward, gated by a new "compile" config section (CompileConfig with enabled: bool = False, backend: str = "inductor"). To make Dynamo tracing safe over ZeRO's dynamic param gather/release, it decorates all TorchBackend communication collectives (all_reduce, all_gather, reduce_scatter, broadcast, all_to_all, send/recv, barrier, etc.) and the PartitionedParameterCoordinator bookkeeping methods (record_module, fetch_sub_module) with @compiler.disable, where compiler.disable maps to torch.compiler.disable. Net effect: enables compiled/fused execution for speedup rather than fixing a prior slowdown.

Rewrites the inference `attn_softmax_v2` CUDA kernel for better GPU efficiency: (1) templates the kernel on `iterations` (`template <int iterations>`, removing the runtime `int iterations` param) so the inner loop bound is a compile-time constant and can be unrolled; (2) changes per-thread indexing from `i*(reduceWidth<<2)+(seq_lane<<2)` to `i*(reduceWidth<<2)+seq_lane` with `+reduceWidth*k` strides, converting 4-contiguous-per-thread loads into coalesced accesses across the warp (commit msg: "Coalesce memory"); (3) hoists mask/alibi handling out of the per-element path into `if(mask&&alibi)/else if(mask)/else if(alibi)/else` branches to cut divergence. Latent inefficiency, not a regression.

ZeRO++ hierarchical partitioning (hpz) keeps an intra-node secondary tensor replica so that parameter all-gathers can use the cheap intra-node process group instead of a full inter-node all-gather. Previously use_secondary_tensor was gated on `params[0].ds_secondary_tensor is not None and not forward`, and the intra-node group was only used `if self.zero_param_process_group and not forward` — so hpz only applied during the backward pass. In forward-heavy / no-grad scenarios (gradient accumulation, activation checkpointing, torch.no_grad, model.eval(), LoRA frozen weights, gradient overflow) the expensive full-world all-gather was used. The commit drops the `forward` flag entirely and gates hpz solely on secondary-tensor presence (`use_secondary_tensor = params[0].ds_secondary_tensor is not None`), invalidating the secondary tensor only when master weights change (`if param.ds_secondary_tensor is None:` guard in _partition_param_sec), so the cheaper intra-node all-gather path is taken in many more scenarios, raising throughput.

Adds tensor-parallel sharding for the lm_head/embed_out final projection under AutoTP inference. The new LmHeadLinearAllreduce.forward shards the input feature dim across ranks (input[..., rank*shard:(rank+1)*shard] @ weight.T) then does inference_all_reduce, distributing the large [hidden, vocab] matmul that was previously run replicated/redundantly on every rank. This reduces per-rank compute and weight memory for the largest final layer, at the cost of one all-reduce.

The Ulysses SP attention registration validates `core_attn_implementation` against a hardcoded allowlist that previously only contained `flash_attention_2` and `sdpa`, raising ValueError otherwise. The commit adds `flash_attention_3` to `supported_attn_implementation`, letting users select the FA3 kernel as the core attention backend. FA3 is a faster/more memory-efficient attention kernel, enabling much longer sequences; it does not alter any existing hot path but unblocks selecting a better one.

Adds an opt-in `non_reentrant_checkpoint` activation-checkpointing function built on PyTorch's `torch.autograd.graph.saved_tensors_hooks`. It enables activation checkpointing (recompute-instead-of-retain, trading compute for memory) for the case where all inputs require no grad. Previously the reentrant `CheckpointFunction` raised a backprop error for such layers, so `PipelineModule.forward` used `_is_checkpointable(funcs)` to *bypass* checkpointing (running `exec_range_func` directly, retaining activations in memory). The new function lets those layers be checkpointed, reducing peak activation memory. It is additive/opt-in — the default `activation_checkpoint_func` remains `checkpointing.checkpoint`, so no default behavior changes here.

In PipelineModule.forward, the activation-checkpoint loop called self._is_checkpointable(funcs) every forward pass. Under torch.compile this call caused a graph break on each forward, breaking the compiled graph and degrading performance. The fix adds _precompute_checkpointable_values() (called once from PipelineEngine after setup) storing per-interval results in self.is_checkpointable_results, and forward now iterates zip(range(...), self.is_checkpointable_results) using the precomputed is_checkpointable_result instead of calling _is_checkpointable, avoiding the graph break.

In ZeRO Stage-3 grad-norm computation, `.item()` was called per-parameter inside the norm-accumulation loop (`total_norm += param_norm.item()**2`) and again on the all-reduced `total_norm_cuda`. Each `.item()` forces a blocking GPU→CPU synchronization, serializing the device on every parameter. The fix keeps accumulation and the inf/nan check as on-device tensor ops (`.isinf()`/`.isnan()`, tensor arithmetic to substitute -1), eliminating the per-param and per-step host syncs.

In non_reentrant_checkpoint, a Python after_backward_hook was unconditionally registered on every leaf tensor via leaf_tensor.register_hook(after_backward_hook). The hook only does real work when PROFILE_TIME or SYNCHRONIZE is enabled (its see_memory_usage call is hardcoded force=False, a no-op). The fix guards the registration loop behind `if PROFILE_TIME or SYNCHRONIZE`, eliminating unnecessary per-leaf-tensor Python callback invocations during the backward pass in the default (non-profiling, non-sync) case.

In ZeRO stage 1/2 with CPU optimizer offload, several CPU buffers (temp_grad_buffer_for_cpu_offload, single_partition_of_fp32_groups[i].grad, and the per-param CPU accumulation buffer) were unconditionally allocated via get_accelerator().pin_memory(...) whenever cpu_offload was true, ignoring the user's offload_optimizer.pin_memory config (default False, documented as trading extra memory overhead for throughput). The fix threads offload_optimizer_config through and only pins when self.cpu_offload_pin_memory is set, so users who set pin_memory=False no longer pay the page-locked-memory overhead.

In PipelineEngine's grad-buffer allocation (`_exec_*` recv path), the send/recv buffer sizes for exchanging gradients between adjacent pipe stages are derived from `sizes_and_dtypes`. The pre-fix code filtered `outputs` with `t.is_floating_point()`, which, when gradient partitioning is active (TP>1 + PP), dropped the small LongTensor metadata used to reconstruct the PartitionedTensor. That caused the two stages to issue send/recv with mismatched buffer counts/sizes, producing a p2p deadlock (hang). The fix branches on `self.is_grad_partitioned` to keep the metadata tensor so both sides agree on buffer shapes.

In InferenceEngine.__init__, when neither an injection_dict nor replace_method=='auto' was set, an unconditional `else` branch (added in #2670/d59b5729) always ran AutoTP.tp_parser(model) — the Automatic Tensor Parallelism path. This meant that a user requesting `replace_with_kernel_inject=True` (the optimized fused-kernel inference path) would instead fall into the AutoTP branch, silently bypassing kernel injection. The fix changes `else:` to `elif not config.replace_with_kernel_inject:` so AutoTP only runs when kernel inject is not requested, letting the intended fused-kernel path proceed.

Two coupled inference-workspace fixes. (1) PR #2359 (commit d5d10b0c) had added an unconditional `if self.config.layer_id == 0:` call to `allocate_workspace(...)` in DeepSpeedTransformerInference.forward, so the workspace (which calls GenWorkSpace / cudaMemGetInfo and may reallocate) ran on EVERY forward pass rather than once; this commit adds a `self.iter == 0` guard so it runs only on the first forward. (2) The hardcoded `#define MAX_OUT_TOKENS 8192` (used for temp_size, cache_size and the `_max_seq_len` cap) is replaced by a configurable `max_out_tokens` defaulting to 1024, and the temp/cache sizing formula is corrected from `head_size * padded_head_size` to `num_heads * effective_head_size`, substantially reducing the default GPU workspace/KV-cache allocation. A softmax.cu grid_dim/reduce_width change (rounding reduce_width to a power-of-2 * WARP_SIZE) is also bundled but is primarily a reduction-correctness fix.

In ZeRO-3's PartitionedParameterCoordinator, `__n_available_params` tracks how much parameter memory is currently gathered and gates prefetching via `max_params_to_prefetch = min(self.__max_n_available_params - self.__n_available_params, self.__prefetch_bucket_sz)`. When a module is invoked more than once per training step (e.g. DPO: a normal call plus a second `no_grad()` call), the counter was driven negative by release_and_reset_all, making `__max_n_available_params - __n_available_params` larger than the intended budget and letting the coordinator over-prefetch/all-gather parameters beyond the memory cap, causing OOM. The fix removes the reset-time-only init and instead sets `self.__n_available_params = 0` inside release_and_reset_all so the budget is correctly re-zeroed. A secondary correctness fix guards `module.ds_grads_remaining` from being reset to 0 on repeated backward hooks.

Introduces ZeRO stage-2: partitions gradients across data-parallel ranks (ZERO_OPTIMIZATION_GRADIENTS=2), plus reduce-scatter for gradient reduction, computation/communication overlap (overlap_comm), and contiguous gradient buffers, alongside activation checkpointing/partitioning in the new deepspeed_checkpointing.py. These reduce per-GPU memory footprint (enabling much larger models) and improve throughput via bucketed reduce-scatter and comm overlap. This is a new feature adding a faster/leaner path, not a fix restoring performance an earlier commit broke.

Changes STEPS_PER_PRINT_DEFAULT from 10 to None, disabling periodic progress reporting by default. Previously every 10 steps the engine called _report_progress and ThroughputTimer.stop() logged throughput while querying get_accelerator().memory_allocated()/max_memory_allocated() and emitting log_dist. Gating this behind `steps_per_print() is not None` / `_is_report_boundary()` avoids that per-report logging + memory-stat overhead by default. Effect on step time is small; primary purpose is logging-verbosity control.

In ZeRO-3, hooks are recursively installed on submodules to gather partitioned params in execution order. For MoE models the expert computation order varies per forward, disrupting ZeRO3 prefetching and adding per-submodule hook overhead (and can deadlock the all-gather). The pre-existing 'leaf module' feature (from #4966) marks a parent module so ZeRO3 gathers all descendants at once and stops inserting hooks. This commit changes the DEFAULT behavior: it adds DEFAULT_LEAF_MODULE_CLASSES (Mixtral/Qwen2/Qwen3 MoE sparse blocks), exposes leaf config via DeepSpeed config, and calls apply_zero_leaf_module_config() in _configure_distributed_model so those MoE blocks are automatically flagged as leaves, improving prefetch efficiency / avoiding hook overhead by default.

Large JAX-backend feature PR adding sequence parallelism (SP), RoPE, and non-gated GeLU FP8 MLP. The perf-relevant part is the addition of `checkpoint_name(dot_1_output, ffn1_ckpt_name)` and `checkpoint_name(dot_2_output, ffn2_ckpt_name)` to the fused MLP fwd rules, letting XLA/`nn.scan` name and rematerialize FFN activations for better activation-checkpointing behavior (memory/throughput), plus SP `with_sharding_constraint` logical-axis annotations that shard sequence dim across TP. All of this is net-new code introduced in this same commit, so it is a latent-inefficiency optimization / new capability, not a fix of a pre-existing regression.

Previously JAX's GroupedGemmV2FFI called nvte_grouped_gemm with `nullptr, // config (use defaults)`, so cuBLASLt derived avg M/N/K algorithm-selection heuristics automatically from the (possibly ragged) total shapes. This commit adds grouped_gemm_avg_dims() to compute accurate per-group average M/N/K (dividing ragged/first dims by num_gemms) and passes them via GroupedMatmulConfigWrapper (set_avg_m/n/k). Per gemm.h, these avg dims are 'Used by cuBLASLt for algorithm selection heuristics', so better per-group estimates let cuBLASLt pick a faster GEMM algorithm.

Adds support for FP8/MXFP8 quantized tensors that hold only column-wise (transpose) data. Previously a saved-for-backward activation kept both the row-wise and column-wise FP8 copies even when only the column-wise (transpose) form is needed for the dgrad GEMM. After the forward GEMM the modules now call `inputmat.update_usage(rowwise_usage=False)` / `ln_out.update_usage(rowwise_usage=False)` to drop the redundant row-wise copy, cutting saved-activation FP8 memory. The C++ side (`Tensor::shape()`, `nvte_tensor_shape`, `flat_first_dim`/`flat_last_dim`, `has_columnwise_data`) is reworked to infer tensor shape from column-wise data alone, and MXFP8 all-gather is taught to gather only column-wise data (less traffic).

Integrates a BF16 grouped GEMM path (nvte_grouped_gemm) for JAX that consumes group sizes that stay on-device, plus a new CUDA-graph-safe FFI variant (te_grouped_gemm_v2_ffi / GroupedGemmV2Handler). The pre-existing grouped GEMM path (te_grouped_gemm_d2h_group_sizes_ffi / GroupedGemmGetGroupSizes) performs a device-to-host copy of group_sizes (cudaEventRecord + cudaStreamWaitEvent + async D2H), which forces a host sync and prevents CUDA-graph capture (the commit even has a step 'disable cuda-graph for GMM'). The V2 path uses a device setup kernel (setup_grouped_gemm_kernel + compute_grouped_tensor_offset) and an int32->int64 conversion kernel to build pointer/dim arrays entirely on device, so no host round-trip is needed and the grouped GEMM (used for MoE expert batching) becomes CUDA-graphable, reducing launch overhead / GPU bubbles. This is a new capability, not restoration of prior performance.

The commit adds Bias/Dbias support to the experimental GroupedLinear op and, as part of that, rewrites the grouped bias-add CUDA kernel. The old kernel launched one block per tensor (blockIdx.x = tensor_idx) with a blockIdx.y column tiling, which underutilizes SMs and is sensitive to per-group row imbalance in MoE workloads. The new kernel uses an SM-filling 2D grid (row blocks x column chunks) with a grid-stride loop over kRowsPerBlock-sized row chunks and a linear scan (find_tensor_for_row) to map rows to their group's bias, plus a warp reduction to compute valid_rows, targeting full-bandwidth (~4 TB/s) execution under load imbalance. It also adds an optional per-row scale (fmaf) path for scale_bias. The changed kernel was itself only introduced in the prior commit 708d7c16 (#2669), so this is optimizing a brand-new experimental path, not restoring performance a stable path previously had.

Adds an opt-in `overwrite_main_grad` weight attribute; when set, the wgrad GEMM is invoked with `accumulate=False` instead of `accumulate=accumulate_wgrad_into_param_main_grad`, so the GEMM overwrites the `main_grad` buffer directly rather than doing a read-modify-write accumulation into it. This enables FSDP gradient fusion: the pre-allocated fused grad buffer no longer needs a separate zero/memset before the first wgrad GEMM, and the accumulating GEMM's read of the output matrix is avoided. It is a latent-inefficiency avoidance for the FSDP-grad-fusion path, not a fix for a prior regression.

Adds `fsdp_pre_all_gather`/`fsdp_post_all_gather` hooks to Float8Tensor plus `__torch_dispatch__` handling for FSDP2 ops (split/new_zeros/as_strided/detach/clone/copy_). `fsdp_pre_all_gather` returns `(self._data,)` — the 1-byte FP8 data — as the all-gather input, so FSDP2 gathers sharded weights in FP8 instead of the default full-precision (bf16/fp16) parameter, roughly halving the all-gather communication volume; `fsdp_post_all_gather` reconstructs a Float8Tensor from the gathered FP8 bytes via make_like. This is a newly-enabled faster comm path, not restoration of a previously-working one.

In JAX _quantize_dbias_impl, when a quantizer requests 2x (rowwise+colwise) tensor-scaling quantization, the fused te_dbias_quantize FFI kernel previously produced both rowwise and colwise casted outputs directly. The commit forces q_layout to ROWWISE (1x) for tensor scaling (`force_1x_quantization = quantizer.scaling_mode.is_tensor_scaling() and quantizer.is_2x2x()`), then derives the colwise output cheaply via `jnp.transpose(rowwise_casted_output, ...)` in JAX. Since tensor scaling shares one scale (colwise_scale_inv = rowwise_scale_inv), the colwise cast is redundant to recompute in the kernel; a transpose is cheaper, improving throughput.

In the JAX FP8 dot/layernorm/geglu forward rules, the kernel (and activation) were quantized via the fused `cast_transpose` custom call, which materialized an explicit FP8 transpose. That extra transpose copy broke XLA's FP8 GEMM pattern matcher when FSDP was enabled, so the GEMM would no longer be recognized/fused as an FP8 GEMM path. The fix replaces `cast_transpose` with a native cast-only (`quantize`/`cast_fp8`) and lets XLA handle the transpose inside `dot_general`, so the FP8 GEMM pattern is preserved and the unnecessary copy is avoided. Two secondary fixes: `tuple(normalize_acts) in geglu_act_pool` (membership test always failed on a list, disabling the fused layernorm-geglu MLP path), and adding 2xACC precision control (Precision.HIGHEST) to the FP8 GEMMs.

Adds support for block-scaled (MXFP8/NVFP4) tensors that already carry GEMM-ready "swizzled" scale factors, plus a `with_gemm_swizzled_scales` flag on tensors. The GEMM-time scale-swizzle path (`swizzle_scales_for_gemm`, `multi_tensor_swizzle_scales_for_gemm`, `inplace_swizzle_scale_for_gemm`) now returns early / filters out tensors whose scales are already swizzled, and quantize kernels (MXFP8/NVFP4) can emit pre-swizzled scales directly. This eliminates a redundant swizzle kernel launch + scale-buffer allocation that previously ran before each GEMM.

For block-scaled recipes (NVFP4/MXFP8) with weight caching across microbatches, the GEMM-ready scale swizzle for the cached weight was recomputed lazily inside every GEMM (swizzle_scales_for_gemm) and discarded. With N microbatches the weight scale swizzle ran 2*N times per step (fprop rowwise + dgrad columnwise) even though the weight is quantized only once. The fix sets weight_quantizer.optimize_for_gemm=True (guarded by `not self.primary_weights_in_fp8`), so the swizzle is done once at quantize time via the post-quantize fallback and persisted on the cached workspace (_with_gemm_swizzled_scales=True), reused by every GEMM (swizzle_scales_for_gemm early-returns) → 2 swizzles per step instead of 2*N. Applied to Linear, LayerNormLinear, LayerNormMLP (fc1+fc2), GroupedLinear. A noop-aware zero_scales_kernel was added in quantize_mxfp8.cuh to keep the padding-clear a CUDA-graph-compatible noop when quantization is skipped (weight caching).

Adds a new backward fusion `BackwardBiasActivation` to the `te.Sequential` fusible-ops framework that combines dbias + dactivation + quantize into a single fused CUDA kernel (`tex.dbias_dgelu` / `tex.dbias_drelu`) instead of running Activation-backward, Bias-backward, and Quantize as three separate ops. The new `fuse_backward_bias_activation` pass rewrites the backward op list, and the test asserts backward_ops shrinks from 3 to 2 fused ops. This reduces kernel launches and extra memory traffic in the backward pass. Prior to this the ops always ran unfused (a latent inefficiency), so it is an optimization rather than a regression-fix.

In the forward pass, `prepare_for_saving(...)` saved BOTH the GEMM workspace weight (weightmat / fc1_weight_final / weights_fp8) AND the original weight Python object (weight / fc1_weight / origin_weights) into `ctx.save_for_backward`. The original full-precision weight parameter was thus redundantly retained alive by the autograd context for the whole forward-to-backward interval, only so backward could re-attach `main_grad`/attributes to it. This commit removes that extra save (drops `fc1_weight`/`fc2_weight`/`weight`/`origin_weights` from the saved tensors) and instead keeps a lightweight `weakref.ref(weight)` plus a cached `overwrite_main_grad` flag, dereferencing it in backward to write `origin_weight.main_grad = main_grad`. Net effect: the original weight tensor is no longer pinned alive by the graph, reducing memory when fuse_wgrad_accumulation is enabled.

In the fusible activation op backward (_ActivationOperation.op_backward), the quantizer argument to _activation_backward_impl (tex.dgelu/dswiglu/etc.) was hardcoded to None, so the gradient dx was always produced in high precision and then quantized to FP8 in a separate downstream step. This commit fetches ctx.prev_op.get_quantizer("backward", 0) and passes it into the backward kernel, fusing the quantize into the activation-backward kernel and eliminating a separate quantize kernel launch plus a full-precision round-trip of dx through memory. It mirrors the forward path, which already fused quantization via next_op.get_quantizer.

For FSDP2 with FP8 2D (128x128) block scaling, the prior implementation all-gathered BOTH rowwise (M,K) data+scales AND columnwise (K,M) data+scales, including transpose/unpad/repad steps. Since columnwise data/scales are just the transpose of rowwise for 2D block scaling, this commit all-gathers only the rowwise tensors and derives columnwise locally via out._create_columnwise() in post_all_gather, eliminating redundant collective communication volume during the FSDP2 unshard.

NVFP4 RHT cast-fusion quantize previously always emitted compact scale factors, forcing te.Linear to run two standalone swizzle kernels (swizzle_row_scaling_kernel + swizzle_col_scaling_kernel) before the cuBLAS LT NVFP4 GEMM to move SF into the layout cuBLAS consumes. The cast-fusion kernel already had a compile-time `kEnableSwizzleSFOutput` switch, but the framework never set the matching `with_gemm_swizzled_scales` flag on NVFP4 outputs (it was hardcoded `false` with a TODO). This PR wires the flag through (driving `kEnableSwizzleSFOutput` from `output.with_gemm_swizzled_scales`) so the quant kernel emits GEMM-swizzled SF directly and `tex.swizzle_scales_for_gemm_` early-returns, eliminating the standalone swizzle pass. A shape/arch gate (`is_eligible_for_rht_cast_fusion`: rows%64==0 && cols%128==0 && SM100/110) prevents enabling it on ineligible shapes that would otherwise abort.

In _Linear.backward, when the weight is frozen (not ctx.requires_wgrad) and there is no bias (not ctx.use_bias), the grad_output quantizer previously produced both rowwise and columnwise FP8 data, but only the columnwise-quantized grad_output is consumed by the dgrad GEMM. The commit adds `ctx.grad_output_quantizer.set_usage(columnwise=False)`... actually disables rowwise (keeps only columnwise) so the redundant rowwise quantization pass and its extra FP8 buffer are skipped, cutting compute and peak memory for frozen-weight FP8 linears. The cast_kernels.cuh edit only enriches an error message (adds 'or IS_DBIAS=true') documenting a constraint hit while doing this.

The old grouped_gemm passed problem shapes as a device `dim_list` tensor, forcing a per-call `cudaMemcpyAsync(dim_list_host, ..., DeviceToHost)` followed by `cudaStreamSynchronize(stream)` (commented "This may break cudaGraph") just to read m/n/k on the host, and concatenated all lhs/rhs/scale inputs via `jnp.concatenate` then split the output via `jnp.split` (extra D2D memcpys). The rewrite passes each tensor as a separate variadic FFI argument, so shapes are read directly from `lhs_i.dimensions()` on the host with no device copy/sync, and the concatenate/split (and a `squeeze()`) are removed — eliminating the host-side stall, the CUDA-graph break, and the D2D memcpys.

In three context-parallel attention backward functions, ctx.saved_tensors (a PyTorch autograd property whose getter re-unpacks the saved-tensor pack on every access) was read 4-6 times per backward call, once per slice. The fix reads it once into a local list `(*saved_tensors,) = ctx.saved_tensors` and slices that list instead, eliminating the redundant per-access unpacking work in the bwd pass.

For THD (packed/variable-length) attention, when max_seqlen is not supplied it is derived from the observed max sequence length. The old code rounded it up to the next power of two (`pow(2, math.ceil(math.log2(seqlens.max().item())))`); the new code rounds up to the next multiple of 64 (`int((seqlens.max().item() + 63) // 64 * 64)`). Power-of-two roundup can nearly double max_seqlen (e.g. 2049 -> 4096 vs 2112), which over-sizes attention tiling/work and buffers (e.g. the ALiBi bias tensor in get_alibi is shaped [..., max_seqlen_q, max_seqlen_kv]), wasting memory and compute. This is a latent inefficiency, not a regression: the power-of-two roundup was introduced by the same feature commit that first added THD support.

In the GemmRs (GEMM + reduce-scatter) cuBLASMp path, the reduce-scatter collective previously operated in the default FP32 precision. This commit sets CUBLASMP_MATMUL_DESCRIPTOR_ATTRIBUTE_COMMUNICATION_TYPE to the output data type (e.g. BF16/FP16), so the reduce-scatter moves the output-precision bytes instead of FP32 — roughly halving the communication volume of the collective and matching UserBuffers behavior. (The companion lld fix for transb=true is a pure correctness fix, not perf.)

For FP8 current-scaling with the unfused (non-cuDNN) LayerNorm/RMSNorm forward path, amax over the norm output was previously computed by a separate full-tensor pass inside a subsequent quantize() call. This commit lets the norm kernel compute amax inline whenever an amax pointer is provided (guarded by `const bool requires_amax = params.amax != nullptr;` and hoisted out of the `if (params.fp8_out)` block), and adds `create_hp_tensor_with_amax`/`quantize_with_amax` so the quantize step reuses the kernel-computed amax instead of rescanning the whole activation tensor. This removes a redundant memory-bandwidth pass over the activations (and a kernel launch) per norm forward.

In the SP backward pass, the input all-gather (gather_along_first_dim/last_dim of ln_out/inputmat) is only needed to feed the wgrad GEMM. Pre-fix, for column-parallel + sequence_parallel it was performed unconditionally, so a frozen weight (weight.requires_grad=False, no wgrad computed) still paid for a redundant all-gather collective (and its handle.wait()). The fix gates the gather on `weight.requires_grad`, sets `handle = None` in the else branch, and guards `handle.wait()` with `handle is not None`, eliminating the wasted collective communication when the weight is frozen.

Adds a clear_tensor_data() helper (sets t.data = torch.Tensor() to force deallocation since del does not release memory under PyTorch's tensor override) and calls it on backward-pass intermediate activations (ln_out_total, fc1_out, gelu_out, grad_output_c, fp8-transposed activations, inputmat_total, dgelu) once each is consumed by its GEMM. Also renames/reuses buffers (dxmat->dgrad, dgelu<-fc2_dgrad) with the comment 'Overwrite data. Deleting the tensor does not release underlying memory.' This lowers peak activation memory in the backward of LayerNormLinear, LayerNormMLP, and Linear.

Adds activation CPU-offloading support to the fusible-ops API (te.ops.*), which previously did not participate in offloading. Each op's forward now calls `mark_activation_offload(...)` on the tensors it will `save_for_backward` when `is_cpu_offload_enabled()`, so saved activations (x_local, mask, norm stats, etc.) are moved GPU→CPU between forward and backward, reducing peak GPU memory. Enables a memory-saving path that was absent for these ops rather than fixing a regression.

Adds an opt-in flag `return_layernorm_output_gathered` to LayerNormLinear/LayerNormMLP that returns the layernorm output in its already-gathered form instead of the sharded (pre-all-gather) form. Under sequence parallelism the forward pass already computes `ln_out_total` via `gather_along_first_dim` for the GEMM; a downstream consumer (e.g. LoRA residual) that needed the gathered layernorm output previously had to perform its own redundant all-gather. Returning the existing gathered tensor eliminates that duplicate collective. The backward path is adjusted so the residual gradient is summed before `reduce_scatter_along_first_dim` (rather than after) to remain correct.

The NVFP4 weight-preswizzle optimization (PR #3093) enabled optimize_for_gemm on cached weights, doing the GEMM scale swizzle once via a post-quantize out-of-place nvte_swizzle_scaling_factors fallback that reallocates/rebinds the scale buffer pointer on each re-quantize. When a CUDA graph captures a GEMM against the cached weight (is_first_microbatch=False, no in-graph re-quantize), the captured GEMM hard-codes the swizzled scale address; the next batch's eager re-quantize frees/rebinds that address, so graph replay reads stale freed memory -> hang/corruption. This commit makes the non-RHT 2D quantize kernel emit GEMM-swizzled scales in-place (byte-compatible with nvte_swizzle_scaling_factors) so the cached scale buffer keeps a stable address and the out-of-place swizzle pass is skipped.

Adds Multi-Node NVLink (MNNVL) support to the UserBuffers comm+GEMM overlap path. `has_mnnvl_fabric()` probes the device via `cuDeviceGetAttribute(CU_DEVICE_ATTRIBUTE_HANDLE_TYPE_FABRIC_SUPPORTED)` + NVML `nvmlDeviceGetGpuFabricInfoV`, and when a fabric is present the multicast/memory handle type is switched from `CU_MEM_HANDLE_TYPE_POSIX_FILE_DESCRIPTOR` to `CU_MEM_HANDLE_TYPE_FABRIC`. This enables the (already-existing) TP comm+GEMM overlap optimization — which hides communication behind GEMM compute to reduce GPU bubbles — to run across nodes connected by NVLink, where it previously could not be set up (POSIX FD handles cannot cross nodes). The commit also removes the dead multi-node pipeline request-fifo machinery (`pipe_rank`, `fifo`, `comm_inter`, `active_req`).

The commit is primarily a feature (enabling FP8 output tensors per GEMM, expanding FP8TensorMeta indices GEMM1_OUTPUT/GEMM2_OUTPUT and passing D_scale/D_amax through te_gemm). Embedded within it is a genuine micro-optimization in update_amax_history: `amax_history = torch.roll(amax_history, -1, 0)` is now guarded by `if amax_history.shape[0] > 1`, avoiding a redundant roll kernel launch (a no-op result for a single-row history) every forward pass. This eliminates an unnecessary GPU kernel per amax-history update when history_len==1, the common default.

GetFusedAttnForwardWorkspaceSizes and GetFusedAttnBackwardWorkspaceSizes call nvte_tensor_pack_create(&aux_output_tensors)/(&aux_input_tensors) to hold auxiliary NVTETensor handles but never destroyed the pack before returning, leaking the tensor-pack allocation. The fix adds nvte_tensor_pack_destroy(&aux_output_tensors) / (&aux_input_tensors) before returning, matching the create/destroy pairing already present in FusedAttnForwardImpl.

Bug fixes in the DGRAD reduce-scatter comm+GEMM overlap path (UbufCommOverlap in comm_gemm_overlap.h). The split RS-overlap loops hardcoded the FP8 template type as __nv_fp8_e4m3 for reducescatter2_userbuff_*_fp8 kernels; the fix wraps the loops in TRANSFORMER_ENGINE_TYPE_SWITCH_FP8ONLY(B_type, fp8_type, ...) so the reduce-scatter dispatches on the actual grad FP8 dtype, plus small plumbing fixes in base.py/layernorm_linear.py. This repairs the recently-added (#754) TP DGRAD reduce-scatter/GEMM overlap so it functions and can deliver its intended compute/comm overlap (throughput) benefit for FP8 configs.

The version gate TRITON_AUTOTUNED_INPUT_OUTPUT_ALIAS_MIN_JAX_VERSION="0.9.3" is compared with packaging.version, which ranks pre-releases below the stable release of the same series (PkgVersion("0.9.2.dev20260317") < "0.9.2" < "0.9.3"). So the gate rejected ALL nightly JAX builds — even nightlies already containing the jax-ml/jax#35218 fix — routing them into the else branch that passes empty input_output_aliases and effectively disables safe Triton autotuning, so kernels run a single non-autotuned config (slower). This commit replaces the single-floor check with is_triton_autotuned_alias_safe() using separate nightly (0.9.2.dev20260317) and stable (0.9.3) floors, re-enabling autotuning on nightly containers; it also fixes the config-constexpr merge order so each autotuning candidate compiles with its own BLOCK_SIZE instead of being overridden by the caller's grid BLOCK_SIZE.

In initialize_ub(), the global _cublas_workspace was unconditionally set to get_workspace().repeat(_NUM_MAX_UB_STREAMS). get_workspace() returns the already-expanded global workspace, so each repeat init/destroy cycle re-applied .repeat(), growing the cuBLAS workspace allocation exponentially (leak). The fix only expands when _cublas_workspace is None or when its size does not already equal the expected expanded size, allocating a fresh base-sized buffer before repeating to avoid re-expanding an already-expanded workspace.

Renames `AddInPlace`→`AddExtraInput` and adds an `in_place` kwarg (default `False`) to both `AddExtraInput` and `MakeExtraOutput`. Previously these ops were unconditionally in-place (`output = extra.detach(); output += input_`), and the fusers only checked `isinstance(op, AddInPlace/MakeExtraOutput)`. Now `fuse_forward_linear_bias_add` and `fuse_backward_linear_add` add `if not op._in_place: continue`, so with the new default the GEMM+bias+add fusion (which uses `accumulate_into_out=True` to write the GEMM result directly into the extra-input buffer, avoiding a separate elementwise add and an output allocation) and the backward linear+add fusion no longer fire. Users must opt in with `in_place=True` to keep the fused, allocation-avoiding path; the safety motivation is that in-place ops "break some autograd assumptions." The commit also sets `tensor.do_not_clear = True` on fuser outputs so they are not freed during backward.

Adds an opt-in "eager" safetensors load strategy: instead of mmap-based safe_open()/get_tensor() (which does on-demand random reads), it reads the entire file into CPU RAM with open(st_file,'rb').read() then safetensors.torch.load(). This avoids inefficient random reads over network filesystems (Lustre/NFS), speeding up model initialization at the cost of more CPU RAM. Default remains "lazy" (mmap), so existing behavior is unchanged.

Checkpoint saving previously forked one child process per write bucket (mp.get_context("fork"), default thread_count=2) to write in parallel. Forking copies (COW) the training process address space and, per the docstring, "Triggering GC during execution leads to CUDA errors (cleaning up tensors owned by the parent process)"; the new arguments warning states forking "may introduce instability on checkpoints." The commit adds a single-process/sequential save path where the last bucket (or all buckets when sequential=True) is written in the master process instead of forking, changes the default thread_count 2->1, adds --dist-ckpt-workers (default 1), and adds `del tensor`/`del async_request` so PersistentAsyncCaller can drop references and free host memory. Net effect: fewer forked writer processes (lower memory/instability), at the cost of write parallelism.

Introduces FullyParallelLoadStrategyWrapper, the load-side counterpart to the existing FullyParallelSaveStrategyWrapper. The intent is to speed up distributed-checkpoint loading by distributing shards so each 'main replica' is read from storage by a single rank (determine_main_replica_uniform_distribution / distribute_main_replicas_with_precomputed_distribution) and then all-gathering tensors across the parallelization group, avoiding redundant reads of the same shard by every rank. This is a latent-inefficiency optimization for checkpoint load, not a fix for a regression.
